[]{#text00000.html#filepos0000229506}

`<font size="5">`{=html} **CHAPTER 3** `</font>`{=html}\
\
`<font size="5">`{=html} **Labeling** `</font>`{=html}

[]{#text00000.html#filepos0000229731}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **3.1 Motivation** `</font>`{=html}

[In Chapter 2 we discussed how to produce a matrix]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*X* [of financial features out of an unstructured dataset. Unsupervised
learning algorithms can learn the patterns from that
matrix]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X* [, for example whether it contains
hierarchical clusters. On the other hand, supervised learning algorithms
require that the rows in]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X* [are associated with an
array of labels or values]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y* [, so that those
labels or values can be predicted on unseen features samples. In this
chapter we will discuss ways to label financial
data.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00000.html#filepos0000230568}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **3.2 The Fixed-Time Horizon Method**
`</font>`{=html}

[As it relates to finance, virtually all ML papers label observations
using the fixed-time horizon method. This method can be described as
follows. Consider a features matrix]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X*
[with]{bgcolor="inherit"} *I* [rows, {]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [}
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}=\ 1,\ ...,`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *I*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
, drawn from some bars with index]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [= 1,
...,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [, where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *I*
[≤]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [. Chapter 2, Section 2.5 discussed sampling
methods that produce the set of features {]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [}
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}=\ 1,\ ...,`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *I*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
. An observation]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [is
assigned a label]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [∈ { − 1,
0, 1},]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00599.jpg){width="224" height="91"}

[where τ is a pre-defined constant threshold,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[is the index of the bar immediately after]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [takes
place,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[+]{bgcolor="inherit"} *h* [is the index of the]{bgcolor="inherit"} *h*
[-th bar after]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[, and]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00188.jpg){width="62" height="18"}
[is the price return over a bar horizon]{bgcolor="inherit"} *h*
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00602.jpg){width="166" height="51"}

[Because the literature almost always works with time
bars,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *h* [implies a fixed-time horizon. The
bibliography section lists multiple ML studies, of which Dixon et al.
\[2016\] is a recent example of this labeling method. Despite its
popularity, there are several reasons to avoid this approach in most
cases. First, as we saw in Chapter 2, time bars do not exhibit good
statistical properties. Second, the same threshold τ is applied
regardless of the observed volatility. Suppose that τ =
1]{bgcolor="inherit"} *E* [− 2, where sometimes we label an observation
as]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [= 1
subject to a realized bar volatility of]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00207.jpg){width="105" height="23"} [(e.g., during the night
session), and sometimes]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00606.jpg){width="105" height="23"} [(e.g., around the open).
The large majority of labels will be 0, even if
return]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00657.jpg){width="62" height="18"}
[was predictable and statistically significant.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[In other words, it is a very common error to label observations
according to a fixed threshold on time bars. Here are a couple of better
alternatives. First, label per a varying threshold]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00610.jpg){width="25" height="19"} [, estimated using a rolling
exponentially weighted standard deviation of returns. Second, use volume
or dollar bars, as their volatilities are much closer to constant
(homoscedasticity). But even these two improvements miss a key flaw of
the fixed-time horizon method: the path followed by prices. Every
investment strategy has stop-loss limits, whether they are self-imposed
by the portfolio manager, enforced by the risk department, or triggered
by a margin call. It is simply unrealistic to build a strategy that
profits from positions that would have been stopped-out by the exchange.
That virtually no publication accounts for that when labeling
observations tells you something about the current state of the
investment literature.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00000.html#filepos0000235552}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **3.3 Computing Dynamic Thresholds**
`</font>`{=html}

[As argued in the previous section, in practice we want to set profit
taking and stop-loss limits that are a function of the risks involved in
a bet. Otherwise, sometimes we will be aiming too high
(]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00733.jpg){width="64" height="21"} [),
and sometimes too low (]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00614.jpg){width="64" height="21"} [), considering the
prevailing volatility.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Snippet 3.1 computes the daily volatility at intraday estimation
points, applying a span of]{bgcolor="inherit"} `span0` [days to an
exponentially weighted moving standard deviation. See the pandas
documentation for details on the]{bgcolor="inherit"} `pandas.Series.ewm`
[function.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 3.1 DAILY VOLATILITY ESTIMATES**

> ![](Image00615.jpg){width="925" height="186"}

[We can use the output of this function to set default profit taking and
stop-loss limits throughout the rest of this
chapter.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00000.html#filepos0000237025}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **3.4 The Triple-Barrier Method**
`</font>`{=html}

[Here I will introduce an alternative labeling method that I have not
found in the literature. If you are an investment professional, I think
you will agree that it makes more sense. I call it the triple-barrier
method because it labels an observation according to the first barrier
touched out of three barriers. First, we set two horizontal barriers and
one vertical barrier. The two horizontal barriers are defined by
profit-taking and stop-loss limits, which are a dynamic function of
estimated volatility (whether realized or implied). The third barrier is
defined in terms of number of bars elapsed since the position was taken
(an expiration limit). If the upper barrier is touched first, we label
the observation as a 1. If the lower barrier is touched first, we label
the observation as a −1. If the vertical barrier is touched first, we
have two choices: the sign of the return, or a 0. I personally prefer
the former as a matter of realizing a profit or loss within limits, but
you should explore whether a 0 works better in your particular
problems.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[You may have noticed that the triple-barrier method is path-dependent.
In order to label an observation, we must take into account the entire
path spanning \[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[+]{bgcolor="inherit"} *h* [\], where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *h* [defines
the vertical barrier (the expiration limit). We will
denote]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[the time of the first barrier touch, and the return associated with the
observed feature is]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00618.jpg){width="43"
height="18"} [. For the sake of clarity,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[≤]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[+]{bgcolor="inherit"} *h* [and the horizontal barriers are not
necessarily symmetric.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Snippet 3.2 implements the triple-barrier method. The function receives
four arguments:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

-   `close` : A pandas series of prices.
-   `events` : A pandas dataframe, with columns,
    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    -   `t1` : The timestamp of vertical barrier. When the value is
        `np.nan` , there will not be a vertical barrier.
    -   `trgt` : The unit width of the horizontal barriers.
-   `ptSl` : A list of two non-negative float values:
    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    -   `ptSl[0]` : The factor that multiplies `trgt` to set the width
        of the upper barrier. If 0, there will not be an upper barrier.
    -   `ptSl[1]` : The factor that multiplies `trgt` to set the width
        of the lower barrier. If 0, there will not be a lower barrier.
-   `molecule` : A list with the subset of event indices that will be
    processed by a single thread. Its use will become clear later on in
    the chapter.

> **SNIPPET 3.2 TRIPLE-BARRIER LABELING METHOD**

> ![](Image00619.jpg){width="886" height="347"}

[The output from this function is a pandas dataframe containing the
timestamps (if any) at which each barrier was touched. As you can see
from the previous description, the method considers the possibility that
each of the three barriers may be disabled. Let us denote a barrier
configuration by the triplet \[]{bgcolor="inherit"} `pt,sl,t1` [\],
where a 0 means that the barrier is inactive and a 1 means that the
barrier is active. The possible eight configurations
are:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

-   Three useful configurations:
    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    -   \[1,1,1\]: This is the standard setup, where we define three
        barrier exit conditions. We would like to realize a profit, but
        we have a maximum tolerance for losses and a holding period.
    -   \[0,1,1\]: In this setup, we would like to exit after a number
        of bars, unless we are stopped-out.
    -   \[1,1,0\]: Here we would like to take a profit as long as we are
        not stopped-out. This is somewhat unrealistic in that we are
        willing to hold the position for as long as it takes.
-   Three less realistic configurations:
    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    -   \[0,0,1\]: This is equivalent to the fixed-time horizon method.
        It may still be useful when applied to volume-, dollar-, or
        information-driven bars, and multiple forecasts are updated
        within the horizon.
    -   \[1,0,1\]: A position is held until a profit is made or the
        maximum holding period is exceeded, without regard for the
        intermediate unrealized losses.
    -   \[1,0,0\]: A position is held until a profit is made. It could
        mean being locked on a losing position for years.
-   Two illogical configurations:
    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    -   \[0,1,0\]: This is an aimless configuration, where we hold a
        position until we are stopped-out.
    -   \[0,0,0\]: There are no barriers. The position is locked
        forever, and no label is generated.

[]{#text00000.html#filepos0000243978}

[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
3.1`</font>`{=html}](#text00000.html#filepos0000244523) [shows two
alternative configurations of the triple-barrier method. On the left,
the configuration is \[1,1,0\], where the first barrier touched is the
lower horizontal one. On the right, the configuration is \[1,1,1\],
where the first barrier touched is the vertical one.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00621.jpg){width="375" height="600"}

[]{#text00000.html#filepos0000244523}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
3.1`</font>`{=html}**](#text00000.html#filepos0000243978) Two
alternative configurations of the triple-barrier method

[]{#text00001.html}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000244731}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **3.5 Learning Side and Size** `</font>`{=html}

[In this section we will discuss how to label examples so that an ML
algorithm can learn both the side and the size of a bet. We are
interested in learning the side of a bet when we do not have an
underlying model to set the sign of our position (long or short). Under
such circumstance, we cannot differentiate between a profit-taking
barrier and a stop-loss barrier, since that requires knowledge of the
side. Learning the side implies that either there are no horizontal
barriers or that the horizontal barriers must be
symmetric.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Snippet 3.3 implements the function]{bgcolor="inherit"} `getEvents` [,
which finds the time of the first barrier touch. The function receives
the following arguments:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

-   `close` : A pandas series of prices.
-   `tEvents` : The pandas timeindex containing the timestamps that will
    seed every triple barrier. These are the timestamps selected by the
    sampling procedures discussed in Chapter 2, Section 2.5.
-   `ptSl` : A non-negative float that sets the width of the two
    barriers. A 0 value means that the respective horizontal barrier
    (profit taking and/or stop loss) will be disabled.
-   `t1` : A pandas series with the timestamps of the vertical barriers.
    We pass a `False` when we want to disable vertical barriers.
-   `trgt` : A pandas series of targets, expressed in terms of absolute
    returns.
-   `minRet` : The minimum target return required for running a triple
    barrier search.
-   `numThreads` : The number of threads concurrently used by the
    function.

> **SNIPPET 3.3 GETTING THE TIME OF FIRST TOUCH**

> ![](Image00624.jpg){width="891" height="397"}

[Suppose that]{bgcolor="inherit"} *I* [= 1]{bgcolor="inherit"} *E* [6
and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *h* [= 1]{bgcolor="inherit"} *E* [3, then the
number of conditions to evaluate is up to one billion on a single
instrument. Many ML tasks are computationally expensive unless you are
familiar with multi-threading, and this is one of them. Here is where
parallel computing comes into play. Chapter 20 discusses a few
multiprocessing functions that we will use throughout the
book.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Function]{bgcolor="inherit"} `mpPandasObj` [calls a multiprocessing
engine, which is explained in depth in Chapter 20. For the moment, you
simply need to know that this function will execute]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`applyPtSlOnT1` [in parallel. Function]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`applyPtSlOnT1` [returns the timestamps at which each barrier is touched
(if any). Then, the time of the first touch is the earliest time among
the three returned by]{bgcolor="inherit"} `applyPtSlOnT1` [. Because we
must learn the side of the bet, we have passed]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`ptSl = [ptSl,ptSl]` [as argument, and we arbitrarily set the side to be
always long (the horizontal barriers are symmetric, so the side is
irrelevant to determining the time of the first touch). The output from
this function is a pandas dataframe with columns:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

-   `t1` : The timestamp at which the first barrier is touched.
-   `trgt` : The target that was used to generate the horizontal
    barriers.

[Snippet 3.4 shows one way to define a vertical barrier. For each index
in]{bgcolor="inherit"} `tEvents` [, it finds the timestamp of the next
price bar at or immediately after a number of days]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`numDays` [. This vertical barrier can be passed as optional
argument]{bgcolor="inherit"} `t1` [in]{bgcolor="inherit"} `getEvents`
[.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 3.4 ADDING A VERTICAL BARRIER**

> ![](Image00625.jpg){width="865" height="72"}

[Finally, we can label the observations using the]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`getBins` [function defined in Snippet 3.5. The arguments are
the]{bgcolor="inherit"} `events` [dataframe we just discussed, and
the]{bgcolor="inherit"} `close` [pandas series of prices. The output is
a dataframe with columns:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

-   `ret` : The return realized at the time of the first touched
    barrier.
-   `bin` : The label, { − 1, 0, 1}, as a function of the sign of the
    outcome. The function can be easily adjusted to label as 0 those
    events when the vertical barrier was touched first, which we leave
    as an exercise.

> **SNIPPET 3.5 LABELING FOR SIDE AND SIZE**

> ![](Image00628.jpg){width="848" height="259"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000250934}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **3.6 Meta-Labeling** `</font>`{=html}

[Suppose that you have a model for setting the side of the bet (long or
short). You just need to learn the size of that bet, which includes the
possibility of no bet at all (zero size). This is a situation that
practitioners face regularly. We often know whether we want to buy or
sell a product, and the only remaining question is how much money we
should risk in such a bet. We do not want the ML algorithm to learn the
side, just to tell us what is the appropriate size. At this point, it
probably does not surprise you to hear that no book or paper has so far
discussed this common problem. Thankfully, that misery ends here. I call
this problem meta-labeling because we want to build a secondary ML model
that learns how to use a primary exogenous model.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Rather than writing an entirely new]{bgcolor="inherit"} `getEvents`
[function, we will make some adjustments to the previous code, in order
to handle meta-labeling. First, we accept a new]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`side` [optional argument (with default]{bgcolor="inherit"} `None` [),
which contains the side of our bets as decided by the primary model.
When]{bgcolor="inherit"} `side` [is not]{bgcolor="inherit"} `None` [,
the function understands that meta-labeling is in play. Second, because
now we know the side, we can effectively discriminate between profit
taking and stop loss. The horizontal barriers do not need to be
symmetric, as in Section 3.5. Argument]{bgcolor="inherit"} `ptSl` [is a
list of two non-negative float values, where]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`ptSl[0]` [is the factor that multiplies]{bgcolor="inherit"} `trgt` [to
set the width of the upper barrier, and]{bgcolor="inherit"} `ptSl[1]`
[is the factor that multiplies]{bgcolor="inherit"} `trgt` [to set the
width of the lower barrier. When either is 0, the respective barrier is
disabled. Snippet 3.6 implements these enhancements.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 3.6 EXPANDING** ` GETEVENTS` **TO INCORPORATE
> META-LABELING**

> ![](Image00337.jpg){width="887" height="394"}

[Likewise, we need to expand the]{bgcolor="inherit"} `getBins`
[function, so that it handles meta-labeling. Snippet 3.7 implements the
necessary changes.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 3.7 EXPANDING** ` GETBINS` **TO INCORPORATE META-LABELING**

> ![](Image00633.jpg){width="853" height="562"}

[Now the possible values for labels in]{bgcolor="inherit"} `out[‘bin’]`
[are {0,1}, as opposed to the previous feasible values {−1,0,1}. The ML
algorithm will be trained to decide whether to take the bet or pass, a
purely binary prediction. When the predicted label is 1, we can use the
probability of this secondary prediction to derive the size of the bet,
where the side (sign) of the position has been set by the primary
model.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000254530}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **3.7 How to Use Meta-Labeling**
`</font>`{=html}

[Binary classification problems present a trade-off between type-I
errors (false positives) and type-II errors (false negatives). In
general, increasing the true positive rate of a binary classifier will
tend to increase its false positive rate. The receiver operating
characteristic (ROC) curve of a binary classifier measures the cost of
increasing the true positive rate, in terms of accepting higher false
positive rates.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000255126}

[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
3.2`</font>`{=html}](#text00001.html#filepos0000256656) [illustrates the
so-called "confusion matrix." On a set of observations, there are items
that exhibit a condition (positives, left rectangle), and items that do
not exhibit a condition (negative, right rectangle). A binary classifier
predicts that some items exhibit the condition (ellipse), where the TP
area contains the true positives and the TN area contains the true
negatives. This leads to two kinds of errors: false positives (FP) and
false negatives (FN). "Precision" is the ratio between the TP area and
the area in the ellipse. "Recall" is the ratio between the TP area and
the area in the left rectangle. This notion of recall (aka true positive
rate) is in the context of classification problems, the analogous to
"power" in the context of hypothesis testing. "Accuracy" is the sum of
the TP and TN areas divided by the overall set of items (square). In
general, decreasing the FP area comes at a cost of increasing the FN
area, because higher precision typically means fewer calls, hence lower
recall. Still, there is some combination of precision and recall that
maximizes the overall efficiency of the classifier. The F1-score
measures the efficiency of a classifier as the harmonic average between
precision and recall (more on this in Chapter 14).]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00637.jpg){width="689" height="600"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000256656}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
3.2`</font>`{=html}**](#text00001.html#filepos0000255126) A
visualization of the "confusion matrix"

[Meta-labeling is particularly helpful when you want to achieve higher
F1-scores. First, we build a model that achieves high recall, even if
the precision is not particularly high. Second, we correct for the low
precision by applying meta-labeling to the positives predicted by the
primary model.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Meta-labeling will increase your F1-score by filtering out the false
positives, where the majority of positives have already been identified
by the primary model. Stated differently, the role of the secondary ML
algorithm is to determine whether a positive from the primary
(exogenous) model is true or false. It is]{bgcolor="inherit"} *not* [its
purpose to come up with a betting opportunity. Its purpose is to
determine whether we should act or pass on the opportunity that has been
presented.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Meta-labeling is a very powerful tool to have in your arsenal, for four
additional reasons. First, ML algorithms are often criticized as black
boxes (see Chapter 1). Meta-labeling allows you to build an ML system on
top of a white box (like a fundamental model founded on economic
theory). This ability to transform a fundamental model into an ML model
should make meta-labeling particularly useful to "quantamental" firms.
Second, the effects of overfitting are limited when you apply
meta-labeling, because ML will not decide the side of your bet, only the
size. Third, by decoupling the side prediction from the size prediction,
meta-labeling enables sophisticated strategy structures. For instance,
consider that the features driving a rally may differ from the features
driving a sell-off. In that case, you may want to develop an ML strategy
exclusively for long positions, based on the buy recommendations of a
primary model, and an ML strategy exclusively for short positions, based
on the sell recommendations of an entirely different primary model.
Fourth, achieving high accuracy on small bets and low accuracy on large
bets will ruin you. As important as identifying good opportunities is to
size them properly, so it makes sense to develop an ML algorithm solely
focused on getting that critical decision (sizing) right. We will retake
this fourth point in Chapter 10. In my experience, meta-labeling ML
models can deliver more robust and reliable outcomes than standard
labeling models.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000259348}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **3.8 The Quantamental Way** `</font>`{=html}

[You may have read in the press that many hedge funds are embracing the
quantamental approach. A simple Google search will show reports that
many hedge funds, including some of the most traditional ones, are
investing tens of millions of dollars in technologies designed to
combine human expertise with quantitative methods. It turns out,
meta-labeling is exactly what these people have been waiting for. Let us
see why.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Suppose that you have a series of features that you believe can
forecast some prices, you just do not know how. Since you do not have a
model to determine the side of each bet, you need to learn both side and
size. You apply what you have learned in Section 3.5, and produce some
labels based on the triple-barrier method with symmetric horizontal
barriers. Now you are ready to fit your algorithm on a training set, and
evaluate the accuracy of your forecasts on a testing set. Alternatively,
you could do the following:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

1.  Use your forecasts from the primary model, and generate meta-labels.
    Remember, horizontal barriers do not need to be symmetric in this
    case.
2.  Fit your model again on the same training set, but this time using
    the meta-labels you just generated.
3.  Combine the "sides" from the first ML model with the "sizes" from
    the second ML model.

[You can always add a meta-labeling layer to any primary model, whether
that is an ML algorithm, an econometric equation, a technical trading
rule, a fundamental analysis, etc. That includes forecasts generated by
a human, solely based on his intuition. In that case, meta-labeling will
help us figure out when we should pursue or dismiss a discretionary
PM\'s call. The features used by such meta-labeling ML algorithm could
range from market information to biometric statistics to psychological
assessments. For example, the meta-labeling ML algorithm could find that
discretionary PMs tend to make particularly good calls when there is a
structural break (Chapter 17), as they may be quicker to grasp a change
in the market regime. Conversely, it may find that PMs under stress, as
evidenced by fewer hours of sleep, fatigue, change in weight, etc. tend
to make inaccurate
predictions.^[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000262020}\ [[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ `<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}1`</font>`{=html}\ `</font>`{=html}](#text00001.html#filepos0000278970)]{.small}^
Many professions require regular psychological exams, and an ML
meta-labeling algorithm may find that those scores are also relevant to
assess our current degree of confidence on a PM\'s predictions. Perhaps
none of these factors affect discretionary PMs, and their brains operate
independently from their emotional being, like cold calculating
machines. My guess is that this is not the case, and therefore
meta-labeling should become an essential ML technique for every
discretionary hedge fund. In the near future, every discretionary hedge
fund will become a quantamental firm, and meta-labeling offers them a
clear path to make that transition.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000262810}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **3.9 Dropping Unnecessary Labels**
`</font>`{=html}

[Some ML classifiers do not perform well when classes are too
imbalanced. In those circumstances, it is preferable to drop extremely
rare labels and focus on the more common outcomes. Snippet 3.8 presents
a procedure that recursively drops observations associated with
extremely rare labels. Function]{bgcolor="inherit"} `dropLabels`
[recursively eliminates those observations associated with classes that
appear less than a fraction]{bgcolor="inherit"} `minPct` [of cases,
unless there are only two classes left.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 3.8 DROPPING UNDER-POPULATED LABELS**

> ![](Image00640.jpg){width="713" height="205"}

[Incidentally, another reason you may want to drop unnecessary labels is
this known sklearn bug:]{bgcolor="inherit"}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/8566`</font>`{=html}](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/8566)
[. This sort of bug is a consequence of very fundamental assumptions in
sklearn implementation, and resolving them is far from trivial. In this
particular instance, the error stems from sklearn\'s decision to operate
with standard numpy arrays rather than structured arrays or pandas
objects. It is unlikely that there will be a fix by the time you are
reading this chapter, or in the near future. In later chapters, we will
study ways to circumvent these sorts of implementation errors, by
building your own classes and expanding sklearn\'s
functionality.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000264704}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **Exercises** `</font>`{=html}

::: {height="1em"}
:::

1.  [Form dollar bars for E-mini S&P 500 futures:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  Apply a symmetric CUSUM filter (Chapter 2, Section 2.5.2.1)
        where the threshold is the standard deviation of daily returns
        (Snippet 3.1).
    2.  Use Snippet 3.4 on a pandas series `t1` , where `numDays = 1` .
    3.  On those sampled features, apply the triple-barrier method,
        where `ptSl = [1,1]` and `t1` is the series you created in point
        1.b.
    4.  Apply `getBins` to generate the labels.

2.  [From exercise 1, use Snippet 3.8 to drop rare
    > > labels.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

3.  [Adjust the]{bgcolor="inherit"} `getBins` [function (Snippet 3.5) to
    > > return a 0 whenever the vertical barrier is the one touched
    > > first.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

4.  [Develop a trend-following strategy based on a popular technical
    > > analysis statistic (e.g., crossing moving averages). For each
    > > observation, the model suggests a side, but not a size of the
    > > bet.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  Derive meta-labels for `ptSl = [1,2]` and `t1` where
        `numDays = 1` . Use as `trgt` the daily standard deviation as
        computed by Snippet 3.1.
    2.  Train a random forest to decide whether to trade or not. Note:
        The decision is whether to trade or not, {0,1}, since the
        underlying model (the crossing moving average) has decided the
        side, {−1,1}.

5.  [Develop a mean-reverting strategy based on Bollinger bands. For
    > > each observation, the model suggests a side, but not a size of
    > > the bet.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  Derive meta-labels for `ptSl = [0,2]` and `t1` where
        `numDays = 1` . Use as `trgt` the daily standard deviation as
        computed by Snippet 3.1.
    2.  Train a random forest to decide whether to trade or not. Use as
        features: volatility, serial correlation, and the crossing
        moving averages from exercise 2.
    3.  What is the accuracy of predictions from the primary model
        (i.e., if the secondary model does not filter the bets)? What
        are the precision, recall, and F1-scores?
    4.  What is the accuracy of predictions from the secondary model?
        What are the precision, recall, and F1-scores?

`<font size="4">`{=html} **Note** `</font>`{=html}

^[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000278970}\ [[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ `<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}1`</font>`{=html}\ `</font>`{=html}](#text00001.html#filepos0000262020)]{.small}^
   You are probably aware of at least one large hedge fund that monitors
the emotional state of their research analysts on a daily basis.

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000279229}

`<font size="5">`{=html} **CHAPTER 4** `</font>`{=html}\
\
`<font size="5">`{=html} **Sample Weights** `</font>`{=html}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000279460}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **4.1 Motivation** `</font>`{=html}

[Chapter 3 presented several new methods for labeling financial
observations. We introduced two novel concepts, the triple-barrier
method and meta-labeling, and explained how they are useful in financial
applications, including quantamental investment strategies. In this
chapter you will learn how to use sample weights to address another
problem ubiquitous in financial applications, namely that observations
are not generated by independent and identically distributed (IID)
processes. Most of the ML literature is based on the IID assumption, and
one reason many ML applications fail in finance is because those
assumptions are unrealistic in the case of financial time
series.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000280322}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **4.2 Overlapping Outcomes** `</font>`{=html}

[In Chapter 3 we assigned a label]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [to an
observed feature]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [,
where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [was a
function of price bars that occurred over an interval
\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[\]. When]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[\>]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *i* [\<]{bgcolor="inherit"} *j* [,
then]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [will both
depend on a common return]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00644.jpg){width="103" height="19"} [, that is, the return over
the interval \[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[, min{]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[}\]. The implication is that the series of labels,
{]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [}
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}=\ 1,\ ...,`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *I*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
, are not IID whenever there is an overlap between any two consecutive
outcomes, ∃]{bgcolor="inherit"} *i* [\|]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[\>]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}+\ 1,\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[..]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Suppose that we circumvent this problem by restricting the bet horizon
to]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[≤]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}+\ 1,\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[. In this case there is no overlap, because every feature outcome is
determined before or at the onset of the next observed feature. That
would lead to coarse models where the features' sampling frequency would
be limited by the horizon used to determine the outcome. On one hand, if
we wished to investigate outcomes that lasted a month, features would
have to be sampled with a frequency up to monthly. On the other hand, if
we increased the sampling frequency to let\'s say daily, we would be
forced to reduce the outcome\'s horizon to one day. Furthermore, if we
wished to apply a path-dependent labeling technique, like the
triple-barrier method, the sampling frequency would be subordinated to
the first barrier\'s touch. No matter what you do, restricting the
outcome\'s horizon to eliminate overlaps is a terrible solution. We must
allow]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[\>]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}+\ 1,\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[, which brings us back to the problem of overlapping outcomes described
earlier.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[This situation is characteristic of financial applications. Most
non-financial ML researchers can assume that observations are drawn from
IID processes. For example, you can obtain blood samples from a large
number of patients, and measure their cholesterol. Of course, various
underlying common factors will shift the mean and standard deviation of
the cholesterol distribution, but the samples are still independent:
There is one observation per subject. Suppose you take those blood
samples, and someone in your laboratory spills blood from each tube into
the following nine tubes to their right. That is, tube 10 contains blood
for patient 10, but also blood from patients 1 through 9. Tube 11
contains blood from patient 11, but also blood from patients 2 through
10, and so on. Now you need to determine the features predictive of high
cholesterol (diet, exercise, age, etc.), without knowing for sure the
cholesterol level of each patient. That is the equivalent challenge that
we face in financial ML, with the additional handicap that the spillage
pattern is non-deterministic and unknown. Finance is not a plug-and-play
subject as it relates to ML applications. Anyone who tells you otherwise
will waste your time and money.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[There are several ways to attack the problem of non-IID labels, and in
this chapter we will address it by designing sampling and weighting
schemes that correct for the undue influence of overlapping
outcomes.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000286034}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **4.3 Number of Concurrent Labels**
`</font>`{=html}

[Two labels]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [are
concurrent at]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [when both are a function of at
least one common return,]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00112.jpg){width="121" height="29"} *.* [The overlap does not
need to be perfect, in the sense of both labels spanning the same time
interval. In this section we are going to compute the number of labels
that are a function of a given return,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *r
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}−\ 1,`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[. First, for each time point]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [= 1,
...,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [, we form a binary array, {1
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
}
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}=\ 1,\ ...,`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *I*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
, where 1
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
∈ {0, 1}. Variable 1
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
= 1 if and only if \[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[\] overlaps with \[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [− 1,]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*t* [\] and 1
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
= 0 otherwise. Recall that the labels' spans {\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[\]}
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}=\ 1,\ ...,`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *I*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
are defined by the]{bgcolor="inherit"} `t1` [object introduced in
Chapter 3. Second, we compute the number of labels concurrent
at]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [,]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00672.jpg){width="102" height="25"} [. Snippet 4.1 illustrates

`<font size="4">`{=html} **4.4 Average Uniqueness of a Label**
`</font>`{=html}

[In this section we are going to estimate a label\'s uniqueness
(non-overlap) as its average uniqueness over its lifespan. First, the
uniqueness of a label]{bgcolor="inherit"} *i* [at
time]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [is]{bgcolor="inherit"} *u
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[= 1
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
]{bgcolor="inherit"} *c
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}−\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [. Second,
the average uniqueness of label]{bgcolor="inherit"} *i* [is the
average]{bgcolor="inherit"} *u
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[over the label\'s lifespan,]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00698.jpg){width="236" height="41"} [. This average uniqueness
can also be interpreted as the reciprocal of the harmonic average
of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *c
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [over the
event\'s lifespan.]{bgcolor="inherit"}
[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000290555}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
4.1`</font>`{=html}](#text00001.html#filepos0000290928) [plots the
histogram of uniqueness values derived from an
object]{bgcolor="inherit"} `t1` [. Snippet 4.2 implements this
calculation.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00714.jpg){width="800" height="501"}

> **SNIPPET 4.2 ESTIMATING THE AVERAGE UNIQUENESS OF A LABEL**

> ![](Image00732.jpg){width="933" height="344"}

[Note that we are making use again of the function]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`mpPandasObj` [, which speeds up calculations via multiprocessing (see
Chapter 20). Computing the average uniqueness associated with
label]{bgcolor="inherit"} *i* [,]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00750.jpg){width="13" height="17"} [, requires information that
is not available until a future time,]{bgcolor="inherit"} `events['t1']`
[. This is not a problem, because]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00769.jpg){width="84" height="26"} [are used on the training
set in combination with label information, and not on the testing set.
These]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00788.jpg){width="84" height="26"}
[are not used for forecasting the label, hence there is no information
leakage. This procedure allows us to assign a uniqueness score between 0
and 1 for each observed feature, in terms of non-overlapping
outcomes.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000292458}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **4.5 Bagging Classifiers and Uniqueness**
`</font>`{=html}

[The probability of not selecting a particular item]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*i* [after]{bgcolor="inherit"} *I* [draws with replacement on a set
of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *I* [items is (1 −]{bgcolor="inherit"} *I
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}−\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^* [)
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *I*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}^ . As the
sample size grows, that probability converges to the asymptotic
value]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00807.jpg){width="188" height="22"}
[. That means that the number of unique observations drawn is expected
to be]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00824.jpg){width="105" height="29"}
[.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Suppose that the maximum number of non-overlapping outcomes
is]{bgcolor="inherit"} *K* [≤]{bgcolor="inherit"} *I* [. Following the
same argument, the probability of not selecting a particular
item]{bgcolor="inherit"} *i* [after]{bgcolor="inherit"} *I* [draws with
replacement on a set of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *I* [items is (1
−]{bgcolor="inherit"} *K
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}−\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^* [)
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *I*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}^ . As the
sample size grows, that probability can be approximated
as]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00499.jpg){width="143" height="28"} [.
That means that the number of unique observations drawn is expected to
be]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00601.jpg){width="143" height="26"} [.
The implication is that incorrectly assuming IID draws leads to
oversampling.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[When sampling with replacement (bootstrap) on observations
with]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00152.jpg){width="124" height="25"} [,
it becomes increasingly likely that in-bag observations will be (1)
redundant to each other, and (2) very similar to out-of-bag
observations. Redundancy of draws makes the bootstrap inefficient (see
Chapter 6). For example, in the case of a random forest, all trees in
the forest will essentially be very similar copies of a single overfit
decision tree. And because the random sampling makes out-of-bag examples
very similar to the in-bag ones, out-of-bag accuracy will be grossly
inflated. We will address this second issue in Chapter 7, when we study
cross-validation under non-IID observations. For the moment, let us
concentrate on the first issue, namely bagging under observations
where]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00773.jpg){width="124" height="25"}
*.*

[A first solution is to drop overlapping outcomes before performing the
bootstrap. Because overlaps are not perfect, dropping an observation
just because there is a partial overlap will result in an extreme loss
of information. I do not advise you to follow this
solution.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[A second and better solution is to utilize the average
uniqueness,]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00010.jpg){width="86"
height="25"} [, to reduce the undue influence of outcomes that contain
redundant information. Accordingly, we could sample only a
fraction]{bgcolor="inherit"} `out['tW'].mean()` [of the observations, or
a small multiple of that. In sklearn, the]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`sklearn.ensemble.BaggingClassifier` [class accepts an
argument]{bgcolor="inherit"} `max_samples` [, which can be set
to]{bgcolor="inherit"} `max_samples=out['tW'].mean()` [. In this way, we
enforce that the in-bag observations are not sampled at a frequency much
higher than their uniqueness. Random forests do not offer
that]{bgcolor="inherit"} `max_samples` [functionality, however, a
solution is to bag a large number of decision trees. We will discuss
this solution further in Chapter 6.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **4.5.1 Sequential Bootstrap** `</font>`{=html}

[A third and better solution is to perform a sequential bootstrap, where
draws are made according to a changing probability that controls for
redundancy. Rao et al. \[1997\] propose sequential resampling with
replacement until]{bgcolor="inherit"} *K* [distinct original
observations appear. Although interesting, their scheme does not fully
apply to our financial problem. In the following sections we introduce
an alternative method that addresses directly the problem of overlapping
outcomes.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[First, an observation]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [is drawn
from a uniform distribution,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *i*
[∼]{bgcolor="inherit"} *U* [\[1,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *I* [\], that is,
the probability of drawing any particular value]{bgcolor="inherit"} *i*
[is originally δ ^[`<font size="2">`{=html}(1)`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
=]{bgcolor="inherit"} *I
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}−\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^* [. For the
second draw, we wish to reduce the probability of drawing an
observation]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [with a
highly overlapping outcome. Remember, a bootstrap allows sampling with
repetition, so it is still possible to draw]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [again, but
we wish to reduce its likelihood, since there is an overlap (in fact, a
perfect overlap) between]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [and
itself. Let us denote as φ the sequence of draws so far, which may
include repetitions. Until now, we know that φ
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}(1)`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^ =
{]{bgcolor="inherit"} *i* [}. The uniqueness of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *j*
[at time]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [is]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00160.jpg){width="232" height="41"} [, as that is the
uniqueness that results from adding alternative]{bgcolor="inherit"} *j*
['s to the existing sequence of draws φ
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}(1)`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^ . The average
uniqueness of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *j* [is the
average]{bgcolor="inherit"} *u
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}(2)`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[over]{bgcolor="inherit"} *j* ['s lifespan,]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00191.jpg){width="249" height="41"} [. We can now make a second
draw based on the updated probabilities {δ
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}(2)`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ }
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}=\ 1,\ ...,`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *I*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
,]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00165.jpg){width="182" height="66"}

[where {δ ^[`<font size="2">`{=html}(2)`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ }
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}=\ 1,\ ..,`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *I*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
are scaled to add up to 1,]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00135.jpg){width="102" height="31"} *.* [We can now do a second
draw, update φ ^[`<font size="2">`{=html}(2)`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
and re-evaluate {δ
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}(3)`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ }
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}=\ 1,\ ...,`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *I*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
. The process is repeated until]{bgcolor="inherit"} *I* [draws have
taken place. This sequential bootstrap scheme has the advantage that
overlaps (even repetitions) are still possible, but decreasingly likely.
The sequential bootstrap sample will be much closer to IID than samples
drawn from the standard bootstrap method. This can be verified by
measuring an increase in]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00153.jpg){width="86" height="25"} [, relative to the standard
bootstrap method.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **4.5.2 Implementation of Sequential
Bootstrap** `</font>`{=html}

[Snippet 4.3 derives an indicator matrix from two arguments: the index
of bars (]{bgcolor="inherit"} `barIx` [), and the pandas
Series]{bgcolor="inherit"} `t1` [, which we used multiple times in
Chapter 3. As a reminder,]{bgcolor="inherit"} `t1` [is defined by an
index containing the time at which the features are observed, and a
values array containing the time at which the label is determined. The
output of this function is a binary matrix indicating what (price) bars
influence the label for each observation.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 4.3 BUILD AN INDICATOR MATRIX**

> ![](Image00170.jpg){width="809" height="178"}

[Snippet 4.4 returns the average uniqueness of each observed feature.
The input is the indicator matrix built by]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`getIndMatrix` [.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 4.4 COMPUTE AVERAGE UNIQUENESS**

> ![](Image00629.jpg){width="557" height="154"}

[Snippet 4.5 gives us the index of the features sampled by sequential
bootstrap. The inputs are the indicator matrix (]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`indM` [) and an optional sample length (]{bgcolor="inherit"} `sLength`
[), with a default value of as many draws as rows in]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`indM` [.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 4.5 RETURN SAMPLE FROM SEQUENTIAL BOOTSTRAP**

> ![](Image00706.jpg){width="674" height="318"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **4.5.3 A Numerical Example** `</font>`{=html}

[Consider a set of labels {]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [}
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}=\ 1,\ 2,\ 3`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
, where label]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [is a function of
return]{bgcolor="inherit"} *r
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}0,\ 3`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [,
label]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}2`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [is a function of
return]{bgcolor="inherit"} *r
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}2,\ 4`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [and
label]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}3`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [is a function of
return]{bgcolor="inherit"} *r
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}4,\ 6`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [. The
outcomes' overlaps are characterized by this indicator matrix {1
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
},]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00183.jpg){width="157" height="144"}

[The procedure starts with φ
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}(0)`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^ = ∅, and a
uniform distribution of probability,]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00186.jpg){width="47" height="29"} [, ∀]{bgcolor="inherit"} *i*
[= 1, 2, 3]{bgcolor="inherit"} *.* [Suppose that we randomly draw a
number from {1, 2, 3}, and 2 is selected. Before we make a second draw
on {1, 2, 3} (remember, a bootstrap samples with repetition), we need to
adjust the probabilities. The set of observations drawn so far is φ
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}(1)`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^ = {2}. The
average uniqueness for the first feature is]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00266.jpg){width="232" height="36"} [, and for the second
feature is]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00194.jpg){width="203"
height="36"} *.* [The probabilities for the second draw
are]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00199.jpg){width="150" height="36"} [.
Two points are worth mentioning: (1) The lowest probability goes to the
feature that was picked in the first draw, as that would exhibit the
highest overlap; and (2) among the two possible draws outside φ
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}(1)`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^ , the greater
probability goes to δ
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}(2)`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}3`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ , as that is the
label with no overlap to φ
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}(1)`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^ . Suppose that
the second draw selects number 3. We leave as an exercise the update of
the probabilities δ
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}(3)`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^ for the third
and final draw. Snippet 4.6 runs a sequential bootstrap on the {1
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
} indicator matrix in this example.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **4.5.4 Monte Carlo Experiments**
`</font>`{=html}

[We can evaluate the efficiency of the sequential bootstrap algorithm
through experimental methods. Snippet 4.7 lists the function that
generates a random]{bgcolor="inherit"} `t1` [series for a number of
observations]{bgcolor="inherit"} `numObs` [(]{bgcolor="inherit"} *I* [).
Each observation is made at a random number, drawn from a uniform
distribution, with boundaries 0 and]{bgcolor="inherit"} `numBars` [,
where]{bgcolor="inherit"} `numBars` [is the number of bars
(]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [). The number of bars spanned by the
observation is determined by drawing a random number from a uniform
distribution with boundaries 0 and]{bgcolor="inherit"} `maxH`
[.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 4.7 GENERATING A RANDOM T1 SERIES**

> ![](Image00203.jpg){width="482" height="219"}

[Snippet 4.8 takes that random]{bgcolor="inherit"} `t1` [series, and
derives the implied indicator matrix,]{bgcolor="inherit"} `indM` [. This
matrix is then subjected to two procedures. In the first one, we derive
the average uniqueness from a standard bootstrap (random sampling with
replacement). In the second one, we derive the average uniqueness by
applying our sequential bootstrap algorithm. Results are reported as a
dictionary.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 4.8 UNIQUENESS FROM STANDARD AND SEQUENTIAL BOOTSTRAPS**

> ![](Image00356.jpg){width="690" height="265"}

[These operations have to be repeated over a large number of iterations.
Snippet 4.9 implements this Monte Carlo using the multiprocessing
techniques discussed in Chapter 20. For example, it will take about 6
hours for a 24-cores server to carry out a Monte Carlo of 1E6
iterations, where]{bgcolor="inherit"} `numObs=10` [,]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`numBars=100` [, and]{bgcolor="inherit"} `maxH=5` [. Without
parallelization, a similar Monte Carlo experiment would have taken about
6 days.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 4.9 MULTI-THREADED MONTE CARLO**

> ![](Image00377.jpg){width="871" height="355"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000310005}

[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
4.2`</font>`{=html}](#text00001.html#filepos0000310792) [plots the
histogram of the uniqueness from standard bootstrapped samples (left)
and the sequentially bootstrapped samples (right). The median of the
average uniqueness for the standard method is 0.6, and the median of the
average uniqueness for the sequential method is 0.7. An ANOVA test on
the difference of means returns a vanishingly small probability.
Statistically speaking, samples from the sequential bootstrap method
have an expected uniqueness that exceeds that of the standard bootstrap
method, at any reasonable confidence level.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00392.jpg){width="560" height="410"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000310792}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
4.2`</font>`{=html}**](#text00001.html#filepos0000310005) Monte Carlo
experiment of standard vs. sequential bootstraps

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000311001}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **4.6 Return Attribution** `</font>`{=html}

[In the previous section we learned a method to bootstrap samples closer
to IID. In this section we will introduce a method to weight those
samples for the purpose of training an ML algorithm. Highly overlapping
outcomes would have disproportionate weights if considered equal to
non-overlapping outcomes. At the same time, labels associated with large
absolute returns should be given more importance than labels with
negligible absolute returns. In short, we need to weight observations by
some function of both uniqueness and absolute
return.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[When labels are a function of the return sign ({ − 1, 1} for standard
labeling or {0, 1} for meta-labeling), the sample weights can be defined
in terms of the sum of the attributed returns over the event\'s
lifespan, \[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[\],]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00410.jpg){width="129" height="65"}

![](Image00220.jpg){width="161" height="66"}

[hence]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00656.jpg){width="92" height="25"}
[. We have scaled these weights to add up to]{bgcolor="inherit"} *I* [,
since libraries (including sklearn) usually define algorithmic
parameters assuming a default weight of 1.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[The rationale for this method is that we wish to weight an observation
as a function of the absolute log returns that can be attributed
uniquely to it. However, this method will not work if there is a
"neutral" (return below threshold) case. For that case, lower returns
should be assigned higher weights, not the reciprocal. The "neutral"
case is unnecessary, as it can be implied by a "−1" or "1" prediction
with low confidence. This is one of several reasons I would generally
advise you to drop "neutral" cases. Snippet 4.10 implements this
method.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 4.10 DETERMINATION OF SAMPLE WEIGHT BY ABSOLUTE RETURN
> ATTRIBUTION**

> ![](Image00115.jpg){width="881" height="291"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000313775}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **4.7 Time Decay** `</font>`{=html}

[Markets are adaptive systems (Lo \[2017\]). As markets evolve, older
examples are less relevant than the newer ones. Consequently, we would
typically like sample weights to decay as new observations arrive.
Let]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00119.jpg){width="211" height="36"} [be
the time-decay factors that will multiply the sample weights derived in
the previous section. The final weight has no decay,]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00123.jpg){width="122" height="36"} [, and all other weights
will be adjusted relative to that. Let]{bgcolor="inherit"} *c* [∈ ( −
1., .1\] be a user-defined parameter that determines the decay function
as follows: For]{bgcolor="inherit"} *c* [∈ \[0, 1\],
then]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [\[1\] =]{bgcolor="inherit"} *c* [, with
linear decay; for]{bgcolor="inherit"} *c* [∈ ( − 1, 0),
then]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00125.jpg){width="148" height="36"} [,
with linear decay between]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00128.jpg){width="165" height="36"} [and]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*d* [\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x* [\] = 0]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00130.jpg){width="126" height="25"} [. For a linear piecewise
function]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [= max{0,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *a*
[+]{bgcolor="inherit"} *bx* [}, such requirements are met by the
following boundary conditions:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

1.  ![](Image00134.jpg){width="335" height="25"} .
2.  Contingent on c:
    1.  ![](Image00137.jpg){width="334" height="41"} , ∀*c* ∈ \[0, 1\]
    2.  ![](Image00140.jpg){width="390" height="41"} , ∀*c* ∈ ( − 1, 0)

[Snippet 4.11 implements this form of time-decay factors. Note that time
is not meant to be chronological. In this implementation, decay takes
place according to cumulative uniqueness,]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00143.jpg){width="124" height="36"} [, because a chronological
decay would reduce weights too fast in the presence of redundant
observations.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 4.11 IMPLEMENTATION OF TIME-DECAY FACTORS**

> ![](Image00147.jpg){width="937" height="256"}

[It is worth discussing a few interesting cases:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

-   *c* = 1 means that there is no time decay.
-   0 \< *c* \< 1 means that weights decay linearly over time, but every
    observation still receives a strictly positive weight, regardless of
    how old.
-   *c* = 0 means that weights converge linearly to zero, as they become
    older.
-   *c* \< 0 means that the oldest portion *cT* of the observations
    receive zero weight (i.e., they are erased from memory).

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000317671}

[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
4.3`</font>`{=html}](#text00001.html#filepos0000318296) [shows the
decayed weights,]{bgcolor="inherit"} `out['w']*df` [, after applying the
decay factors for]{bgcolor="inherit"} *c* [∈ {1, .75, .5, 0, −.25, −.5}.
Although not necessarily practical, the procedure allows the possibility
of generating weights that increase as they get older, by
setting]{bgcolor="inherit"} *c* [\> 1.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00150.jpg){width="560" height="418"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000318296}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
4.3`</font>`{=html}**](#text00001.html#filepos0000317671)
Piecewise-linear time-decay factors

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000318480}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **4.8 Class Weights** `</font>`{=html}

[In addition to sample weights, it is often useful to apply class
weights. Class weights are weights that correct for underrepresented
labels. This is particularly critical in classification problems where
the most important classes have rare occurrences (King and Zeng
\[2001\]). For example, suppose that you wish to predict liquidity
crisis, like the flash crash of May 6, 2010. These events are rare
relative to the millions of observations that take place in between
them. Unless we assign higher weights to the samples associated with
those rare labels, the ML algorithm will maximize the accuracy of the
most common labels, and flash crashes will be deemed to be outliers
rather than rare events.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[ML libraries typically implement functionality to handle class weights.
For example, sklearn penalizes errors in samples of]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`class[j]` [,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *j=1,...,J* [, with
weighting]{bgcolor="inherit"} `class_weight[j]` [rather than 1.
Accordingly, higher class weights on label]{bgcolor="inherit"} *j* [will
force the algorithm to achieve higher accuracy on]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*j* [. When class weights do not add up to]{bgcolor="inherit"} *J* [,
the effect is equivalent to changing the regularization parameter of the
classifier.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[In financial applications, the standard labels of a classification
algorithm are { − 1, 1}, where the zero (or neutral) case will be
implied by a prediction with probability only slightly above 0.5 and
below some neutral threshold. There is no reason for favoring accuracy
of one class over the other, and as such a good default is to
assign]{bgcolor="inherit"} `class_weight='balanced'` [. This choice
re-weights observations so as to simulate that all classes appeared with
equal frequency. In the context of bagging classifiers, you may want to
consider the argument]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`class_weight='balanced_subsample'` [, which means
that]{bgcolor="inherit"} `class_weight='balanced'` [will be applied to
the in-bag bootstrapped samples, rather than to the entire dataset. For
full details, it is helpful to read the source code
implementing]{bgcolor="inherit"} `class_weight` [in sklearn. Please also
be aware of this reported bug:]{bgcolor="inherit"}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/4324`</font>`{=html}](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/4324)
[.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000321336}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **Exercises** `</font>`{=html}

::: {height="1em"}
:::

1.  [In Chapter 3, we denoted as]{bgcolor="inherit"} `t1` [a pandas
    > > series of timestamps where the first barrier was touched, and
    > > the index was the timestamp of the observation. This was the
    > > output of the]{bgcolor="inherit"} `getEvents`
    > > [function.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  Compute a `t1` series on dollar bars derived from E-mini S&P 500
        futures tick data.
    2.  Apply the function `mpNumCoEvents` to compute the number of
        overlapping outcomes at each point in time.
    3.  Plot the time series of the number of concurrent labels on the
        primary axis, and the time series of exponentially weighted
        moving standard deviation of returns on the secondary axis.
    4.  Produce a scatterplot of the number of concurrent labels
        (x-axis) and the exponentially weighted moving standard
        deviation of returns (y-axis). Can you appreciate a
        relationship?

2.  [Using the function]{bgcolor="inherit"} `mpSampleTW` [, compute the
    > > average uniqueness of each label. What is the first-order serial
    > > correlation, AR(1), of this time series? Is it statistically
    > > significant? Why?]{bgcolor="inherit"}

3.  [Fit a random forest to a financial dataset
    > > where]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00577.jpg){width="124"
    > > height="25"} [.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  What is the mean out-of-bag accuracy?
    2.  What is the mean accuracy of k-fold cross-validation (without
        shuffling) on the same dataset?
    3.  Why is out-of-bag accuracy so much higher than cross-validation
        accuracy? Which one is more correct / less biased? What is the
        source of this bias?

4.  [Modify the code in Section 4.7 to apply an exponential time-decay
    > > factor.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

5.  [Consider you have applied meta-labels to events determined by a
    > > trend-following model. Suppose that two thirds of the labels are
    > > 0 and one third of the labels are 1.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  What happens if you fit a classifier without balancing class
        weights?
    2.  A label 1 means a true positive, and a label 0 means a false
        positive. By applying balanced class weights, we are forcing the
        classifier to pay more attention to the true positives, and less
        attention to the false positives. Why does that make sense?
    3.  What is the distribution of the predicted labels, before and
        after applying balanced class weights?

6.  [Update the draw probabilities for the final draw in Section
    > > 4.5.3.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

7.  [In Section 4.5.3, suppose that number 2 is picked again in the
    > > second draw. What would be the updated probabilities for the
    > > third draw?]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **Bibliography** `</font>`{=html}

[Sample weighting is a common topic in the ML learning literature.
However the practical problems discussed in this chapter are
characteristic of investment applications, for which the academic
literature is extremely scarce. Below are some publications that
tangentially touch some of the issues discussed in this
chapter.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

1.  Efron, B. (1979): "Bootstrap methods: Another look at the
    jackknife." *Annals of Statistics* , Vol. 7, pp. 1--26.
2.  Efron, B. (1983): "Estimating the error rote of a prediction rule:
    Improvement on cross-validation." *Journal of the American
    Statistical Association* , Vol. 78, pp. 316--331.
3.  Bickel, P. and D. Freedman (1981): "Some asymptotic theory for the
    bootstrap." *Annals of Statistics* , Vol. 9, pp. 1196--1217.
4.  Gine, E. and J. Zinn (1990): "Bootstrapping general empirical
    measures." *Annals of Probability* , Vol. 18, pp. 851--869.
5.  Hall, P. and E. Mammen (1994): "On general resampling algorithms and
    their performance in distribution estimation." *Annals of
    Statistics* , Vol. 24, pp. 2011--2030.
6.  Mitra, S. and P. Pathak (1984): "The nature of simple random
    sampling." *Annals of Statistics* , Vol. 12, pp. 1536--1542.
7.  Pathak, P. (1964): "Sufficiency in sampling theory." *Annals of
    Mathematical Statistics* , Vol. 35, pp. 795--808.
8.  Pathak, P. (1964): "On inverse sampling with unequal probabilities."
    *Biometrika* , Vol. 51, pp. 185--193.
9.  Praestgaard, J. and J. Wellner (1993): "Exchangeably weighted
    bootstraps of the general empirical process." *Annals of
    Probability* , Vol. 21, pp. 2053--2086.
10. Rao, C., P. Pathak and V. Koltchinskii (1997): "Bootstrap by
    sequential resampling." *Journal of Statistical Planning and
    Inference* , Vol. 64, No. 2, pp. 257--281.

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000329259}

`<font size="5">`{=html} **CHAPTER 5** `</font>`{=html}\
\
`<font size="5">`{=html} **Fractionally Differentiated Features**
`</font>`{=html}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000329512}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **5.1 Motivation** `</font>`{=html}

[It is known that, as a consequence of arbitrage forces, financial
series exhibit low signal-to-noise ratios (López de Prado \[2015\]). To
make matters worse, standard stationarity transformations, like integer
differentiation, further reduce that signal by removing memory. Price
series have memory, because every value is dependent upon a long history
of previous levels. In contrast, integer differentiated series, like
returns, have a memory cut-off, in the sense that history is disregarded
entirely after a finite sample window. Once stationarity transformations
have wiped out all memory from the data, statisticians resort to complex
mathematical techniques to extract whatever residual signal remains. Not
surprisingly, applying these complex techniques on memory-erased series
likely leads to false discoveries. In this chapter we introduce a data
transformation method that ensures the stationarity of the data while
preserving as much memory as possible.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000330658}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **5.2 The Stationarity vs. Memory Dilemma**
`</font>`{=html}

[It is common in finance to find non-stationary time series. What makes
these series non-stationary is the presence of memory, i.e., a long
history of previous levels that shift the series\' mean over time. In
order to perform inferential analyses, researchers need to work with
invariant processes, such as returns on prices (or changes in
log-prices), changes in yield, or changes in volatility. These data
transformations make the series stationary, at the expense of removing
all memory from the original series (Alexander \[2001\], chapter 11).
Although stationarity is a necessary property for inferential purposes,
it is rarely the case in signal processing that we wish all memory to be
erased, as that memory is the basis for the model\'s predictive power.
For example, equilibrium (stationary) models need some memory to assess
how far the price process has drifted away from the long-term expected
value in order to generate a forecast. The dilemma is that returns are
stationary, however memory-less, and prices have memory, however they
are non-stationary. The question arises: What is the minimum amount of
differentiation that makes a price series stationary while preserving as
much memory as possible? Accordingly, we would like to generalize the
notion of returns to consider]{bgcolor="inherit"} *stationary series
where not all memory is erased.* [Under this framework, returns are just
one kind of (and in most cases suboptimal) price transformation among
many other possibilities.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Part of the importance of cointegration methods is their ability to
model series with memory. But why would the particular case of zero
differentiation deliver best outcomes? Zero differentiation is as
arbitrary as 1-step differentiation. There is a wide region between
these two extremes (fully differentiated series on one hand, and zero
differentiated series on the other) that can be explored through
fractional differentiation for the purpose of developing a highly
predictive ML model.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Supervised learning algorithms typically require stationary features.
The reason is that we need to map a previously unseen (unlabeled)
observation to a collection of labeled examples, and infer from them the
label of that new observation. If the features are not stationary, we
cannot map the new observation to a large number of known examples. But
stationarity does not ensure predictive power. Stationarity is a
necessary, non-sufficient condition for the high performance of an ML
algorithm. The problem is, there is a trade-off between stationarity and
memory. We can always make a series more stationary through
differentiation, but it will be at the cost of erasing some memory,
which will defeat the forecasting purpose of the ML algorithm. In this
chapter, we will study one way to resolve this
dilemma.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000333813}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **5.3 Literature Review** `</font>`{=html}

[Virtually all the financial time series literature is based on the
premise of making non-stationary series stationary through integer
transformation (see Hamilton \[1994\] for an example). This raises two
questions: (1) Why would integer 1 differentiation (like the one used
for computing returns on log-prices) be optimal? (2) Is
over-differentiation one reason why the literature has been so biased in
favor of the efficient markets hypothesis?]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[The notion of fractional differentiation applied to the predictive time
series analysis dates back at least to Hosking \[1981\]. In that paper,
a family of ARIMA processes was generalized by permitting the degree of
differencing to take fractional values. This was useful because
fractionally differenced processes exhibit long-term persistence and
antipersistence, hence enhancing the forecasting power compared to the
standard ARIMA approach. In the same paper, Hosking states: "Apart from
a passing reference by Granger (1978), fractional differencing does not
appear to have been previously mentioned in connection with time series
analysis."]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[After Hosking\'s paper, the literature on this subject has been
surprisingly scarce, adding up to eight journal articles written by only
nine authors: Hosking, Johansen, Nielsen, MacKinnon, Jensen, Jones,
Popiel, Cavaliere, and Taylor. See the references for details. Most of
those papers relate to technical matters, such as fast algorithms for
the calculation of fractional differentiation in continuous stochastic
processes (e.g., Jensen and Nielsen \[2014\]).]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Differentiating the stochastic process is a computationally expensive
operation. In this chapter we will take a practical, alternative, and
novel approach to recover stationarity: We will generalize the
difference operator to non-integer steps.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000335994}
