> **SNIPPET 5.1 WEIGHTING FUNCTION**

> ![](Image00246.jpg){width="851" height="592"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **5.4.3 Convergence** `</font>`{=html}

[Let us consider the convergence of the weights. From the above result,
we can see that for]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [\>]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d*
[, if ω
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *k*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}−\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
≠ 0, then]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00190.jpg){width="160"
height="44"} [, and ω
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *k*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ = 0
otherwise. Consequently, the weights converge asymptotically to zero, as
an infinite product of factors within the unit circle. Also, for a
positive]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k*
[\<]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [+ 1, we have]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00281.jpg){width="78" height="29"} [, which makes the initial
weights alternate in sign. For a non-integer]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [,
once]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [≥]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [+ 1, ω
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *k*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ will be
negative if int\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [\] is even, and positive
otherwise. Summarizing,]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00300.jpg){width="101" height="26"} [(converges to zero from
the left) when int\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [\] is even,
and]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00202.jpg){width="101" height="28"}
[(converges to zero from the right) when Int\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d*
[\] is odd. In the special case]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [∈ (0, 1), this
means that − 1 \< ω
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *k*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ \< 0,
∀]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [\> 0]{bgcolor="inherit"} *.* [This
alternation of weight signs is necessary to make]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00336.jpg){width="91" height="24"} [stationary, as memory wanes
or is offset over the long run.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000345623}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **5.5 Implementation** `</font>`{=html}

[In this section we will explore two alternative implementations of
fractional differentiation: the standard "expanding window" method, and
a new method that I call "fixed-width window fracdiff"
(FFD).]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **5.5.1 Expanding Window** `</font>`{=html}

[Let us discuss how to fractionally differentiate a (finite) time series
in practice. Suppose a time series with]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [real
observations, {]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[}, ]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [= 1, ...,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [.
Because of data limitations, the fractionally differentiated
value]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00206.jpg){width="23" height="21"}
[cannot be computed on an infinite series of weights. For instance, the
last point]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00209.jpg){width="23"
height="21"} [will use weights {ω
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *k*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
}, ]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [= 0, ...,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [− 1,
and]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00213.jpg){width="37" height="21"}
[will use weights {ω
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *k*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
}, ]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [= 0, ...,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T*
[−]{bgcolor="inherit"} *l* [− 1. This means that the initial points will
have a different amount of memory compared to the final points. For
each]{bgcolor="inherit"} *l* [, we can determine the relative
weight-loss,]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00216.jpg){width="111"
height="46"} [. Given a tolerance level τ ∈ \[0, 1\], we can determine
the value]{bgcolor="inherit"} *l* [\* such that]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00425.jpg){width="55" height="18"} [and]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00741.jpg){width="73" height="19"} [. This
value]{bgcolor="inherit"} *l* [\* corresponds to the first
results]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00744.jpg){width="87" height="24"}
[where the weight-loss is beyond the acceptable threshold, λ
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ \> τ (e.g.,
τ = 0.01)]{bgcolor="inherit"} *.*

[From our earlier discussion, it is clear that]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00749.jpg){width="20" height="18"} [depends on the convergence
speed of {ω ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *k*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
}, which in turn depends on]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [∈ \[0, 1\].
For]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [= 1, ω
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *k*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ =
0, ∀]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [\> 1, and λ
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *l*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ =
0, ∀]{bgcolor="inherit"} *l* [\> 1, hence it suffices to
drop]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00753.jpg){width="19" height="21"} [.
As]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [→ 0
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}+`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *l* [\* increases, and a larger portion of the
initial]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00754.jpg){width="87" height="24"}
[needs to be dropped in order to keep the
weight-loss]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00755.jpg){width="55"
height="18"} [.]{bgcolor="inherit"}
[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000349525}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
5.3`</font>`{=html}](#text00001.html#filepos0000350067) [plots the
E-mini S&P 500 futures trade bars of size 1E4, rolled forward,
fractionally differentiated, with parameters (]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d*
[= .4, τ = 1) on the top and parameters (]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [= .4,
τ = 1]{bgcolor="inherit"} *E* [− 2) on the bottom.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00758.jpg){width="360" height="600"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000350067}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
5.3`</font>`{=html}**](#text00001.html#filepos0000349525) Fractional
differentiation without controlling for weight loss (top plot) and after
controlling for weight loss with an expanding window (bottom plot)

[The negative drift in both plots is caused by the negative weights that
are added to the initial observations as the window is expanded. When we
do not control for weight loss, the negative drift is extreme, to the
point that only that trend is visible. The negative drift is somewhat
more moderate in the right plot, after controlling for the weight loss,
however, it is still substantial, because values]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00762.jpg){width="108" height="24"} [are computed on an
expanding window. This problem can be corrected by a fixed-width window,
implemented in Snippet 5.2.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 5.2 STANDARD FRACDIFF (EXPANDING WINDOW)**

> ![](Image00767.jpg){width="920" height="616"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **5.5.2 Fixed-Width Window Fracdiff**
`</font>`{=html}

[Alternatively, fractional differentiation can be computed using a
fixed-width window, that is, dropping the weights after their modulus
(\|ω ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *k*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ \|)
falls below a given threshold value (τ)]{bgcolor="inherit"} *.* [This is
equivalent to finding the first]{bgcolor="inherit"} *l* [\* such
that]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00771.jpg){width="69" height="19"}
[and]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00775.jpg){width="87" height="20"} [,
setting a new variable]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00779.jpg){width="20" height="18"}

![](Image00781.jpg){width="169" height="48"}

[and]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00783.jpg){width="142" height="26"} [,
for]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [=]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T*
[−]{bgcolor="inherit"} *l* [\* + 1, ...,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T*
[.]{bgcolor="inherit"} []{#text00001.html#filepos0000352607}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
5.4`</font>`{=html}](#text00001.html#filepos0000353033) [plots E-mini
S&P 500 futures trade bars of size 1E4, rolled forward, fractionally
differentiated (]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [= .4, τ =
1]{bgcolor="inherit"} *E* [− 5).]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00786.jpg){width="613" height="480"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000353033}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
5.4`</font>`{=html}**](#text00001.html#filepos0000352607) Fractional
differentiation after controlling for weight loss with a fixed-width
window

[This procedure has the advantage that the same vector of weights is
used across all estimates of]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00791.jpg){width="91" height="24"} [, hence avoiding the
negative drift caused by an expanding window\'s added weights. The
result is a driftless blend of level plus noise, as expected. The
distribution is no longer Gaussian, as a result of the skewness and
excess kurtosis that comes with memory, however it is stationary.
Snippet 5.3 presents an implementation of this idea.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 5.3 THE NEW FIXED-WIDTH WINDOW FRACDIFF METHOD**

> ![](Image00794.jpg){width="919" height="536"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000354132}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **5.6 Stationarity with Maximum Memory
Preservation** `</font>`{=html}

[Consider a series {]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [}
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}=\ 1,\ ...,`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *T*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
. Applying the fixed-width window fracdiff (FFD) method on this series,
we can compute the minimum coefficient]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [\* such
that the resulting fractionally differentiated
series]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00798.jpg){width="91" height="24"}
[is stationary. This coefficient]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [\* quantifies
the amount of memory that needs to be removed to achieve stationarity.
If]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00801.jpg){width="91" height="20"} [is
already stationary, then]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [\* =
0]{bgcolor="inherit"} *.* [If]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00803.jpg){width="91" height="20"} [contains a unit root,
then]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [\* \< 1]{bgcolor="inherit"} *.*
[If]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00806.jpg){width="91" height="20"}
[exhibits explosive behavior (like in a bubble),
then]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [\* \> 1]{bgcolor="inherit"} *.* [A case of
particular interest is 0 \<]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [\* ≪ 1, when the
original series is "mildly non-stationary." In this case, although
differentiation is needed, a full integer differentiation removes
excessive memory (and predictive power).]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000355959}

[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
5.5`</font>`{=html}](#text00001.html#filepos0000357571) [illustrates
this concept. On the right y-axis, it plots the ADF statistic computed
on E-mini S&P 500 futures log-prices, rolled forward using the ETF trick
(see Chapter 2), downsampled to daily frequency, going back to the
contract\'s inception. On the x-axis, it displays
the]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [value used to generate the series on which
the ADF statistic was computed. The original series has an ADF statistic
of --0.3387, while the returns series has an ADF statistic of --46.9114.
At a 95% confidence level, the test\'s critical value is --2.8623. The
ADF statistic crosses that threshold in the vicinity
of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [= 0.35]{bgcolor="inherit"} *.* [The left
y-axis plots the correlation between the original series
(]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [= 0) and the differentiated series at
various]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [values. At]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [=
0.35 the correlation is still very high, at 0.995. This confirms that
the procedure introduced in this chapter has been successful in
achieving stationarity without giving up too much memory. In contrast,
the correlation between the original series and the returns series is
only 0.03, hence showing that the standard integer differentiation wipes
out the series' memory almost entirely.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00808.jpg){width="640" height="446"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000357571}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
5.5`</font>`{=html}**](#text00001.html#filepos0000355959) ADF statistic
as a function of *d* , on E-mini S&P 500 futures log-prices

[Virtually all finance papers attempt to recover stationarity by
applying an integer differentiation]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [= 1 ≫ 0.35,
which means that most studies have over-differentiated the series, that
is, they have removed much more memory than was necessary to satisfy
standard econometric assumptions. Snippet 5.4 lists the code used to
produce these results.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 5.4 FINDING THE MINIMUM** ***D*** **VALUE THAT PASSES THE
> ADF TEST**

> ![](Image00809.jpg){width="896" height="397"}

[The example on E-mini futures is by no means an
exception.]{bgcolor="inherit"} []{#text00001.html#filepos0000358629}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Table 5.1`</font>`{=html}](#text00001.html#filepos0000359372)
[shows the ADF statistics after applying FFD(]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [)
on various values of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [, for 87 of the most
liquid futures worldwide. In all cases, the standard]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*d* [= 1 used for computing returns implies over-differentiation. In
fact, in all cases stationarity is achieved with]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d*
[\< 0.6]{bgcolor="inherit"} *.* [In some cases, like orange juice (JO1
Comdty) or live cattle (LC1 Comdty) no differentiation at all was
needed.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000359372}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Table
5.1`</font>`{=html}**](#text00001.html#filepos0000358629) **ADF
Statistic on FFD(** ***d*** **) for Some of the Most Liquid Futures
Contracts**

![](Image00812.jpg){width="822" height="390"}

![](Image00815.jpg){width="820" height="424"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000359851}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **5.7 Conclusion** `</font>`{=html}

[To summarize, most econometric analyses follow one of two
paradigms:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

1.  Box-Jenkins: Returns are stationary, however memory-less.
2.  Engle-Granger: Log-prices have memory, however they are
    non-stationary. Cointegration is the trick that makes regression
    work on non-stationary series, so that memory is preserved. However
    the number of cointegrated variables is limited, and the
    cointegrating vectors are notoriously unstable.

[In contrast, the FFD approach introduced in this chapter shows that
there is no need to give up all of the memory in order to gain
stationarity. And there is no need for the cointegration trick as it
relates to ML forecasting. Once you become familiar with FFD, it will
allow you to achieve stationarity without renouncing to memory (or
predictive power).]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[In practice, I suggest you experiment with the following transformation
of your features: First, compute a cumulative sum of the time series.
This guarantees that some order of differentiation is needed. Second,
compute the FFD(]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [) series for
various]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [∈ \[0, 1\]. Third, determine the
minimum]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [such that the p-value of the ADF
statistic on FFD(]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [) falls below 5%. Fourth, use
the FFD(]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [) series as your predictive
feature.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000361715}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **Exercises** `</font>`{=html}

::: {height="1em"}
:::

1.  [Generate a time series from an IID Gaussian random process. This is
    > > a memory-less, stationary series:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  Compute the ADF statistic on this series. What is the p-value?
    2.  Compute the cumulative sum of the observations. This is a
        non-stationary series without memory.
        1.  What is the order of integration of this cumulative series?
        2.  Compute the ADF statistic on this series. What is the
            p-value?
    3.  Differentiate the series twice. What is the p-value of this
        over-differentiated series?

2.  [Generate a time series that follows a sinusoidal function. This is
    > > a stationary series with memory.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  Compute the ADF statistic on this series. What is the p-value?
    2.  Shift every observation by the same positive value. Compute the
        cumulative sum of the observations. This is a non-stationary
        series with memory.
        1.  Compute the ADF statistic on this series. What is the
            p-value?
        2.  Apply an expanding window fracdiff, with τ = 1*E* − 2*.* For
            what minimum *d* value do you get a p-value below 5%?
        3.  Apply FFD, with τ = 1*E* − 5*.* For what minimum *d* value
            do you get a p-value below 5%?

3.  [Take the series from exercise 2.b:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  Fit the series to a sine function. What is the R-squared?
    2.  Apply FFD(*d = 1* ). Fit the series to a sine function. What is
        the R-squared?
    3.  What value of *d* maximizes the R-squared of a sinusoidal fit on
        FFD(*d* ). Why?

4.  [Take the dollar bar series on E-mini S&P 500 futures. Using the
    > > code in Snippet 5.3, for some]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [∈ \[0,
    > > 2\], compute]{bgcolor="inherit"}
    > > `fracDiff_FFD(fracDiff_FFD(series,d),-d)` [. What do you get?
    > > Why?]{bgcolor="inherit"}

5.  [Take the dollar bar series on E-mini S&P 500
    > > futures.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  Form a new series as a cumulative sum of log-prices.
    2.  Apply FFD, with τ = 1*E* − 5*.* Determine for what minimum *d* ∈
        \[0, 2\] the new series is stationary.
    3.  Compute the correlation of the fracdiff series to the original
        (untransformed) series.
    4.  Apply an Engel-Granger cointegration test on the original and
        fracdiff series. Are they cointegrated? Why?
    5.  Apply a Jarque-Bera normality test on the fracdiff series.

6.  [Take the fracdiff series from exercise 5.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  Apply a CUSUM filter (Chapter 2), where *h* is twice the
        standard deviation of the series.
    2.  Use the filtered timestamps to sample a features' matrix. Use as
        one of the features the fracdiff value.
    3.  Form labels using the triple-barrier method, with symmetric
        horizontal barriers of twice the daily standard deviation, and a
        vertical barrier of 5 days.
    4.  Fit a bagging classifier of decision trees where:
        1.  The observed features are bootstrapped using the sequential
            method from Chapter 4.
        2.  On each bootstrapped sample, sample weights are determined
            using the techniques from Chapter 4.

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000367476}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000371207}

`<font size="5">`{=html} **CHAPTER 6** `</font>`{=html}\
\
`<font size="5">`{=html} **Ensemble Methods** `</font>`{=html}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000371440}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **6.1 Motivation** `</font>`{=html}

[In this chapter we will discuss two of the most popular ML ensemble
methods.^[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000371661}\ [[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ `<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}1`</font>`{=html}\ `</font>`{=html}](#text00001.html#filepos0000402654)]{.small}^
In the references and footnotes you will find books and articles that
introduce these techniques. As everywhere else in this book, the
assumption is that you have already used these approaches. The goal of
this chapter is to explain what makes them effective, and how to avoid
common errors that lead to their misuse in finance.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000372131}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **6.2 The Three Sources of Errors**
`</font>`{=html}

[ML models generally suffer from three
errors:^[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000372338}\ [[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ `<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}2`</font>`{=html}\ `</font>`{=html}](#text00001.html#filepos0000403004)]{.small}^
]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

1.  **Bias:** This error is caused by unrealistic assumptions. When bias
    is high, the ML algorithm has failed to recognize important
    relations between features and outcomes. In this situation, the
    algorithm is said to be "underfit."
2.  **Variance:** This error is caused by sensitivity to small changes
    in the training set. When variance is high, the algorithm has
    overfit the training set, and that is why even minimal changes in
    the training set can produce wildly different predictions. Rather
    than modelling the general patterns in the training set, the
    algorithm has mistaken noise with signal.
3.  **Noise:** This error is caused by the variance of the observed
    values, like unpredictable changes or measurement errors. This is
    the irreducible error, which cannot be explained by any model.

[Consider a training set of observations {]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [}
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}=\ 1,\ ...,`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *n*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
and real-valued outcomes {]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [}
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}=\ 1,\ ...,`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *n*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
. Suppose a function]{bgcolor="inherit"} *f* [\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x*
[\] exists, such that]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y* [=]{bgcolor="inherit"} *f*
[\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x* [\] + ϵ, where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *ϵ* [is
white noise with E\[ϵ
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ \] = 0 and
E\[ϵ ^[`<font size="2">`{=html}2`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ \] = σ
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}ϵ`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}2`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^ . We would like to
estimate the function]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00818.jpg){width="31"
height="21"} [that best fits]{bgcolor="inherit"} *f*
[\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x* [\], in the sense of making the variance of
the estimation error]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00821.jpg){width="115"
height="27"} [minimal (the mean squared error cannot be zero, because of
the noise represented by σ
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}2`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}ϵ`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ ). This
mean-squared error can be decomposed as]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00826.jpg){width="450" height="96"}

[An ensemble method is a method that combines a set of weak learners,
all based on the same learning algorithm, in order to create a
(stronger) learner that performs better than any of the individual ones.
Ensemble methods help reduce bias and/or variance.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000375734}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **6.3 Bootstrap Aggregation** `</font>`{=html}

[Bootstrap aggregation (bagging) is an effective way of reducing the
variance in forecasts. It works as follows: First,
generate]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [training datasets by random
sampling]{bgcolor="inherit"} *with replacement.* [Second,
fit]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [estimators, one on each training set. These
estimators are fit independently from each other, hence the models can
be fit in parallel. Third, the ensemble forecast is
the]{bgcolor="inherit"} *simple* [average of the individual forecasts
from the]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [models. In the case of categorical
variables, the probability that an observation belongs to a class is
given by the proportion of estimators that classify that observation as
a member of that class (majority voting). When the base estimator can
make forecasts with a prediction probability, the bagging classifier may
derive a mean of the probabilities.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[If you use sklearn\'s]{bgcolor="inherit"} `BaggingClassifier` [class to
compute the out-of-bag accuracy, you should be aware of this
bug:]{bgcolor="inherit"}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/8933`</font>`{=html}](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/8933)
[. One workaround is to rename the labels in integer sequential
order.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **6.3.1 Variance Reduction** `</font>`{=html}

[Bagging\'s main advantage is that it reduces forecasts' variance, hence
helping address overfitting. The variance of the bagged prediction (φ
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *c* [\]) is a function of the number of bagged
estimators (]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [), the average variance of a
single estimator\'s prediction (]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00830.jpg){width="10" height="12"} [), and the average
correlation among their forecasts (]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00833.jpg){width="10" height="16"} [):]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00836.jpg){width="533" height="334"}

[where σ
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
is the covariance of predictions by estimators]{bgcolor="inherit"} *i*
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *j* [;]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00838.jpg){width="80" height="25"}
![](Image00843.jpg){width="232" height="26"} [; and]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00849.jpg){width="369" height="28"}
![](Image00850.jpg){width="92" height="28"} [.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[The equation above shows that bagging is only effective to the extent
that]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00851.jpg){width="42" height="17"} [;
as]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00223.jpg){width="252" height="29"} [.
One of the goals of sequential bootstrapping (Chapter 4) is to produce
samples as independent as possible, thereby reducing]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00003.jpg){width="10" height="16"} [, which should lower the
variance of bagging classifiers.]{bgcolor="inherit"}
[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000379588}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
6.1`</font>`{=html}](#text00001.html#filepos0000380113) [plots the
standard deviation of the bagged prediction as a function
of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [∈ \[5, 30\],]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00007.jpg){width="73" height="17"} [and]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00012.jpg){width="43" height="13"} *.*

![](Image00083.jpg){width="627" height="480"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000380113}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
6.1`</font>`{=html}**](#text00001.html#filepos0000379588) Standard
deviation of the bagged prediction

`<font size="4">`{=html} **6.3.2 Improved Accuracy** `</font>`{=html}

[Consider a bagging classifier that makes a prediction
on]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [classes by majority voting
among]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [independent classifiers. We can label the
predictions as {0, 1}, where 1 means a correct prediction. The accuracy
of a classifier is the probability]{bgcolor="inherit"} *p* [of labeling
a prediction as 1. On average we will get]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Np*
[predictions labeled as 1, with variance]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Np* [(1
−]{bgcolor="inherit"} *p* [). Majority voting makes the correct
prediction when the most forecasted class is observed. For example,
for]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [= 10 and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [= 3, the
bagging classifier made a correct prediction when
class]{bgcolor="inherit"} *A* [was observed and the cast votes were
\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *A* [,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *B*
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *C* [\] = \[4, 3, 3\]. However, the bagging
classifier made an incorrect prediction when class]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*A* [was observed and the cast votes were \[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *A*
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *B* [,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *C* [\] = \[4, 1, 5\].
A sufficient condition is that the sum of these labels
is]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00016.jpg){width="51" height="28"} [. A
necessary (non-sufficient) condition is that]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00019.jpg){width="51" height="28"} [, which occurs with
probability]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00138.jpg){width="468" height="71"}

[The implication is that for a sufficiently large]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*N* [, say]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00026.jpg){width="130"
height="26"} [, then]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00174.jpg){width="184"
height="29"} [, hence the bagging classifier\'s accuracy exceeds the
average accuracy of the individual classifiers. Snippet 6.1 implements
this calculation.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 6.1 ACCURACY OF THE BAGGING CLASSIFIER**

> ![](Image00031.jpg){width="421" height="155"}

[This is a strong argument in favor of bagging any classifier in
general, when computational requirements permit it. However, unlike
boosting, bagging cannot improve the accuracy of poor classifiers: If
the individual learners are poor classifiers (]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00035.jpg){width="50" height="29"} [), majority voting will
still perform poorly (although with lower variance).]{bgcolor="inherit"}
[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000383462}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
6.2`</font>`{=html}](#text00001.html#filepos0000384361) [illustrates
these facts. Because it is easier to achieve]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00037.jpg){width="47" height="17"} [than]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00041.jpg){width="45" height="29"} [, bagging is more likely to
be successful in reducing variance than in reducing
bias.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[For further analysis on this topic, the reader is directed to
Condorcet\'s Jury Theorem. Although the theorem is derived for the
purposes of majority voting in political science, the problem addressed
by this theorem shares similarities with the above
discussion.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00269.jpg){width="640" height="467"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000384361}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
6.2`</font>`{=html}**](#text00001.html#filepos0000383462) Accuracy of a
bagging classifier as a function of the individual estimator\'s accuracy
(*P* ), the number of estimators (*N* ), and *k * = 2

`<font size="4">`{=html} **6.3.3 Observation Redundancy**
`</font>`{=html}

[In Chapter 4 we studied one reason why financial observations cannot be
assumed to be IID. Redundant observations have two detrimental effects
on bagging. First, the samples drawn with replacement are more likely to
be virtually identical, even if they do not share the same observations.
This makes]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00046.jpg){width="42"
height="17"} [, and bagging will not reduce variance, regardless
of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N.* [For example, if each observation
at]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [is labeled according to the return
between]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t + 100* [, we
should sample 1% of the observations per bagged estimator, but not more.
Chapter 4, Section 4.5 recommended three alternative solutions, one of
which consisted of setting]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`max_samples=out[‘tW’].mean()` [in sklearn\'s implementation of the
bagging classifier class. Another (better) solution was to apply the
sequential bootstrap method.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[The second detrimental effect from observation redundancy is that
out-of-bag accuracy will be inflated. This happens because random
sampling with replacement places in the training set samples that are
very similar to those out-of-bag. In such a case, a proper stratified
k-fold cross-validation without shuffling before partitioning will show
a much lower testing-set accuracy than the one estimated out-of-bag. For
this reason, it is advisable to set]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`StratifiedKFold(n_splits=k,` `shuffle=False)` [when using that sklearn
class, cross-validate the bagging classifier, and ignore the out-of-bag
accuracy results. A low number]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [is preferred to
a high one, as excessive partitioning would again place in the testing
set samples too similar to those used in the training
set.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000386869}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **6.4 Random Forest** `</font>`{=html}

[Decision trees are known to be prone to overfitting, which increases
the variance of the
forecasts.^[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000387116}\ [[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ `<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}3`</font>`{=html}\ `</font>`{=html}](#text00001.html#filepos0000403445)]{.small}^
In order to address this concern, the random forest (RF) method was
designed to produce ensemble forecasts with lower
variance.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[RF shares some similarities with bagging, in the sense of training
independently individual estimators over bootstrapped subsets of the
data. The key difference with bagging is that random forests incorporate
a second level of randomness: When optimizing each node split, only a
random subsample (without replacement) of the attributes will be
evaluated, with the purpose of further decorrelating the
estimators.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Like bagging, RF reduces forecasts' variance without overfitting
(remember, as long as]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00050.jpg){width="42"
height="17"} [). A second advantage is that RF evaluates feature
importance, which we will discuss in depth in Chapter 8. A third
advantage is that RF provides out-of-bag accuracy estimates, however in
financial applications they are likely to be inflated (as discussed in
Section 6.3.3). But like bagging, RF will not necessarily exhibit lower
bias than individual decision trees.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[If a large number of samples are redundant (non-IID), overfitting will
still take place: Sampling randomly with replacement will build a large
number of essentially identical trees (]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00322.jpg){width="42" height="17"} [), where each decision tree
is overfit (a flaw for which decision trees are notorious). Unlike
bagging, RF always fixes the size of the bootstrapped samples to match
the size of the training dataset. Let us review ways we can address this
RF overfitting problem in sklearn. For illustration purposes, I will
refer to sklearn\'s classes; however, these solutions can be applied to
any implementation:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

1.  Set a parameter `max_features` to a lower value, as a way of forcing
    discrepancy between trees.
2.  Early stopping: Set the regularization parameter
    `min_weight_fraction_leaf` to a sufficiently large value (e.g., 5%)
    such that out-of-bag accuracy converges to out-of-sample (k-fold)
    accuracy.
3.  Use `BaggingClassifier` on `DecisionTreeClassifier` where
    `max_samples` is set to the average uniqueness (`avgU` ) between
    samples.
    1.  `clf=DecisionTreeClassifier(criterion=‘entropy’,max_features=‘auto’,class_weight=‘balanced’)`
    2.  `bc=BaggingClassifier(base_estimator=clf,n_estimators=1000,max_samples=avgU,max_features=1.)`
4.  Use `BaggingClassifier` on `RandomForestClassifier` where
    `max_samples` is set to the average uniqueness (`avgU` ) between
    samples.
    1.  `clf=RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=1,criterion=‘entropy’,bootstrap=False,class_weight=‘balanced_subsample’)`
    2.  `bc=BaggingClassifier(base_estimator=clf,n_estimators=1000,max_samples=avgU,max_features=1.)`
5.  Modify the RF class to replace standard bootstrapping with
    sequential bootstrapping.

[In summary, Snippet 6.2 demonstrates three alternative ways of setting
up an RF, using different classes.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 6.2 THREE WAYS OF SETTING UP AN RF**

> ![](Image00055.jpg){width="949" height="235"}

[When fitting decision trees, a rotation of the features space in a
direction that aligns with the axes typically reduces the number of
levels needed by the tree. For this reason, I suggest you fit RF on a
PCA of the features, as that may speed up calculations and reduce some
overfitting (more on this in Chapter 8). Also, as discussed in Chapter
4, Section 4.8,]{bgcolor="inherit"} `class_weight=‘balanced_subsample’`
[will help you prevent the trees from misclassifying minority
classes.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000392033}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **6.5 Boosting** `</font>`{=html}

[Kearns and Valiant \[1989\] were among the first to ask whether one
could combine weak estimators in order to achieve one with high
accuracy. Shortly after, Schapire \[1990\] demonstrated that the answer
to that question was affirmative, using the procedure we today call
boosting. In general terms, it works as follows: First, generate one
training set by random sampling with replacement, according to some
sample weights (initialized with uniform weights). Second, fit one
estimator using that training set. Third, if the single estimator
achieves an accuracy greater than the acceptance threshold (e.g., 50% in
a binary classifier, so that it performs better than chance), the
estimator is kept, otherwise it is discarded. Fourth, give more weight
to misclassified observations, and less weight to correctly classified
observations. Fifth, repeat the previous steps until]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*N* [estimators are produced. Sixth, the ensemble forecast is
the]{bgcolor="inherit"} *weighted* [average of the individual forecasts
from the]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [models, where the weights are
determined by the accuracy of the individual estimators. There are many
boosting algorithms, of which AdaBoost is one of the most popular (Geron
\[2017\]).]{bgcolor="inherit"} []{#text00001.html#filepos0000393467}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
6.3`</font>`{=html}](#text00001.html#filepos0000393759) [summarizes the
decision flow of a standard AdaBoost implementation.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00060.jpg){width="448" height="600"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000393759}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
6.3`</font>`{=html}**](#text00001.html#filepos0000393467) AdaBoost
decision flow

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000393930}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **6.6 Bagging vs. Boosting in Finance**
`</font>`{=html}

[From the above description, a few aspects make boosting quite different
from
bagging:^[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000394181}\ [[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ `<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}4`</font>`{=html}\ `</font>`{=html}](#text00001.html#filepos0000403829)]{.small}^
]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

-   Individual classifiers are fit sequentially.
-   Poor-performing classifiers are dismissed.
-   Observations are weighted differently in each iteration.
-   The ensemble forecast is a weighted average of the individual
    learners.

[Boosting\'s main advantage is that it reduces both variance and bias in
forecasts. However, correcting bias comes at the cost of greater risk of
overfitting. It could be argued that in financial applications bagging
is generally preferable to boosting. Bagging addresses overfitting,
while boosting addresses underfitting. Overfitting is often a greater
concern than underfitting, as it is not difficult to overfit an ML
algorithm to financial data, because of the low signal-to-noise ratio.
Furthermore, bagging can be parallelized, while generally boosting
requires sequential running.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000395477}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **6.7 Bagging for Scalability**
`</font>`{=html}

[As you know, several popular ML algorithms do not scale well with the
sample size. Support vector machines (SVMs) are a prime example. If you
attempt to fit an SVM on a million observations, it may take a while
until the algorithm converges. And even once it has converged, there is
no guarantee that the solution is a global optimum, or that it is not
overfit.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[One practical approach is to build a bagging algorithm, where the base
estimator belongs to a class that does not scale well with the sample
size, like SVM. When defining that base estimator, we will impose a
tight early stopping condition. For example, in sklearn\'s SVM
implementation, you could set a low value for the]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`max_iter` [parameter, say 1E5 iterations. The default value
is]{bgcolor="inherit"} `max_iter=-1` [, which tells the estimator to
continue performing iterations until errors fall below a tolerance
level. Alternatively, you could raise the tolerance level through the
parameter]{bgcolor="inherit"} `tol` [, which has a default
value]{bgcolor="inherit"} `tol=1E-3` [. Either of these two parameters
will force an early stop. You can stop other algorithms early with
equivalent parameters, like the number of levels in an RF
(]{bgcolor="inherit"} `max_depth` [), or the minimum weighted fraction
of the sum total of weights (of all the input samples) required to be at
a leaf node (]{bgcolor="inherit"} `min_weight_fraction_leaf`
[).]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Given that bagging algorithms can be parallelized, we are transforming
a large sequential task into many smaller ones that are run
simultaneously. Of course, the early stopping will increase the variance
of the outputs from the individual base estimators; however, that
increase can be more than offset by the variance reduction associated
with the bagging algorithm. You can control that reduction by adding
more independent base estimators. Used in this way, bagging will allow
you to achieve fast and robust estimates on extremely large
datasets.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000397874}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **Exercises** `</font>`{=html}

::: {height="1em"}
:::

1.  [Why is bagging based on random sampling with replacement? Would
    > > bagging still reduce a forecast\'s variance if sampling were
    > > without replacement?]{bgcolor="inherit"}

2.  [Suppose that your training set is based on highly overlap labels
    > > (i.e., with low uniqueness, as defined in Chapter
    > > 4).]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  Does this make bagging prone to overfitting, or just
        ineffective? Why?
    2.  Is out-of-bag accuracy generally reliable in financial
        applications? Why?

3.  [Build an ensemble of estimators, where the base estimator is a
    > > decision tree.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  How is this ensemble different from an RF?
    2.  Using sklearn, produce a bagging classifier that behaves like an
        RF. What parameters did you have to set up, and how?

4.  [Consider the relation between an RF, the number of trees it is
    > > composed of, and the number of features
    > > utilized:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  Could you envision a relation between the minimum number of
        trees needed in an RF and the number of features utilized?
    2.  Could the number of trees be too small for the number of
        features used?
    3.  Could the number of trees be too high for the number of
        observations available?

5.  [How is out-of-bag accuracy different from stratified k-fold (with
    > > shuffling) cross-validation accuracy?]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000400644}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000404229}

`<font size="5">`{=html} **CHAPTER 7** `</font>`{=html}\
\
`<font size="5">`{=html} **Cross-Validation in Finance**
`</font>`{=html}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000404473}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **7.1 Motivation** `</font>`{=html}

[The purpose of cross-validation (CV) is to determine the generalization
error of an ML algorithm, so as to prevent overfitting. CV is yet
another instance where standard ML techniques fail when applied to
financial problems. Overfitting will take place, and CV will not be able
to detect it. In fact, CV will contribute to overfitting through
hyper-parameter tuning. In this chapter we will learn why standard CV
fails in finance, and what can be done about it.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000405116}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **7.2 The Goal of Cross-Validation**
`</font>`{=html}

[One of the purposes of ML is to learn the general structure of the
data, so that we can produce predictions on future, unseen features.
When we test an ML algorithm on the same dataset as was used for
training, not surprisingly, we achieve spectacular results. When ML
algorithms are misused that way, they are no different from file
lossy-compression algorithms: They can summarize the data with extreme
fidelity, yet with zero forecasting power.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[CV splits observations drawn from an IID process into two sets:
the]{bgcolor="inherit"} *training* [set and the]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*testing* [set. Each observation in the complete dataset belongs to one,
and only one, set. This is done as to prevent leakage from one set into
the other, since that would defeat the purpose of testing on unseen
data. Further details can be found in the books and articles listed in
the references section.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[There are many alternative CV schemes, of which one of the most popular
is k-fold CV.]{bgcolor="inherit"} []{#text00001.html#filepos0000406409}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
7.1`</font>`{=html}](#text00001.html#filepos0000407324) [illustrates
the]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [train/test splits carried out by a k-fold
CV, where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [= 5. In this
scheme:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

1.  The dataset is partitioned into *k* subsets.
2.  For *i = 1,...,k*
    1.  The ML algorithm is trained on all subsets excluding *i.*
    2.  The fitted ML algorithm is tested on *i.*

![](Image00063.jpg){width="640" height="368"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000407324}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
7.1`</font>`{=html}**](#text00001.html#filepos0000406409) Train/test
splits in a 5-fold CV scheme

[The outcome from k-fold CV is a]{bgcolor="inherit"} *kx1* [array of
cross-validated performance metrics. For example, in a binary
classifier, the model is deemed to have learned something if the
cross-validated accuracy is over 1/2, since that is the accuracy we
would achieve by tossing a fair coin.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[In finance, CV is typically used in two settings: model development
(like hyper-parameter tuning) and backtesting. Backtesting is a complex
subject that we will discuss thoroughly in Chapters 10--16. In this
chapter, we will focus on CV for model development.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000408213}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **7.3 Why K-Fold CV Fails in Finance**
`</font>`{=html}

[By now you may have read quite a few papers in finance that present
k-fold CV evidence that an ML algorithm performs well. Unfortunately, it
is almost certain that those results are wrong. One reason k-fold CV
fails in finance is because observations cannot be assumed to be drawn
from an IID process. A second reason for CV\'s failure is that the
testing set is used multiple times in the process of developing a model,
leading to multiple testing and selection bias. We will revisit this
second cause of failure in Chapters 11--13. For the time being, let us
concern ourselves exclusively with the first cause of
failure.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Leakage takes place when the training set contains information that
also appears in the testing set. Consider a serially correlated
feature]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X* [that is associated with
labels]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Y* [that are formed on overlapping
data:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

-   Because of the serial correlation, *X
    ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* ≈ *X
    ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}+\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
    .
-   Because labels are derived from overlapping datapoints, *Y
    ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* ≈ *Y
    ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}+\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
    .

[By placing]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t + 1* [in
different sets, information is leaked. When a classifier is first
trained on (]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [), and
then it is asked to predict E\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}+\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[\|]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}+\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[\] based on an observed]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}+\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[, this classifier is more likely to achieve]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}+\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[= E\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}+\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[\|]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}+\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[\] even if]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X* [is an irrelevant
feature.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[If]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X* [is a predictive feature, leakage will
enhance the performance of an already valuable strategy. The problem is
leakage in the presence of irrelevant features, as this leads to false
discoveries. There are at least two ways to reduce the likelihood of
leakage:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

1.  Drop from the training set any observation *i* where *Y
    ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* is a
    function of information used to determine *Y
    ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* , and
    *j* belongs to the testing set.
    1.  For example, *Y
        ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* and
        *Y ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
        should not span overlapping periods (see Chapter 4 for a
        discussion of sample uniqueness).
2.  Avoid overfitting the classifier. In this way, even if some leakage
    occurs, the classifier will not be able to profit from it. Use:
    1.  Early stopping of the base estimators (see Chapter 6).
    2.  Bagging of classifiers, while controlling for oversampling on
        redundant examples, so that the individual classifiers are as
        diverse as possible.
        1.  Set `max_samples` to the average uniqueness.
        2.  Apply sequential bootstrap (Chapter 4).

[Consider the case where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [are formed
on overlapping information, where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *i* [belongs to
the training set and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *j* [belongs to the testing
set. Is this a case of informational leakage? Not necessarily, as long
as]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [are
independent. For leakage to take place, it must occur that
(]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [) ≈
(]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [), and it
does not suffice that]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[≈]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [or
even]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[≈]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000414858}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **7.4 A Solution: Purged K-Fold CV**
`</font>`{=html}

[One way to reduce leakage is to purge from the training set all
observations whose labels overlapped in time with those labels included
in the testing set. I call this process "purging." In addition, since
financial features often incorporate series that exhibit serial
correlation (like ARMA processes), we should eliminate from the training
set observations that immediately follow an observation in the testing
set. I call this process "embargo."]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **7.4.1 Purging the Training Set**
`</font>`{=html}

[Suppose a testing observation whose label]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [is decided
based on the information set Φ
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ . In order
to prevent the type of leakage described in the previous section, we
would like to purge from the training set any observation whose
label]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [is decided
based on the information set Φ
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ , such that
Φ ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ ∩Φ
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ =
∅]{bgcolor="inherit"} *.*

[In particular, we will determine that there is informational overlap
between two observations]{bgcolor="inherit"} *i*
[and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *j* [whenever]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [are
concurrent (see Chapter 4, Section 4.3), in the sense that both labels
are contingent on at least one common random draw. For example, consider
a label]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [that is a
function of observations in the closed range]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [∈
\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[\],]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[=]{bgcolor="inherit"} *f* [\[\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[\]\] (with some abuse of notation). For example, in the context of the
triple-barrier labeling method (Chapter 3), it means that the label is
the sign of the return spanning between price bars with
indices]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[, that is]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00065.jpg){width="84"
height="23"} [. A label]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[=]{bgcolor="inherit"} *f* [\[\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[\]\] overlaps with]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [if any of
the three sufficient conditions is met:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

1.  *t
    ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
    ≤ *t
    ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
    ≤ *t
    ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
2.  *t
    ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
    ≤ *t
    ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
    ≤ *t
    ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
3.  *t
    ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
    ≤ *t
    ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
    ≤ *t
    ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
    ≤ *t
    ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*

[Snippet 7.1 implements this purging of observations from the training
set. If the testing set is contiguous, in the sense that no training
observations occur between the first and last testing observation, then
purging can be accelerated: The object]{bgcolor="inherit"} `testTimes`
[can be a pandas series with a single item, spanning the entire testing
set.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 7.1 PURGING OBSERVATION IN THE TRAINING SET**

> ![](Image00068.jpg){width="883" height="344"}

[When leakage takes place, performance improves merely by
increasing]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [→]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [,
where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [is the number of bars. The reason is that
the larger the number of testing splits, the greater the number of
overlapping observations in the training set. In many cases, purging
suffices to prevent leakage: Performance will improve as we
increase]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [, because we allow the model to
recalibrate more often. But beyond a certain value]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*k* [\*, performance will not improve, indicating that the backtest is
not profiting from leaks.]{bgcolor="inherit"}
[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000421536}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
7.2`</font>`{=html}](#text00001.html#filepos0000421908) [plots one
partition of the k-fold CV. The test set is surrounded by two train
sets, generating two overlaps that must be purged to prevent
leakage.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00071.jpg){width="553" height="480"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000421908}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
7.2`</font>`{=html}**](#text00001.html#filepos0000421536) Purging
overlap in the training set

`<font size="4">`{=html} **7.4.2 Embargo** `</font>`{=html}

[For those cases where purging is not able to prevent all leakage, we
can impose an embargo on training observations]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*after* [every test set. The embargo does not need to affect training
observations prior to a test set, because training
labels]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[=]{bgcolor="inherit"} *f* [\[\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[\]\], where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[\<]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[(training ends before testing begins), contain information that was
available at the testing time]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[. In other words, we are only concerned with training
labels]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[=]{bgcolor="inherit"} *f* [\[\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[\]\] that take place immediately after the test,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[≤]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[≤]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[+]{bgcolor="inherit"} *h.* [We can implement this embargo
period]{bgcolor="inherit"} *h* [by setting]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[=]{bgcolor="inherit"} *f* [\[\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[+]{bgcolor="inherit"} *h* [\]\] before purging. A small
value]{bgcolor="inherit"} *h* [≈ .01]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [often
suffices to prevent all leakage, as can be confirmed by testing that
performance does not improve indefinitely by
increasing]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [→]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T*
[.]{bgcolor="inherit"} []{#text00001.html#filepos0000425092}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
7.3`</font>`{=html}](#text00001.html#filepos0000425442) [illustrates the
embargoing of train observations immediately after the testing set.
Snippet 7.2 implements the embargo logic.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00074.jpg){width="554" height="480"}

7.3`</font>`{=html}**](#text00001.html#filepos0000425092) Embargo of

`<font size="4">`{=html} **7.4.3 The Purged K-Fold Class**
`</font>`{=html}

[In the previous sections we have discussed how to produce
training/testing splits when labels overlap. That introduced the notion
of purging and embargoing, in the particular context of model
development. In general, we need to purge and embargo overlapping
training observations whenever we produce a train/test split, whether it
is for hyper-parameter fitting, backtesting, or performance evaluation.
Snippet 7.3 extends scikit-learn\'s]{bgcolor="inherit"} `KFold` [class
to account for the possibility of leakages of testing information into
the training set.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 7.3 CROSS-VALIDATION CLASS WHEN OBSERVATIONS OVERLAP**

> ![](Image00461.jpg){width="940" height="681"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000426899}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **7.5 Bugs in Sklearn\'s Cross-Validation**
`</font>`{=html}

[You would think that something as critical as cross-validation would be
perfectly implemented in one of the most popular ML libraries.
Unfortunately that is not the case, and this is one of the reasons you
must always read all the code you run, and a strong point in favor of
open source. One of the many upsides of open-source code is that you can
verify everything and adjust it to your needs. Snippet 7.4 addresses two
known sklearn bugs:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

1.  Scoring functions do not know `classes_` , as a consequence of
    sklearn\'s reliance on numpy arrays rather than pandas series:
    [`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/6231`</font>`{=html}](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/6231)
2.  `cross_val_score` will give different results because it passes
    weights to the fit method, but not to the `log_loss` method:
    [`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/9144`</font>`{=html}](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/9144)

> **SNIPPET 7.4 USING THE** ` PURGEDKFOLD` **CLASS**

> ![](Image00477.jpg){width="938" height="552"}

[Please understand that it may take a long time until a fix for these
bugs is agreed upon, implemented, tested, and released. Until then, you
should use]{bgcolor="inherit"} `cvScore` [in Snippet 7.4, and avoid
running the function]{bgcolor="inherit"} `cross_val_score`
[.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000428942}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **Exercises** `</font>`{=html}

::: {height="1em"}
:::

1.  [Why is shuffling a dataset before conducting k-fold CV generally a
    > > bad idea in finance? What is the purpose of shuffling? Why does
    > > shuffling defeat the purpose of k-fold CV in financial
    > > datasets?]{bgcolor="inherit"}

2.  [Take a pair of matrices (]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X*
    > > [,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y* [), representing observed features
    > > and labels. These could be one of the datasets derived from the
    > > exercises in Chapter 3.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  Derive the performance from a 10-fold CV of an RF classifier on
        (*X* , *y* ), without shuffling.
    2.  Derive the performance from a 10-fold CV of an RF on (*X* , *y*
        ), with shuffling.
    3.  Why are both results so different?
    4.  How does shuffling leak information?

3.  [Take the same pair of matrices (]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X*
    > > [,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y* [) you used in
    > > exercise 2.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  Derive the performance from a 10-fold purged CV of an RF on (*X*
        , *y* ), with 1% embargo.
    2.  Why is the performance lower?
    3.  Why is this result more realistic?

4.  [In this chapter we have focused on one reason why k-fold CV fails
    > > in financial applications, namely the fact that some information
    > > from the testing set leaks into the training set. Can you think
    > > of a second reason for CV\'s failure?]{bgcolor="inherit"}

5.  [Suppose you try one thousand configurations of the same investment
    > > strategy, and perform a CV on each of them. Some results are
    > > guaranteed to look good, just by sheer luck. If you only publish
    > > those positive results, and hide the rest, your audience will
    > > not be able to deduce that these results are false positives, a
    > > statistical fluke. This phenomenon is called "selection
    > > bias."]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  Can you imagine one procedure to prevent this?
    2.  What if we split the dataset in three sets: training,
        validation, and testing? The validation set is used to evaluate
        the trained parameters, and the testing is run only on the one
        configuration chosen in the validation phase. In what case does
        this procedure still fail?
    3.  What is the key to avoiding selection bias?

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000432718}

    .
