[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000434781}

`<font size="5">`{=html} **CHAPTER 8** `</font>`{=html}\
\
`<font size="5">`{=html} **Feature Importance** `</font>`{=html}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000435016}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **8.1 Motivation** `</font>`{=html}

[One of the most pervasive mistakes in financial research is to take
some data, run it through an ML algorithm, backtest the predictions, and
repeat the sequence until a nice-looking backtest shows up. Academic
journals are filled with such pseudo-discoveries, and even large hedge
funds constantly fall into this trap. It does not matter if the backtest
is a walk-forward out-of-sample. The fact that we are repeating a test
over and over on the same data will likely lead to a false discovery.
This methodological error is so notorious among statisticians that they
consider it scientific fraud, and the American Statistical Association
warns against it in its ethical guidelines (American Statistical
Association \[2016\], Discussion \#4). It typically takes about 20 such
iterations to discover a (false) investment strategy subject to the
standard significance level (false positive rate) of 5%. In this chapter
we will explore why such an approach is a waste of time and money, and
how feature importance offers an alternative.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000436227}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **8.2 The Importance of Feature Importance**
`</font>`{=html}

[A striking facet of the financial industry is that so many very
seasoned portfolio managers (including many with a quantitative
background) do not realize how easy it is to overfit a backtest. How to
backtest properly is not the subject of this chapter; we will address
that extremely important topic in Chapters 11--15. The goal of this
chapter is to explain one of the analyses that must be
performed]{bgcolor="inherit"} *before* [any backtest is carried
out.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Suppose that you are given a pair of matrices (]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X*
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y* [), that respectively contain features and
labels for a particular financial instrument. We can fit a classifier on
(]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X* [,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y* [) and evaluate the
generalization error through a purged k-fold cross-validation (CV), as
we saw in Chapter 7. Suppose that we achieve good performance. The next
natural question is to try to understand what features contributed to
that performance. Maybe we could add some features that strengthen the
signal responsible for the classifier\'s predictive power. Maybe we
could eliminate some of the features that are only adding noise to the
system. Notably, understanding feature importance opens up the
proverbial black box. We can gain insight into the patterns identified
by the classifier if we understand what source of information is
indispensable to it. This is one of the reasons why the black box mantra
is somewhat overplayed by the ML skeptics. Yes, the algorithm has
learned without us directing the process (that is the whole point of
ML!) in a black box, but that does not mean that we cannot (or should
not) take a look at what the algorithm has found. Hunters do not blindly
eat everything their smart dogs retrieve for them, do
they?]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Once we have found what features are important, we can learn more by
conducting a number of experiments. Are these features important all the
time, or only in some specific environments? What triggers a change in
importance over time? Can those regime switches be predicted? Are those
important features also relevant to other related financial instruments?
Are they relevant to other asset classes? What are the most relevant
features across all financial instruments? What is the subset of
features with the highest rank correlation across the entire investment
universe? This is a much better way of researching strategies than the
foolish backtest cycle. Let me state this maxim as one of the most
critical lessons I hope you learn from this book:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> `<font size="4">`{=html} **Snippet 8.1 Marcos' First Law of
> Backtesting---Ignore at your own peril** `</font>`{=html}

> > ["Backtesting is not a research tool. Feature importance
> > is."]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> > > `<font size="2">`{=html} [Marcos López de
> > > Prado]{bgcolor="inherit"} `</font>`{=html}
> > > `<font size="2">`{=html} *Advances in Financial Machine Learning*
> > > `</font>`{=html} `<font size="2">`{=html}
> > > [(2018)]{bgcolor="inherit"} `</font>`{=html}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000439835}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **8.3 Feature Importance with Substitution
Effects** `</font>`{=html}

[I find it useful to distinguish between feature importance methods
based on whether they are impacted by substitution effects. In this
context, a substitution effect takes place when the estimated importance
of one feature is reduced by the presence of other related features.
Substitution effects are the ML analogue of what the statistics and
econometrics literature calls "multi-collinearity." One way to address
linear substitution effects is to apply PCA on the raw features, and
then perform the feature importance analysis on the orthogonal features.
See Belsley et al. \[1980\], Goldberger \[1991, pp. 245--253\], and Hill
et al. \[2001\] for further details.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **8.3.1 Mean Decrease Impurity**
`</font>`{=html}

[Mean decrease impurity (MDI) is a fast, explanatory-importance
(in-sample, IS) method specific to tree-based classifiers, like RF. At
each node of each decision tree, the selected feature splits the subset
it received in such a way that impurity is decreased. Therefore, we can
derive for each decision tree how much of the overall impurity decrease
can be assigned to each feature. And given that we have a forest of
trees, we can average those values across all estimators and rank the
features accordingly. See Louppe et al. \[2013\] for a detailed
description. There are some important considerations you must keep in
mind when working with MDI:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

1.  Masking effects take place when some features are systematically
    ignored by tree-based classifiers in favor of others. In order to
    avoid them, set `max_features=int(1)` when using sklearn\'s RF
    class. In this way, only one random feature is considered per level.
    1.  Every feature is given a chance (at some random levels of some
        random trees) to reduce impurity.
    2.  Make sure that features with zero importance are not averaged,
        since the only reason for a 0 is that the feature was not
        randomly chosen. Replace those values with `np.nan` .
2.  The procedure is obviously IS. Every feature will have some
    importance, even if they have no predictive power whatsoever.
3.  MDI cannot be generalized to other non-tree based classifiers.
4.  By construction, MDI has the nice property that feature importances
    add up to 1, and every feature importance is bounded between 0
    and 1.
5.  The method does not address substitution effects in the presence of
    correlated features. MDI dilutes the importance of substitute
    features, because of their interchangeability: The importance of two
    identical features will be halved, as they are randomly chosen with
    equal probability.
6.  Strobl et al. \[2007\] show experimentally that MDI is biased
    towards some predictor variables. White and Liu \[1994\] argue that,
    in case of single decision trees, this bias is due to an unfair
    advantage given by popular impurity functions toward predictors with
    a large number of categories.

[Sklearn\'s]{bgcolor="inherit"} `RandomForest` [class implements MDI as
the default feature importance score. This choice is likely motivated by
the ability to compute MDI on the fly, with minimum computational
cost.^[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000443800}\ [[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ `<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}1`</font>`{=html}\ `</font>`{=html}](#text00001.html#filepos0000475986)]{.small}^
Snippet 8.2 illustrates an implementation of MDI, incorporating the
considerations listed earlier.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 8.2 MDI FEATURE IMPORTANCE**

> ![](Image00087.jpg){width="962" height="238"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **8.3.2 Mean Decrease Accuracy**
`</font>`{=html}

[Mean decrease accuracy (MDA) is a slow, predictive-importance
(out-of-sample, OOS) method. First, it fits a classifier; second, it
derives its performance OOS according to some performance score
(accuracy, negative log-loss, etc.); third, it permutates each column of
the features matrix (]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X* [), one column at a time,
deriving the performance OOS after each column\'s permutation. The
importance of a feature is a function of the loss in performance caused
by its column\'s permutation. Some relevant considerations
include:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

1.  This method can be applied to any classifier, not only tree-based
    classifiers.
2.  MDA is not limited to accuracy as the sole performance score. For
    example, in the context of meta-labeling applications, we may prefer
    to score a classifier with F1 rather than accuracy (see Chapter 14,
    Section 14.8 for an explanation). That is one reason a better
    descriptive name would have been "permutation importance." When the
    scoring function does not correspond to a metric space, MDA results
    should be used as a ranking.
3.  Like MDI, the procedure is also susceptible to substitution effects
    in the presence of correlated features. Given two identical
    features, MDA always considers one to be redundant to the other.
    Unfortunately, MDA will make both features appear to be outright
    irrelevant, even if they are critical.
4.  Unlike MDI, it is possible that MDA concludes that all features are
    unimportant. That is because MDA is based on OOS performance.
5.  The CV must be purged and embargoed, for the reasons explained in
    Chapter 7.

[Snippet 8.3 implements MDA feature importance with sample weights, with
purged k-fold CV, and with scoring by negative log-loss or accuracy. It
measures MDA importance as a function of the improvement (from
permutating to not permutating the feature), relative to the maximum
possible score (negative log-loss of 0, or accuracy of 1). Note that, in
some cases, the improvement may be negative, meaning that the feature is
actually detrimental to the forecasting power of the ML
algorithm.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 8.3 MDA FEATURE IMPORTANCE**

> ![](Image00504.jpg){width="934" height="896"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000447153}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **8.4 Feature Importance without Substitution
Effects** `</font>`{=html}

[Substitution effects can lead us to discard important features that
happen to be redundant. This is not generally a problem in the context
of prediction, but it could lead us to wrong conclusions when we are
trying to understand, improve, or simplify a model. For this reason, the
following single feature importance method can be a good complement to
MDI and MDA.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **8.4.1 Single Feature Importance**
`</font>`{=html}

[Single feature importance (SFI) is a cross-section
predictive-importance (out-of-sample) method. It computes the OOS
performance score of each feature in isolation. A few
considerations:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

1.  This method can be applied to any classifier, not only tree-based
    classifiers.
2.  SFI is not limited to accuracy as the sole performance score.
3.  Unlike MDI and MDA, no substitution effects take place, since only
    one feature is taken into consideration at a time.
4.  Like MDA, it can conclude that all features are unimportant, because
    performance is evaluated via OOS CV.

[The main limitation of SFI is that a classifier with two features can
perform better than the bagging of two single-feature classifiers. For
example, (1) feature B may be useful only in combination with feature A;
or (2) feature B may be useful in explaining the splits from feature A,
even if feature B alone is inaccurate. In other words, joint effects and
hierarchical importance are lost in SFI. One alternative would be to
compute the OOS performance score from subsets of features, but that
calculation will become intractable as more features are considered.
Snippet 8.4 demonstrates one possible implementation of the SFI method.
A discussion of the function]{bgcolor="inherit"} `cvScore` [can be found
in Chapter 7.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 8.4 IMPLEMENTATION OF SFI**

> ![](Image00092.jpg){width="955" height="208"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **8.4.2 Orthogonal Features** `</font>`{=html}

[As argued in Section 8.3, substitution effects dilute the importance of
features measured by MDI, and significantly underestimate the importance
of features measured by MDA. A partial solution is to orthogonalize the
features before applying MDI and MDA. An orthogonalization procedure
such as principal components analysis (PCA) does not prevent all
substitution effects, but at least it should alleviate the impact of
linear substitution effects.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Consider a matrix {]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *n*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[} of stationary features, with observations]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [=
1, ...,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [and variables]{bgcolor="inherit"} *n*
[= 1, ...,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [. First, we compute the standardized
features matrix]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Z* [, such that]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*Z
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *n*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[= σ ^[`<font size="2">`{=html}−\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *n*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
(]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *n*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[− μ ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *n*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ ),
where μ ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *n*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ is
the mean of {]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *n*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[}
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}=\ 1,\ ...,`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *T*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
and σ ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *n*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ is the
standard deviation of {]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *n*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[}
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}=\ 1,\ ...,`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *T*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
. Second, we compute the eigenvalues Λ and
eigenvectors]{bgcolor="inherit"} *W* [such that]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Z*
[\']{bgcolor="inherit"} *ZW* [=]{bgcolor="inherit"} *W* [Λ, where Λ is
an]{bgcolor="inherit"} *NxN* [diagonal matrix with main entries sorted
in descending order, and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *W* [is
an]{bgcolor="inherit"} *NxN* [orthonormal matrix. Third, we derive the
orthogonal features as]{bgcolor="inherit"} *P* [=]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*ZW* [. We can verify the orthogonality of the features by noting
that]{bgcolor="inherit"} *P* [\']{bgcolor="inherit"} *P*
[=]{bgcolor="inherit"} *W* [\']{bgcolor="inherit"} *Z*
[\']{bgcolor="inherit"} *ZW* [=]{bgcolor="inherit"} *W*
[\']{bgcolor="inherit"} *W* [Λ]{bgcolor="inherit"} *W*
[\']{bgcolor="inherit"} *W* [= Λ.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[The diagonalization is done on]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Z* [rather
than]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X* [, for two reasons: (1) centering the data
ensures that the first principal component is correctly oriented in the
main direction of the observations. It is equivalent to adding an
intercept in a linear regression; (2) re-scaling the data makes PCA
focus on explaining correlations rather than variances. Without
re-scaling, the first principal components would be dominated by the
columns of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X* [with highest variance, and we would
not learn much about the structure or relationship between the
variables.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Snippet 8.5 computes the smallest number of orthogonal features that
explain at least 95% of the variance of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Z*
[.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 8.5 COMPUTATION OF ORTHOGONAL FEATURES**

> ![](Image00541.jpg){width="935" height="589"}

[Besides addressing substitution effects, working with orthogonal
features provides two additional benefits: (1) orthogonalization can
also be used to reduce the dimensionality of the features
matrix]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X* [, by dropping features associated with
small eigenvalues. This usually speeds up the convergence of ML
algorithms; (2) the analysis is conducted on features designed to
explain the structure of the data.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Let me stress this latter point. An ubiquitous concern throughout the
book is the risk of overfitting. ML algorithms will always find a
pattern, even if that pattern is a statistical fluke. You should always
be skeptical about the purportedly important features identified by any
method, including MDI, MDA, and SFI. Now, suppose that you derive
orthogonal features using PCA. Your PCA analysis has determined that
some features are more "principal" than others, without any knowledge of
the labels (unsupervised learning). That is, PCA has ranked features
without any possible overfitting in a classification sense. When your
MDI, MDA, or SFI analysis selects as most important (using label
information) the same features that PCA chose as principal (ignoring
label information), this constitutes confirmatory evidence that the
pattern identified by the ML algorithm is not entirely overfit. If the
features were entirely random, the PCA ranking would have no
correspondance with the feature importance ranking.]{bgcolor="inherit"}
[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000455929}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
8.1`</font>`{=html}](#text00001.html#filepos0000456463) [displays the
scatter plot of eigenvalues associated with an eigenvector (x-axis)
paired with MDI of the feature associated with an engenvector (y-axis).
The Pearson correlation is 0.8491 (p-value below 1E-150), evidencing
that PCA identified informative features and ranked them correctly
without overfitting.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00100.jpg){width="640" height="471"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000456463}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
8.1`</font>`{=html}**](#text00001.html#filepos0000455929) Scatter plot
of eigenvalues (x-axis) and MDI levels (y-axis) in log-log scale

[I find it useful to compute the weighted Kendall\'s tau between the
feature importances and their associated eigenvalues (or equivalently,
their inverse PCA rank). The closer this value is to 1, the stronger is
the consistency between PCA ranking and feature importance ranking. One
argument for preferring a weighted Kendall\'s tau over the standard
Kendall is that we want to prioritize rank concordance among the most
importance features. We do not care so much about rank concordance among
irrelevant (likely noisy) features. The hyperbolic-weighted Kendall\'s
tau for the sample in]{bgcolor="inherit"}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
8.1`</font>`{=html}](#text00001.html#filepos0000456463) [is
0.8206.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Snippet 8.6 shows how to compute this correlation using Scipy. In this
example, sorting the features in descending importance gives us a PCA
rank sequence very close to an ascending list. Because
the]{bgcolor="inherit"} `weightedtau` [function gives higher weight to
higher values, we compute the correlation on the inverse PCA
ranking,]{bgcolor="inherit"} `pcRank**-1` [. The resulting weighted
Kendall\'s tau is relatively high, at 0.8133.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 8.6 COMPUTATION OF WEIGHTED KENDALL\'S TAU BETWEEN FEATURE
> IMPORTANCE AND INVERSE PCA RANKING**

> ![](Image00586.jpg){width="733" height="128"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000458286}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **8.5 Parallelized vs. Stacked Feature
Importance** `</font>`{=html}

[There are at least two research approaches to feature importance.
First, for each security]{bgcolor="inherit"} *i* [in an investment
universe]{bgcolor="inherit"} *i* [= 1, ...,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *I* [,
we form a dataset (]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [), and
derive the feature importance in parallel. For example, let us denote λ
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *k*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
the importance of feature]{bgcolor="inherit"} *j* [on
instrument]{bgcolor="inherit"} *i* [according to
criterion]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k.* [Then we can aggregate all results
across the entire universe to derive a combined Λ
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *k*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
importance of feature]{bgcolor="inherit"} *j* [according to
criterion]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k.* [Features that are important across a
wide variety of instruments are more likely to be associated with an
underlying phenomenon, particularly when these feature importances
exhibit high rank correlation across the criteria. It may be worth
studying in-depth the theoretical mechanism that makes these features
predictive. The main advantage of this approach is that it is
computationally fast, as it can be parallelized. A disadvantage is that,
due to substitution effects, important features may swap their ranks
across instruments, increasing the variance of the estimated λ
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *k*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
. This disadvantage becomes relatively minor if we average λ
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *k*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
across instruments for a sufficiently large investment
universe.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[A second alternative is what I call "features stacking." It consists in
stacking all datasets]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00609.jpg){width="117" height="24"} [into a single combined
dataset (]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X* [,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y* [),
where]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00109.jpg){width="17" height="21"}
[is a transformed instance of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [(e.g.,
standardized on a rolling trailing window). The purpose of this
transformation is to ensure some distributional
homogeneity,]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00335.jpg){width="55"
height="21"} [. Under this approach, the classifier must learn what
features are more important across all instruments simultaneously, as if
the entire investment universe were in fact a single instrument.
Features stacking presents some advantages: (1) The classifier will be
fit on a much larger dataset than the one used with the parallelized
(first) approach; (2) the importance is derived directly, and no
weighting scheme is required for combining the results; (3) conclusions
are more general and less biased by outliers or overfitting; and (4)
because importance scores are not averaged across instruments,
substitution effects do not cause the dampening of those
scores.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[I usually prefer features stacking, not only for features importance
but whenever a classifier can be fit on a set of instruments, including
for the purpose of model prediction. That reduces the likelihood of
overfitting an estimator to a particular instrument or small dataset.
The main disadvantage of stacking is that it may consume a lot of memory
and resources, however that is where a sound knowledge of HPC techniques
will come in handy (Chapters 20--22).]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000462771}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **8.6 Experiments with Synthetic Data**
`</font>`{=html}

[In this section, we are going to test how these feature importance
methods respond to synthetic data. We are going to generate a dataset
(]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X* [,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y* [) composed on
three kinds of features:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

1.  Informative: These are features that are used to determine the
    label.
2.  Redundant: These are random linear combinations of the informative
    features. They will cause substitution effects.
3.  Noise: These are features that have no bearing on determining the
    observation\'s label.

[Snippet 8.7 shows how we can generate a synthetic dataset of 40
features where 10 are informative, 10 are redundant, and 20 are noise,
on 10,000 observations. For details on how sklearn generates synthetic
datasets, visit:]{bgcolor="inherit"}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.datasets.make\_classification.html`</font>`{=html}](http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.datasets.make_classification.html)
[.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 8.7 CREATING A SYNTHETIC DATASET**

> ![](Image00338.jpg){width="964" height="454"}

[Given that we know for certain what feature belongs to each class, we
can evaluate whether these three feature importance methods perform as
designed. Now we need a function that can carry out each analysis on the
same dataset. Snippet 8.8 accomplishes that, using bagged decision trees
as default classifier (Chapter 6).]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 8.8 CALLING FEATURE IMPORTANCE FOR ANY METHOD**

> ![](Image00342.jpg){width="934" height="713"}

[Finally, we need a main function to call all components, from data
generation to feature importance analysis to collection and processing
of output. These tasks are performed by Snippet 8.9.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 8.9 CALLING ALL COMPONENTS**

> ![](Image00344.jpg){width="894" height="655"}

[For the aesthetically inclined, Snippet 8.10 provides a nice layout for
plotting feature importances.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 8.10 FEATURE IMPORTANCE PLOTTING FUNCTION**

> ![](Image00349.jpg){width="952" height="479"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000466053}

[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
8.2`</font>`{=html}](#text00001.html#filepos0000467068) [shows results
for MDI. For each feature, the horizontal bar indicates the mean MDI
value across all the decision trees, and the horizontal line is the
standard deviation of that mean. Since MDI importances add up to 1, if
all features were equally important, each importance would have a value
of 1/40. The vertical dotted line marks that 1/40 threshold, separating
features whose importance exceeds what would be expected from
undistinguishable features. As you can see, MDI does a very good job in
terms of placing all informative and redundant features above the red
dotted line, with the exception of R\_5, which did not make the cut by a
small margin. Substitution effects cause some informative or redundant
features to rank better than others, which was
expected.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00352.jpg){width="580" height="480"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000467068}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
8.2`</font>`{=html}**](#text00001.html#filepos0000466053) MDI feature
importance computed on a synthetic dataset

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000467240}

[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
8.3`</font>`{=html}](#text00001.html#filepos0000468012) [shows that MDA
also did a good job. Results are consistent with those from MDI\'s in
the sense that all the informed and redundant features rank better than
the noise feature, with the exception of R\_6, likely due to a
substitution effect. One not so positive aspect of MDA is that the
standard deviation of the means are somewhat higher, although that could
be addressed by increasing the number of partitions in the purged k-fold
CV, from, say, 10 to 100 (at the cost of 10 × the computation time
without parallelization).]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00355.jpg){width="593" height="480"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000468012}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
8.3`</font>`{=html}**](#text00001.html#filepos0000467240) MDA feature
importance computed on a synthetic dataset

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000468184}

[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
8.4`</font>`{=html}](#text00001.html#filepos0000468942) [shows that SFI
also does a decent job; however, a few important features rank worse
than noise (I\_6, I\_2, I\_9, I\_1, I\_3, R\_5), likely due to joint
effects.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[The labels are a function of a combination of features, and trying to
forecast them independently misses the joint effects. Still, SFI is
useful as a complement to MDI and MDA, precisely because both types of
analyses are affected by different kinds of
problems.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00360.jpg){width="572" height="480"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000468942}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
8.4`</font>`{=html}**](#text00001.html#filepos0000468184) SFI feature
importance computed on a synthetic dataset

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000469145}

2.  [From exercise 1, generate a new dataset]{bgcolor="inherit"}

        )}

        , each of 1E5 observations.

        ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* ,

        )}

        .

        (*X* , *y* ).

        reliable?

    > > allow for masking effects. That means, do not

[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}http://blog.datadive.net/selecting-good-features-part-iii-random-forests/`</font>`{=html}](http://blog.datadive.net/selecting-good-features-part-iii-random-forests/)

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000476302}

`<font size="5">`{=html} **CHAPTER 9** `</font>`{=html}\
\
`<font size="5">`{=html} **Hyper-Parameter Tuning with
Cross-Validation** `</font>`{=html}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000476563}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **9.1 Motivation** `</font>`{=html}

[Hyper-parameter tuning is an essential step in fitting an ML algorithm.
When this is not done properly, the algorithm is likely to overfit, and
live performance will disappoint. The ML literature places special
attention on cross-validating any tuned hyper-parameter. As we have seen
in Chapter 7, cross-validation (CV) in finance is an especially
difficult problem, where solutions from other fields are likely to fail.
In this chapter we will discuss how to tune hyper-parameters using the
purged k-fold CV method. The references section lists studies that
propose alternative methods that may be useful in specific
problems.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000477372}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **9.2 Grid Search Cross-Validation**
`</font>`{=html}

[Grid search cross-validation conducts an exhaustive search for the
combination of parameters that maximizes the CV performance, according
to some user-defined score function. When we do not know much about the
underlying structure of the data, this is a reasonable first approach.
Scikit-learn has implemented this logic in the
function]{bgcolor="inherit"} `GridSearchCV` [, which accepts a CV
generator as an argument. For the reasons explained in Chapter 7, we
need to pass our]{bgcolor="inherit"} `PurgedKFold` [class (Snippet 7.3)
in order to prevent that]{bgcolor="inherit"} `GridSearchCV` [overfits
the ML estimator to leaked information.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 9.1 GRID SEARCH WITH PURGED K-FOLD CROSS-VALIDATION**

> ![](Image00387.jpg){width="838" height="452"}

[Snippet 9.1 lists function]{bgcolor="inherit"} `clfHyperFit` [, which
implements a purged]{bgcolor="inherit"} `GridSearchCV` [. The
argument]{bgcolor="inherit"} `fit_params` [can be used to
pass]{bgcolor="inherit"} `sample_weight` [, and]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`param_grid` [contains the values that will be combined into a grid. In
addition, this function allows for the bagging of the tuned estimator.
Bagging an estimator is generally a good idea for the reasons explained
in Chapter 6, and the above function incorporates logic to that
purpose.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[I advise you to use]{bgcolor="inherit"} `scoring=‘f1’` [in the context
of meta-labeling applications, for the following reason. Suppose a
sample with a very large number of negative (i.e., label '0') cases. A
classifier that predicts all cases to be negative will achieve
high]{bgcolor="inherit"} `‘accuracy’` [or]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`‘neg_log_loss’` [, even though it has not learned from the features how
to discriminate between cases. In fact, such a model achieves zero
recall and undefined precision (see Chapter 3, Section 3.7).
The]{bgcolor="inherit"} `‘f1’` [score corrects for that performance
inflation by scoring the classifier in terms of precision and recall
(see Chapter 14, Section 14.8).]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[For other (non-meta-labeling) applications, it is fine to
use]{bgcolor="inherit"} `‘accuracy’` [or]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`‘neg_log_loss’` [, because we are equally interested in predicting all
cases. Note that a relabeling of cases has no impact
on]{bgcolor="inherit"} `‘accuracy’` [or]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`‘neg_log_loss’` [, however it will have an impact
on]{bgcolor="inherit"} `‘f1’` [.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[This example introduces nicely one limitation of
sklearn\'s]{bgcolor="inherit"} `Pipelines` [: Their fit method does not
expect a]{bgcolor="inherit"} `sample_weight` [argument. Instead, it
expects a]{bgcolor="inherit"} `fit_params` [keyworded argument. That is
a bug that has been reported in GitHub; however, it may take some time
to fix it, as it involves rewriting and testing much functionality.
Until then, feel free to use the workaround in Snippet 9.2. It creates a
new class, called]{bgcolor="inherit"} `MyPipeline` [, which inherits all
methods from sklearn\'s]{bgcolor="inherit"} `Pipeline` [. It overwrites
the inherited]{bgcolor="inherit"} `fit` [method with a new one that
handles the argument]{bgcolor="inherit"} `sample_weight` [, after which
it redirects to the parent class.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 9.2 AN ENHANCED PIPELINE CLASS**

> ![](Image00389.jpg){width="872" height="128"}

[If you are not familiar with this technique for expanding classes, you
may want to read this introductory Stackoverflow
post:]{bgcolor="inherit"}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}http://stackoverflow.com/questions/
576169/understanding-python-super-with-init-methods`</font>`{=html}](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/576169/understanding-python-super-with-init-methods)
[.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000482285}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **9.3 Randomized Search Cross-Validation**
`</font>`{=html}

[For ML algorithms with a large number of parameters, a grid search
cross-validation (CV) becomes computationally intractable. In this case,
an alternative with good statistical properties is to sample each
parameter from a distribution (Begstra et al. \[2011, 2012\]). This has
two benefits: First, we can control for the number of combinations we
will search for, regardless of the dimensionality of the problem (the
equivalent to a computational budget). Second, having parameters that
are relatively irrelevant performance-wise will not substantially
increase our search time, as would be the case with grid search
CV.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Rather than writing a new function to work with]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`RandomizedSearchCV` [, let us expand Snippet 9.1 to incorporate an
option to this purpose. A possible implementation is Snippet
9.3.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 9.3 RANDOMIZED SEARCH WITH PURGED K-FOLD CV**

> ![](Image00391.jpg){width="859" height="590"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **9.3.1 Log-Uniform Distribution**
`</font>`{=html}

[It is common for some ML algorithms to accept non-negative
hyper-parameters only. That is the case of some very popular parameters,
such as]{bgcolor="inherit"} `C` [in the SVC classifier
and]{bgcolor="inherit"} `gamma` [in the RBF
kernel.^[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000484050}\ [[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ `<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}1`</font>`{=html}\ `</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000501318)]{.small}^
We could draw random numbers from a uniform distribution bounded between
0 and some large value, say 100. That would mean that 99% of the values
would be expected to be greater than 1. That is not necessarily the most
effective way of exploring the feasibility region of parameters whose
functions do not respond linearly. For example, an SVC can be as
responsive to an increase in]{bgcolor="inherit"} `C` [from 0.01 to 1 as
to an increase in]{bgcolor="inherit"} `C` [from 1 to
100.^[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000484671}\ [[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ `<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}2`</font>`{=html}\ `</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000501610)]{.small}^
So sampling]{bgcolor="inherit"} `C` [from a]{bgcolor="inherit"} *U*
[\[0, 100\] (uniform) distribution will be inefficient. In those
instances, it seems more effective to draw values from a distribution
where the logarithm of those draws will be distributed uniformly. I call
that a "log-uniform distribution," and since I could not find it in the
literature, I must define it properly.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[A random variable]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x* [follows a log-uniform
distribution between]{bgcolor="inherit"} *a* [\> 0
and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *b* [\>]{bgcolor="inherit"} *a* [if and only if
log \[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x* [\] ∼]{bgcolor="inherit"} *U*
[\[log \[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *a* [\], log \[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *b*
[\]\]. This distribution has a CDF:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00394.jpg){width="324" height="92"}

[From this, we derive a PDF:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00569.jpg){width="284" height="94"}

![](Image00401.jpg){width="640" height="447"}

[]{#text00001.html#filepos0000486173}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
9.1`</font>`{=html}**](#text00001.html#filepos0000487213) Result from
testing the `logUniform_gen` class

[Note that the CDF is invariant to the base of the logarithm,
since]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00403.jpg){width="121" height="58"}
[for any base]{bgcolor="inherit"} *c* [, thus the random variable is not
a function of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *c.* [Snippet 9.4 implements (and
tests) in]{bgcolor="inherit"} `scipy.stats` [a random variable where
\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *a* [,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *b* [\] =
\[1]{bgcolor="inherit"} *E* [− 3, 1]{bgcolor="inherit"} *E* [3\], hence
log \[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x* [\] ∼]{bgcolor="inherit"} *U*
[\[log \[1]{bgcolor="inherit"} *E* [− 3\], log \[1]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*E* [3\]\].]{bgcolor="inherit"} []{#text00001.html#filepos0000487213}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
9.1`</font>`{=html}](#text00001.html#filepos0000486173) [illustrates the
uniformity of the samples in log-scale.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 9.4 THE** ` LOGUNIFORM_GEN` **CLASS**

> ![](Image00406.jpg){width="935" height="465"}

[]{#text00002.html}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000487673}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **9.4 Scoring and Hyper-parameter Tuning**
`</font>`{=html}

[Snippets 9.1 and 9.3 set]{bgcolor="inherit"} `scoring=‘f1’` [for
meta-labeling applications. For other applications, they
set]{bgcolor="inherit"} `scoring=‘neg_log_loss’` [rather than the
standard]{bgcolor="inherit"} `scoring=‘accuracy’` [. Although accuracy
has a more intuitive interpretation, I suggest that you
use]{bgcolor="inherit"} `neg_log_loss` [when you are tuning
hyper-parameters for an investment strategy. Let me explain my
reasoning.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Suppose that your ML investment strategy predicts that you should buy a
security, with high probability. You will enter a large long position,
as a function of the strategy\'s confidence. If the prediction was
erroneous, and the market sells off instead, you will lose a lot of
money. And yet, accuracy accounts equally for an erroneous buy
prediction with high probability and for an erroneous buy prediction
with low probability. Moreover, accuracy can offset a miss with high
probability with a hit with low probability.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Investment strategies profit from predicting the right label with high
confidence. Gains from good predictions with low confidence will not
suffice to offset the losses from bad predictions with high confidence.
For this reason, accuracy does not provide a realistic scoring of the
classifier\'s performance. Conversely, log
loss^[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000489345}\ [[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ `<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}3`</font>`{=html}\ `</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000501946)]{.small}^
(aka cross-entropy loss) computes the log-likelihood of the classifier
given the true label, which takes predictions' probabilities into
account. Log loss can be estimated as follows:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00409.jpg){width="456" height="57"}

[where]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

-   *p
    ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *n*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *k*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
    is the probability associated with prediction *n* of label *k.*
-   *Y* is a 1-of-*K* binary indicator matrix, such that *y
    ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *n*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *k*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
    = 1 when observation *n* was assigned label *k* out of *K* possible
    labels, and 0 otherwise.

[Suppose that a classifier predicts two 1s, where the true labels are 1
and 0. The first prediction is a hit and the second prediction is a
miss, thus accuracy is 50%.]{bgcolor="inherit"}
[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000490708}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
9.2`</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000491183) [plots the
cross-entropy loss when these predictions come from probabilities
ranging \[0.5, 0.9\]. One can observe that on the right side of the
figure, log loss is large due to misses with high probability, even
though the accuracy is 50% in all cases.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00411.jpg){width="800" height="587"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000491183}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
9.2`</font>`{=html}**](#text00002.html#filepos0000490708) Log loss as a
function of predicted probabilities of hit and miss

[There is a second reason to prefer cross-entropy loss over accuracy. CV
scores a classifier by applying sample weights (see Chapter 7, Section
7.5). As you may recall from Chapter 4, observation weights were
determined as a function of the observation\'s absolute return. The
implication is that sample weighted cross-entropy loss estimates the
classifier\'s performance in terms of variables involved in a PnL
(mark-to-market profit and losses) calculation: It uses the correct
label for the side, probability for the position size, and sample weight
for the observation\'s return/outcome. That is the right ML performance
metric for hyper-parameter tuning of financial applications, not
accuracy.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[When we use log loss as a scoring statistic, we often prefer to change
its sign, hence referring to "neg log loss]{bgcolor="inherit"} *.* ["
The reason for this change is cosmetic, driven by intuition: A high neg
log loss value is preferred to a low neg log loss value, just as with
accuracy. Keep in mind this sklearn bug when you use]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`neg_log_loss` [:]{bgcolor="inherit"}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/9144`</font>`{=html}](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/9144)
[. To circumvent this bug, you should use the]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`cvScore` [function presented in Chapter 7.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000492944}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **Exercises** `</font>`{=html}

::: {height="1em"}
:::

1.  [Using the function]{bgcolor="inherit"} `getTestData` [from Chapter
    > > 8, form a synthetic dataset of 10,000 observations with 10
    > > features, where 5 are informative and 5 are
    > > noise.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  Use `GridSearchCV` on 10-fold CV to find the `C` , `gamma`
        optimal hyper-parameters on a SVC with RBF kernel, where
        `param_grid = {'C':[1E-2,1E-1,1,10,100],'gamma':[1E-2,1E-1,1,10,100]}`
        and the scoring function is `neg_log_loss` .
    2.  How many nodes are there in the grid?
    3.  How many fits did it take to find the optimal solution?
    4.  How long did it take to find this solution?
    5.  How can you access the optimal result?
    6.  What is the CV score of the optimal parameter combination?
    7.  How can you pass sample weights to the SVC?

2.  [Using the same dataset from exercise 1,]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  Use `RandomizedSearchCV` on 10-fold CV to find the `C` , `gamma`
        optimal hyper-parameters on an SVC with RBF kernel, where
        `param_distributions = {‘C’:logUniform(a = 1E-2,b = 1E2),‘gamma’:logUniform(a = 1E-2,b = 1E2)},n_iter = 25`
        and `neg_log_loss` is the scoring function.
    2.  How long did it take to find this solution?
    3.  Is the optimal parameter combination similar to the one found in
        exercise 1?
    4.  What is the CV score of the optimal parameter combination? How
        does it compare to the CV score from exercise 1?

3.  [From exercise 1,]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  Compute the Sharpe ratio of the resulting in-sample forecasts,
        from point 1.a (see Chapter 14 for a definition of Sharpe
        ratio).
    2.  Repeat point 1.a, this time with `accuracy` as the scoring
        function. Compute the in-sample forecasts derived from the
        hyper-tuned parameters.
    3.  What scoring method leads to higher (in-sample) Sharpe ratio?

4.  [From exercise 2,]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  Compute the Sharpe ratio of the resulting in-sample forecasts,
        from point 2.a.
    2.  Repeat point 2.a, this time with `accuracy` as the scoring
        function. Compute the in-sample forecasts derived from the
        hyper-tuned parameters.
    3.  What scoring method leads to higher (in-sample) Sharpe ratio?

5.  [Read the definition of log loss,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *L*
    > > [\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Y* [,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *P*
    > > [\].]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  Why is the scoring function `neg_log_loss` defined as the
        negative log loss, − *L* \[*Y* , *P* \]?
    2.  What would be the outcome of maximizing the log loss, rather
        than the negative log loss?

6.  [Consider an investment strategy that sizes its bets equally,
    > > regardless of the forecast\'s confidence. In this case, what is
    > > a more appropriate scoring function for hyper-parameter tuning,
    > > accuracy or cross-entropy loss?]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000498144}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000503563}

`<font size="5">`{=html} **CHAPTER 10** `</font>`{=html}\
\
`<font size="5">`{=html} **Bet Sizing** `</font>`{=html}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000503791}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **10.1 Motivation** `</font>`{=html}

[There are fascinating parallels between strategy games and investing.
Some of the best portfolio managers I have worked with are excellent
poker players, perhaps more so than chess players. One reason is bet
sizing, for which Texas Hold\'em provides a great analogue and training
ground. Your ML algorithm can achieve high accuracy, but if you do not
size your bets properly, your investment strategy will inevitably lose
money. In this chapter we will review a few approaches to size bets from
ML predictions.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000504483}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **10.2 Strategy-Independent Bet Sizing
Approaches** `</font>`{=html}

[Consider two strategies on the same instrument. Let]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*m
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[∈ \[ − 1, 1\] be the bet size of strategy]{bgcolor="inherit"} *i* [at
time]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [, where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *m
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[= −1 indicates a full short position and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *m
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[= 1 indicates a full long position. Suppose that one strategy produced
a sequence of bet sizes \[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *m
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}1,\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *m
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}1,\ 2`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *m
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}1,\ 3`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [\] = \[.5,
1, 0\], as the market price followed a sequence \[]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*p ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *p
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}2`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *p
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}3`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [\] = \[1, .5,
1.25\], where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *p
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [is the
price at time]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t.* [The other strategy produced a
sequence \[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *m
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}2,\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *m
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}2,\ 2`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *m
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}2,\ 3`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [\] = \[1,
.5, 0\], as it was forced to reduce its bet size once the market moved
against the initial full position. Both strategies produced forecasts
that turned out to be correct (the price increased by 25%
between]{bgcolor="inherit"} *p
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *p
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}3`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [), however the
first strategy made money (0.5) while the second strategy lost money
(−.125).]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[We would prefer to size positions in such way that we reserve some cash
for the possibility that the trading signal strengthens before it
weakens. One option is to compute the series]{bgcolor="inherit"} *c
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[=]{bgcolor="inherit"} *c
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *l*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[−]{bgcolor="inherit"} *c
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *s*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[, where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *c
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *l*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[is the number of concurrent long bets at time]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t*
[, and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *c
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *s*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[is the number of concurrent short bets at time]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t.*
[This bet concurrency is derived, for each side, similarly to how we
computed label concurrency in Chapter 4 (recall the]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`t1` [object, with overlapping time spans). We fit a mixture of two
Gaussians on {]{bgcolor="inherit"} *c
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [},
applying a method like the one described in López de Prado and Foreman
\[2014\]. Then, the bet size is derived as]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00413.jpg){width="236" height="98"}

[where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *F* [\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x* [\] is the
CDF of the fitted mixture of two Gaussians for a
value]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x* [. For example, we could size the bet as
0.9 when the probability of observing a signal of greater value is only
0.1. The stronger the signal, the smaller the probability that the
signal becomes even stronger, hence the greater the bet
size.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[A second solution is to follow a budgeting approach. We compute the
maximum number (or some other quantile) of concurrent long
bets,]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00417.jpg){width="77" height="20"} [,
and the maximum number of concurrent short bets,]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00420.jpg){width="79" height="20"} [. Then we derive the bet
size as]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00422.jpg){width="239" height="34"}
[, where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *c
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *l*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[is the number of concurrent long bets at time]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t*
[, and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *c
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *s*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[is the number of concurrent short bets at time]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t.*
[The goal is that the maximum position is not reached before the last
concurrent signal is triggered.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[A third approach is to apply meta-labeling, as we explained in Chapter
3. We fit a classifier, such as an SVC or RF, to determine the
probability of misclassification, and use that probability to derive the
bet size.
^[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000510669}\ [[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ `<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}1`</font>`{=html}\ `</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000536150)]{.small}^
This approach has a couple of advantages: First, the ML algorithm that
decides the bet sizes is independent of the primary model, allowing for
the incorporation of features predictive of false positives (see
Chapter 3). Second, the predicted probability can be directly translated
into bet size. Let us see how.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000511123}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **10.3 Bet Sizing from Predicted
Probabilities** `</font>`{=html}

[Let us denote]{bgcolor="inherit"} *p* [\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x* [\]
the probability that label]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x* [takes place. For two
possible outcomes,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x* [∈ { − 1, 1}, we would like
to test the null hypothesis]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00424.jpg){width="141" height="29"} [. We compute the test
statistic]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00426.jpg){width="347"
height="45"} [, with]{bgcolor="inherit"} *z* [∈ ( − ∞, +∞) and
where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Z* [represents the standard Normal
distribution. We derive the bet size as]{bgcolor="inherit"} *m* [=
2]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Z* [\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *z* [\] − 1,
where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *m* [∈ \[ − 1, 1\] and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Z*
[\[.\] is the CDF of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Z* [.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[For more than two possible outcomes, we follow a one-versus-rest
method. Let]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X* [= { − 1, ..., 0, ..., 1} be various
labels associated with bet sizes, and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x*
[∈]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X* [the predicted label. In other words, the
label is identified by the bet size associated with it. For each
label]{bgcolor="inherit"} *i* [= 1, ..., \|\|]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X*
[\|\|, we estimate a probability]{bgcolor="inherit"} *p
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [,
with]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00429.jpg){width="93" height="28"} *.*
[We define]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00431.jpg){width="105"
height="18"} [as the probability of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x* [, and we
would like to test for]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00433.jpg){width="100" height="32"}
[.^[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000513399}\ [[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ `<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}2`</font>`{=html}\ `</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000536731)]{.small}^
We compute the test statistic]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00434.jpg){width="123" height="47"} [, with]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*z* [∈ \[0., . + ∞). We derive the bet size as]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00000.jpg){width="141" height="53"} [,
where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *m* [∈ \[ − 1, 1\] and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Z*
[\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *z* [\] regulates the size for a
prediction]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x* [(where the side is implied
by]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x* [).]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000514124}

[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
10.1`</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000514747) [plots the bet
size as a function of test statistic. Snippet 10.1 implements the
translation from probabilities to bet size. It handles the possibility
that the prediction comes from a meta-labeling estimator, as well from a
standard labeling estimator. In step \#2, it also averages active bets,
and discretizes the final value, which we will explain in the following
sections.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00399.jpg){width="640" height="391"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000514747}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
10.1`</font>`{=html}**](#text00002.html#filepos0000514124) Bet size from
predicted probabilities

> **SNIPPET 10.1 FROM PROBABILITIES TO BET SIZE**

> ![](Image00465.jpg){width="929" height="305"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000515184}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **10.4 Averaging Active Bets** `</font>`{=html}

[Every bet is associated with a holding period, spanning from the time
it originated to the time the first barrier is
touched,]{bgcolor="inherit"} `t1` [(see Chapter 3). One possible
approach is to override an old bet as a new bet arrives; however, that
is likely to lead to excessive turnover. A more sensible approach is to
average all sizes across all bets still active at a given point in time.
Snippet 10.2 illustrates one possible implementation of this
idea.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 10.2 BETS ARE AVERAGED AS LONG AS THEY ARE STILL ACTIVE**

> ![](Image00544.jpg){width="948" height="577"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000516129}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **10.5 Size Discretization** `</font>`{=html}

[Averaging reduces some of the excess turnover, but still it is likely
that small trades will be triggered with every prediction. As this
jitter would cause unnecessary overtrading, I suggest you discretize the
bet size as]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00014.jpg){width="142"
height="36"} [, where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d* [∈ (0, ..1\] determines
the degree of discretization.]{bgcolor="inherit"}
[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000516715}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
10.2`</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000517025) [illustrates
the discretization of the bet size. Snippet 10.3 implements this
notion.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00724.jpg){width="640" height="392"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000517025}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
10.2`</font>`{=html}**](#text00002.html#filepos0000516715)
Discretization of the bet size, d = 0.2

> **SNIPPET 10.3 SIZE DISCRETIZATION TO PREVENT OVERTRADING**

> ![](Image00120.jpg){width="729" height="154"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000517478}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **10.6 Dynamic Bet Sizes and Limit Prices**
`</font>`{=html}

[Recall the triple-barrier labeling method presented in Chapter 3.
Bar]{bgcolor="inherit"} *i* [is formed at time]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[, at which point we forecast the first barrier that will be touched.
That prediction implies a forecasted price,]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00023.jpg){width="69" height="23"} [, consistent with the
barriers' settings. In the period elapsed until the outcome takes
place,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [∈ \[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[\], the price]{bgcolor="inherit"} *p
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [fluctuates
and additional forecasts may be formed,]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00156.jpg){width="69" height="23"} [, where]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*j* [∈ \[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *i* [+ 1,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *I* [\]
and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[≤]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[. In Sections 10.4 and 10.5 we discussed methods for averaging the
active bets and discretizing the bet size as new forecasts are formed.
In this section we will introduce an approach to adjust bet sizes as
market price]{bgcolor="inherit"} *p
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [and
forecast price]{bgcolor="inherit"} *f
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [fluctuate.
In the process, we will derive the order\'s limit
price.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Let]{bgcolor="inherit"} *q
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [be the
current position,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Q* [the maximum absolute position
size, and]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00028.jpg){width="21"
height="20"} [the target position size associated with
forecast]{bgcolor="inherit"} *f
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [, such
that]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00193.jpg){width="188" height="20"}

![](Image00210.jpg){width="157" height="44"}

[where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *m* [\[ω,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x* [\] is the
bet size,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x* [=]{bgcolor="inherit"} *f
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[−]{bgcolor="inherit"} *p
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [is the
divergence between the current market price and the forecast, ω is a
coefficient that regulates the width of the sigmoid function, and
Int\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x* [\] is the integer value
of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x* [. Note that for a real-valued price
divergence]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x* [, − 1 \<]{bgcolor="inherit"} *m*
[\[ω,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x* [\] \< 1, the integer
value]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00231.jpg){width="21" height="20"}
[is bounded]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00249.jpg){width="111"
height="20"} [.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[The target position size]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00044.jpg){width="21" height="20"} [can be dynamically adjusted
