`<font size="4">`{=html} **Notes** `</font>`{=html}

^[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000536150}\ [[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ `<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}1`</font>`{=html}\ `</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000510669)]{.small}^
   The references section lists a number of articles that explain how
these probabilities are derived. Usually these probabilities incorporate
information about the goodness of the fit, or confidence in the
prediction. See Wu et al. \[2004\], and visit
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/svm.html\#scores-and-probabilities`</font>`{=html}](http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/svm.html#scores-and-probabilities)
.

^[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000536731}\ [[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ `<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}2`</font>`{=html}\ `</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000513399)]{.small}^
   Uncertainty is absolute when all outcomes are equally likely.

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000536917}

`<font size="5">`{=html} **CHAPTER 11** `</font>`{=html}\
\
`<font size="5">`{=html} **The Dangers of Backtesting** `</font>`{=html}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000537161}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **11.1 Motivation** `</font>`{=html}

[Backtesting is one of the most essential, and yet least understood,
techniques in the quant arsenal. A common misunderstanding is to think
of backtesting as a research tool. Researching and backtesting is like
drinking and driving. Do not research under the influence of a backtest.
Most backtests published in journals are flawed, as the result of
selection bias on multiple tests (Bailey, Borwein, López de Prado, and
Zhu \[2014\]; Harvey et al. \[2016\]). A full book could be written
listing all the different errors people make while backtesting. I may be
the academic author with the largest number of journal articles on
backtesting^[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000537944}\ [[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ `<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}1`</font>`{=html}\ `</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000575620)]{.small}^
and investment performance metrics, and still I do not feel I would have
the stamina to compile all the different errors I have seen over the
past 20 years. This chapter is not a crash course on backtesting, but a
short list of some of the common errors that even seasoned professionals
make.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000538378}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **11.2 Mission Impossible: The Flawless
Backtest** `</font>`{=html}

[In its narrowest definition, a backtest is a historical simulation of
how a strategy would have performed should it have been run over a past
period of time. As such, it is a hypothetical, and by no means an
experiment. At a physics laboratory, like Berkeley Lab, we can repeat an
experiment while controlling for environmental variables, in order to
deduce a precise cause-effect relationship. In contrast, a backtest
is]{bgcolor="inherit"} *not* [an experiment, and it does not prove
anything. A backtest guarantees nothing, not even achieving that Sharpe
ratio if we could travel back in time in our retrofitted DeLorean DMC-12
(Bailey and López de Prado \[2012\]). Random draws would have been
different. The past would not repeat itself.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[What is the point of a backtest then? It is a sanity check on a number
of variables, including bet sizing, turnover, resilience to costs, and
behavior under a given scenario. A good backtest can be extremely
helpful, but backtesting well is extremely hard. In 2014 a team of
quants at Deutsche Bank, led by Yin Luo, published a study under the
title "Seven Sins of Quantitative Investing" (Luo et al. \[2014\]). It
is a very graphic and accessible piece that I would advise everyone in
this business to read carefully. In it, this team mentions the usual
suspects:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

1.  **Survivorship bias:** Using as investment universe the current one,
    hence ignoring that some companies went bankrupt and securities were
    delisted along the way.
2.  **Look-ahead bias:** Using information that was not public at the
    moment the simulated decision would have been made. Be certain about
    the timestamp for each data point. Take into account release dates,
    distribution delays, and backfill corrections.
3.  **Storytelling:** Making up a story *ex-post* to justify some random
    pattern.
4.  **Data mining and data snooping:** Training the model on the testing
    set.
5.  **Transaction costs:** Simulating transaction costs is hard because
    the only way to be certain about that cost would have been to
    interact with the trading book (i.e., to do the actual trade).
6.  **Outliers:** Basing a strategy on a few extreme outcomes that may
    never happen again as observed in the past.
7.  **Shorting:** Taking a short position on cash products requires
    finding a lender. The cost of lending and the amount available is
    generally unknown, and depends on relations, inventory, relative
    demand, etc.

[These are just a few basic errors that most papers published in
journals make routinely. Other common errors include computing
performance using a non-standard method (Chapter 14); ignoring hidden
risks; focusing only on returns while ignoring other metrics; confusing
correlation with causation; selecting an unrepresentative time period;
failing to expect the unexpected; ignoring the existence of stop-out
limits or margin calls; ignoring funding costs; and forgetting practical
aspects (Sarfati \[2015\]). There are many more, but really, there is no
point in listing them, because of the title of the next
section.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000542210}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **11.3 Even If Your Backtest Is Flawless, It Is
Probably Wrong** `</font>`{=html}

[Congratulations! Your backtest is flawless in the sense that everyone
can reproduce your results, and your assumptions are so conservative
that not even your boss could object to them. You have paid for every
trade more than double what anyone could possibly ask. You have executed
hours after the information was known by half the globe, at a
ridiculously low volume participation rate. Despite all these egregious
costs, your backtest still makes a lot of money. Yet, this flawless
backtest is probably wrong. Why? Because only an expert can produce a
flawless backtest. Becoming an expert means that you have run tens of
thousands of backtests over the years. In conclusion, this is not the
first backtest you produce, so we need to account for the possibility
that this is a false discovery, a statistical fluke that inevitably
comes up after you run multiple tests on the same
dataset.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[The maddening thing about backtesting is that, the better you become at
it, the more likely false discoveries will pop up. Beginners fall for
the seven sins of Luo et al. \[2014\] (there are more, but who\'s
counting?). Professionals may produce flawless backtests, and will still
fall for multiple testing, selection bias, or backtest overfitting
(Bailey and López de Prado \[2014b\]).]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000543781}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **11.4 Backtesting Is Not a Research Tool**
`</font>`{=html}

[Chapter 8 discussed substitution effects, joint effects, masking, MDI,
MDA, SFI, parallelized features, stacked features, etc. Even if some
features are very important, it does not mean that they can be monetized
through an investment strategy. Conversely, there are plenty of
strategies that will appear to be profitable even though they are based
on irrelevant features. Feature importance is a true research tool,
because it helps us understand the nature of the patterns uncovered by
the ML algorithm, regardless of their monetization. Critically, feature
importance is derived]{bgcolor="inherit"} *ex-ante* [, before the
historical performance is simulated.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[In contrast, a backtest is not a research tool. It provides us with
very little insight into the reason why a particular strategy would have
made money. Just as a lottery winner may feel he has done something to
deserve his luck, there is always some]{bgcolor="inherit"} *ex-post*
[story (Luo\'s sin number three). Authors claim to have found hundreds
of "alphas" and "factors," and there is always some convoluted
explanation for them. Instead, what they have found are the lottery
tickets that won the last game. The winner has cashed out, and those
numbers are useless for the next round. If you would not pay extra for
those lottery tickets, why would you care about those hundreds of
alphas? Those authors never tell us about all the tickets that were
sold, that is, the millions of simulations it took to find these "lucky"
alphas.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[The purpose of a backtest is to discard bad models, not to improve
them. Adjusting your model based on the backtest results is a waste of
time . . . and it\'s dangerous. Invest your time and effort in getting
all the components right, as we\'ve discussed elsewhere in the book:
structured data, labeling, weighting, ensembles, cross-validation,
feature importance, bet sizing, etc. By the time you are backtesting, it
is too late. Never backtest until your model has been fully specified.
If the backtest fails, start all over. If you do that, the chances of
finding a false discovery will drop substantially, but still they will
not be zero.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000546295}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **11.5 A Few General Recommendations**
`</font>`{=html}

[Backtest overfitting can be defined as selection bias on multiple
backtests. Backtest overfitting takes place when a strategy is developed
to perform well on a backtest, by monetizing random historical patterns.
Because those random patterns are unlikely to occur again in the future,
the strategy so developed will fail. Every backtested strategy is
overfit to some extent as a result of "selection bias": The only
backtests that most people share are those that portray supposedly
winning investment strategies.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[How to address backtest overfitting is arguably the most fundamental
question in quantitative finance. Why? Because if there was an easy
answer to this question, investment firms would achieve high performance
with certainty, as they would invest only in winning backtests. Journals
would assess with confidence whether a strategy may be a false positive.
Finance could become a true science in the Popperian and Lakatosian
sense (López de Prado \[2017\]). What makes backtest overfitting so hard
to assess is that the probability of false positives changes with every
new test conducted on the same dataset, and that information is either
unknown by the researcher or not shared with investors or referees.
While there is no easy way to prevent overfitting, a number of steps can
help reduce its presence.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

1.  Develop models for entire asset classes or investment universes,
    rather than for specific securities (Chapter 8). Investors
    diversify, hence they do not make mistake *X* only on security *Y.*
    If you find mistake *X* only on security *Y* , no matter how
    apparently profitable, it is likely a false discovery.
2.  Apply bagging (Chapter 6) as a means to both prevent overfitting and
    reduce the variance of the forecasting error. If bagging
    deteriorates the performance of a strategy, it was likely overfit to
    a small number of observations or outliers.
3.  Do not backtest until all your research is complete (Chapters
    1--10).
4.  Record every backtest conducted on a dataset so that the probability
    of backtest overfitting may be estimated on the final selected
    result (see Bailey, Borwein, López de Prado, and Zhu \[2017a\] and
    Chapter 14), and the Sharpe ratio may be properly deflated by the
    number of trials carried out (Bailey and López de Prado \[2014b\]).
5.  Simulate scenarios rather than history (Chapter 12). A standard
    backtest is a historical simulation, which can be easily overfit.
    History is just the random path that was realized, and it could have
    been entirely different. Your strategy should be profitable under a
    wide range of scenarios, not just the anecdotal historical path. It
    is harder to overfit the outcome of thousands of "what if"
    scenarios.
6.  If the backtest fails to identify a profitable strategy, start from
    scratch. Resist the temptation of reusing those results. Follow the
    Second Law of Backtesting.

> `<font size="4">`{=html} **SNIPPET 11.1 MARCOS' SECOND LAW OF
> BACKTESTING** `</font>`{=html}

> > ["Backtesting while researching is like drinking and driving. Do not
> > research under the influence of a backtest."]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> > > `<font size="2">`{=html} [Marcos López de
> > > Prado]{bgcolor="inherit"} `</font>`{=html}
> > > `<font size="2">`{=html} *Advances in Financial Machine Learning*
> > > `</font>`{=html} `<font size="2">`{=html}
> > > [(2018)]{bgcolor="inherit"} `</font>`{=html}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000550515}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **11.6 Strategy Selection** `</font>`{=html}

[In Chapter 7 we discussed how the presence of serial conditionality in
labels defeats standard k-fold cross-validation, because the random
sampling will spatter redundant observations into both the training and
testing sets. We must find a different (true out-of-sample) validation
procedure: a procedure that evaluates our model on the observations
least likely to be correlated/redundant to those used to train the
model. See Arlot and Celisse \[2010\] for a survey.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Scikit-learn has implemented a walk-forward timefolds method.
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}2`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^ Under this
approach, testing moves forward (in the time direction) with the goal of
preventing leakage. This is consistent with the way historical backtests
(and trading) are done. However, in the presence of long-range serial
dependence, testing one observation away from the end of the training
set may not suffice to avoid informational leakage. We will retake this
point in Chapter 12, Section 12.2.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[One disadvantage of the walk-forward method is that it can be easily
overfit. The reason is that without random sampling, there is a single
path of testing that can be repeated over and over until a false
positive appears. Like in standard CV, some randomization is needed to
avoid this sort of performance targeting or backtest optimization, while
avoiding the leakage of examples correlated to the training set into the
testing set. Next, we will introduce a CV method for strategy selection,
based on the estimation of the probability of backtest overfitting
(PBO). We leave for Chapter 12 an explanation of CV methods for
backtesting.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Bailey et al. \[2017a\] estimate the PBO through the combinatorially
symmetric cross-validation (CSCV) method. Schematically, this procedure
works as follows.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[First, we form a matrix]{bgcolor="inherit"} *M* [by collecting the
performance series from the]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [trials. In
particular, each column]{bgcolor="inherit"} *n* [= 1,
...,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [represents a vector of PnL (mark-to-market
profits and losses) over]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [= 1,
...,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [observations associated with a particular
model configuration tried by the researcher.]{bgcolor="inherit"} *M* [is
therefore a real-valued matrix of order (]{bgcolor="inherit"} *TxN* [).
The only conditions we impose are that (1)]{bgcolor="inherit"} *M* [is a
true matrix, that is, with the same number of rows for each column,
where observations are synchronous for every row across
the]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [trials, and (2) the performance evaluation
metric used to choose the "optimal" strategy can be estimated on
subsamples of each column. For example, if that metric is the Sharpe
ratio, we assume that the IID Normal distribution assumption can be held
on various slices of the reported performance. If different model
configurations trade with different frequencies, observations are
aggregated (downsampled) to match a common index]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t*
[= 1, ...,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Second, we partition]{bgcolor="inherit"} *M* [across rows, into an even
number]{bgcolor="inherit"} *S* [of disjoint submatrices of equal
dimensions. Each of these submatrices]{bgcolor="inherit"} *M
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *s*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [,
with]{bgcolor="inherit"} *s* [= 1, ...,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *S* [, is of
order]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00630.jpg){width="48" height="28"}
[.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Third, we form all combinations]{bgcolor="inherit"} *C
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *S*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *M
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *s*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [, taken in
groups of size]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00226.jpg){width="8"
height="28"} [. This gives a total number of
combinations]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00229.jpg){width="368" height="72"}

[For instance, if]{bgcolor="inherit"} *S = 16* [, we will form 12,780
combinations. Each combination]{bgcolor="inherit"} *c*
[∈]{bgcolor="inherit"} *C
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *S*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [is
composed of]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00232.jpg){width="23"
height="26"} [submatrices]{bgcolor="inherit"} *M
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *s*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Fourth, for each combination]{bgcolor="inherit"} *c*
[∈]{bgcolor="inherit"} *C
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *S*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [,
we:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    constitute *c. J* is a matrix of order

2.  Form the *testing set* ![](Image00242.jpg){width="10" height="17"} ,

    ![](Image00245.jpg){width="10" height="17"} is the

    rows of *M* that are not part of *J.*
3.  Form a vector *R* of performance statistics of order *N* , where the
    *n* -th item of *R* reports the performance associated with the *n*

4.  Determine the element *n* \* such that
    ![](Image00250.jpg){width="69" height="18"} , ∀*n* = 1, ..., *N* .
    In other words, ![](Image00252.jpg){width="149" height="19"} .
5.  Form a vector ![](Image00256.jpg){width="12" height="17"} of
    performance statistics of order *N* , where the *n* -th item of
    ![](Image00260.jpg){width="12" height="17"} reports the performance

    height="17"} (the testing set).
6.  Determine the relative rank of ![](Image00267.jpg){width="28"

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000561842}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **Exercises** `</font>`{=html}

::: {height="1em"}
:::

1.  [An analyst fits an RF classifier where some of the features include
    > > seasonally adjusted employment data. He aligns with January data
    > > the seasonally adjusted value of January, etc. What "sin" has he
    > > committed?]{bgcolor="inherit"}

2.  [An analyst develops an ML algorithm where he generates a signal
    > > using closing prices, and executed at close. What\'s the
    > > sin?]{bgcolor="inherit"}

3.  [There is a 98.51% correlation between total revenue generated by
    > > arcades and computer science doctorates awarded in the United
    > > States. As the number of doctorates is expected to grow, should
    > > we invest in arcades companies? If not, what\'s the
    > > sin?]{bgcolor="inherit"}

4.  [The]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Wall Street Journal* [has reported that
    > > September is the only month of the year that has negative
    > > average stock returns, looking back 20, 50, and 100 years.
    > > Should we sell stocks at the end of August? If not, what\'s the
    > > sin?]{bgcolor="inherit"}

5.  [We download P/E ratios from Bloomberg, rank stocks every month,
    > > sell the top quartile, and buy the long quartile. Performance is
    > > amazing. What\'s the sin?]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000563893}

[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}http://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/cf\_dev/AbsByAuth.cfm?per\_id=434076`</font>`{=html}](http://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/cf_dev/AbsByAuth.cfm?per_id=434076)

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000576018}

`<font size="5">`{=html} **CHAPTER 12** `</font>`{=html}\
\
`<font size="5">`{=html} **Backtesting through Cross-Validation**
`</font>`{=html}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000576272}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **12.1 Motivation** `</font>`{=html}

[A backtest evaluates out-of-sample the performance of an investment
strategy using past observations. These past observations can be used in
two ways: (1) in a narrow sense, to simulate the historical performance
of an investment strategy, as if it had been run in the past; and (2) in
a broader sense, to simulate scenarios that did not happen in the past.
The first (narrow) approach, also known as walk-forward, is so prevalent
that, in fact, the term "backtest" has become a]{bgcolor="inherit"} *de
facto* [synonym for "historical simulation." The second (broader)
approach is far less known, and in this chapter we will introduce some
novel ways to carry it out. Each approach has its pros and cons, and
each should be given careful consideration.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000577230}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **12.2 The Walk-Forward Method**
`</font>`{=html}

[The most common backtest method in the literature is the walk-forward
(WF) approach. WF is a historical simulation of how the strategy would
have performed in past. Each strategy decision is based on observations
that predate that decision. As we saw in Chapter 11, carrying out a
flawless WF simulation is a daunting task that requires extreme
knowledge of the data sources, market microstructure, risk management,
performance measurement standards (e.g., GIPS), multiple testing
methods, experimental mathematics, etc. Unfortunately, there is no
generic recipe to conduct a backtest. To be accurate and representative,
each backtest must be customized to evaluate the assumptions of a
particular strategy.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[WF enjoys two key advantages: (1) WF has a clear historical
interpretation. Its performance can be reconciled with paper trading.
(2) History is a filtration; hence, using trailing data guarantees that
the testing set is out-of-sample (no leakage), as long as purging has
been properly implemented (see Chapter 7, Section 7.4.1). It is a common
mistake to find leakage in WF backtests, where]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`t1.index` [falls within the training set, but]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`t1.values` [fall within the testing set (see Chapter 3). Embargoing is
not needed in WF backtests, because the training set always predates the
testing set.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **12.2.1 Pitfalls of the Walk-Forward Method**
`</font>`{=html}

[WF suffers from three major disadvantages: First, a single scenario is
tested (the historical path), which can be easily overfit (Bailey et al.
\[2014\]). Second, WF is not necessarily representative of future
performance, as results can be biased by the particular sequence of
datapoints. Proponents of the WF method typically argue that predicting
the past would lead to overly optimistic performance estimates. And yet,
very often fitting an outperforming model on the reversed sequence of
observations will lead to an underperforming WF backtest. The truth is,
it is as easy to overfit a walk-forward backtest as to overfit a
walk-backward backtest, and the fact that changing the sequence of
observations yields inconsistent outcomes is evidence of that
overfitting. If proponents of WF were right, we should observe that
walk-backwards backtests systematically outperform their walk-forward
counterparts. That is not the case, hence the main argument in favor of
WF is rather weak.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[To make this second disadvantage clearer, suppose an equity strategy
that is backtested with a WF on S&P 500 data, starting January 1, 2007.
Until March 15, 2009, the mix of rallies and sell-offs will train the
strategy to be market neutral, with low confidence on every position.
After that, the long rally will dominate the dataset, and by January 1,
2017, buy forecasts will prevail over sell forecasts. The performance
would be very different had we played the information backwards, from
January 1, 2017 to January 1, 2007 (a long rally followed by a sharp
sell-off). By exploiting a particular sequence, a strategy selected by
WF may set us up for a debacle.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[The third disadvantage of WF is that the initial decisions are made on
a smaller portion of the total sample. Even if a warm-up period is set,
most of the information is used by only a small portion of the
decisions. Consider a strategy with a warm-up period that
uses]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [observations out
of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T.* [This strategy makes half of its
decisions]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00307.jpg){width="53"
height="36"} [on an average number of datapoints,]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00311.jpg){width="371" height="53"}

[which is only a]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00314.jpg){width="58"
height="29"} [fraction of the observations. Although this problem is
attenuated by increasing the warm-up period, doing so also reduces the
length of the backtest.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000581868}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **12.3 The Cross-Validation Method**
`</font>`{=html}

[Investors often ask how a strategy would perform if subjected to a
stress scenario as unforeseeable as the 2008 crisis, or the dot-com
bubble, or the taper tantrum, or the China scare of 2015--2016, etc. One
way to answer is to split the observations into two sets, one with the
period we wish to test (testing set), and one with the rest (training
set). For example, a classifier would be trained on the period January
1, 2009--January 1, 2017, then tested on the period January 1,
2008--December 31, 2008. The performance we will obtain for 2008 is not
historically accurate, since the classifier was trained on data that was
only available after 2008. But historical accuracy was not the goal of
the test. The objective of the test was to subject a
strategy]{bgcolor="inherit"} *ignorant* [of 2008 to a stress scenario
such as 2008.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[The goal of backtesting through cross-validation (CV) is not to derive
historically accurate performance, but to infer future performance from
a number of out-of-sample scenarios. For each period of the backtest, we
simulate the performance of a classifier that knew everything except for
that period.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **Advantages** `</font>`{=html}

::: {height="8pt"}
:::

1.  The test is not the result of a particular (historical) scenario. In
    fact, CV tests *k* alternative scenarios, of which only one
    corresponds with the historical sequence.
2.  Every decision is made on sets of equal size. This makes outcomes
    comparable across periods, in terms of the amount of information
    used to make those decisions.
3.  Every observation is part of one and only one testing set. There is
    no warm-up subset, thereby achieving the longest possible
    out-of-sample simulation.

`<font size="4">`{=html} **Disadvantages** `</font>`{=html}

::: {height="8pt"}
:::

1.  Like WF, a single backtest path is simulated (although not the
    historical one). There is one and only one forecast generated per
    observation.
2.  CV has no clear historical interpretation. The output does not
    simulate how the strategy would have performed in the past, but how
    it may perform *in the future* under various stress scenarios (a
    useful result in its own right).
3.  Because the training set does not trail the testing set, leakage is
    possible. Extreme care must be taken to avoid leaking testing
    information into the training set. See Chapter 7 for a discussion on
    how purging and embargoing can help prevent informational leakage in
    the context of CV.

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000585069}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **12.4 The Combinatorial Purged
Cross-Validation Method** `</font>`{=html}

[In this section I will present a new method, which addresses the main
drawback of the WF and CV methods, namely that those schemes test a
single path. I call it the "combinatorial purged cross-validation"
(CPCV) method. Given a number φ of backtest paths targeted by the
researcher, CPCV generates the precise number of combinations of
training/testing sets needed to generate those paths, while purging
training observations that contain leaked
information.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **12.4.1 Combinatorial Splits**
`</font>`{=html}

[Consider]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [observations partitioned
into]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [groups without shuffling, where
groups]{bgcolor="inherit"} *n* [= 1, ...,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [− 1
are of size ⌊]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [/]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [⌋,
the]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [th group is of size]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T*
[− ⌊]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [/]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N*
[⌋(]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [− 1), and ⌊.⌋ is the floor or integer
function. For a testing set of size]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [groups, the
number of possible training/testing splits is]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00317.jpg){width="205" height="55"}

[Since each combination involves]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [tested groups,
the total number of tested groups is]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00320.jpg){width="58" height="32"} [. And since we have
computed all possible combinations, these tested groups are uniformly
distributed across all]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [(each group belongs to
the same number of training and testing sets). The implication is that
from]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [-sized testing sets on]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*N* [groups we can backtest a total number of paths
φ\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k*
[\],]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00323.jpg){width="314" height="55"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000587653}

[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
12.1`</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000588582) [illustrates
the composition of train/test splits for]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [= 6
and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [= 2]{bgcolor="inherit"} *.* [There
are]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00326.jpg){width="71" height="33"}
[splits, indexed as]{bgcolor="inherit"} *S1, ... ,S15.* [For each split,
the figure marks with a cross (]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x* [) the groups
included in the testing set, and leaves unmarked the groups that form
the training set. Each group forms part of φ\[6, 2\] = 5 testing sets,
therefore this train/test split scheme allows us to compute 5 backtest
paths.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00330.jpg){width="640" height="124"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000588582}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
12.1`</font>`{=html}**](#text00002.html#filepos0000587653) Paths
generated for ***φ*** \[6, 2\] = 5

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000588747}

[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
12.2`</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000590228) [shows the
assignment of each tested group to one backtest path. For example, path
1 is the result of combining the forecasts from (]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*G* [1,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *S* [1), (]{bgcolor="inherit"} *G*
[2,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *S* [1), (]{bgcolor="inherit"} *G*
[3,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *S* [2), (]{bgcolor="inherit"} *G*
[4,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *S* [3), (]{bgcolor="inherit"} *G*
[5,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *S* [4) and (]{bgcolor="inherit"} *G*
[6,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *S* [5). Path 2 is the result of combining
forecasts from (]{bgcolor="inherit"} *G* [1,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *S*
[2), (]{bgcolor="inherit"} *G* [2,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *S* [6),
(]{bgcolor="inherit"} *G* [3,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *S* [6),
(]{bgcolor="inherit"} *G* [4,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *S* [7),
(]{bgcolor="inherit"} *G* [5,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *S* [8) and
(]{bgcolor="inherit"} *G* [6,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *S* [9), and so
on.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00332.jpg){width="640" height="120"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000590228}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
12.2`</font>`{=html}**](#text00002.html#filepos0000588747) Assignment of
testing groups to each of the 5 paths

[These paths are generated by training the classifier on a portion θ = 1
−]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [/]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [of the data for
each combination. Although it is theoretically possible to train on a
portion θ \< 1/2, in practice we will assume that]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*k* [≤]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [/2]{bgcolor="inherit"} *.* [The portion
of data in the training set θ increases with]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N*
[→]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [but it decreases with]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*k* [→]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [/2]{bgcolor="inherit"} *.* [The number
of paths φ\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [\]
increases with]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [→]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [and
with]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [→]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N*
[/2]{bgcolor="inherit"} *.* [In the limit, the largest number of paths
is achieved by setting]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [=]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*T* [and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [=]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [/2
=]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [/2, at the expense of training the classifier
on only half of the data for each combination (θ =
1/2).]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **12.4.2 The Combinatorial Purged
Cross-Validation Backtesting Algorithm** `</font>`{=html}

[In Chapter 7 we introduced the concepts of purging and embargoing in
the context of CV. We will now use these concepts for backtesting
through CV. The CPCV backtesting algorithm proceeds as
follows:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

1.  Partition *T* observations into *N* groups without shuffling, where
    groups *n* = 1, ..., *N* − 1 are of size ⌊*T* /*N* ⌋, and the *N* th
    group is of size *T* − ⌊*T* /*N* ⌋(*N* − 1).
2.  Compute all possible training/testing splits, where for each split
    *N* − *k* groups constitute the training set and *k* groups
    constitute the testing set.
3.  For any pair of labels (*y
    ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* , *y
    ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* ),
    where *y
    ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* belongs
    to the training set and *y
    ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* belongs
    to the testing set, apply the `PurgedKFold` class to purge *y
    ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* if *y
    ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* spans
    over a period used to determine label *y
    ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* . This
    class will also apply an embargo, should some testing samples
    predate some training samples.
4.  Fit classifiers on the ![](Image00519.jpg){width="46" height="32"}
    training sets, and produce forecasts on the respective
    ![](Image00523.jpg){width="46" height="32"} testing sets.
5.  Compute the φ\[*N* , *k* \] backtest paths. You can calculate one
    Sharpe ratio from each path, and from that derive the empirical
    distribution of the strategy\'s Sharpe ratio (rather than a single
    Sharpe ratio, like WF or CV).

`<font size="4">`{=html} **12.4.3 A Few Examples** `</font>`{=html}

[For]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [= 1, we will obtain
φ\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [, 1\] = 1 path, in which case CPCV reduces
to CV. Thus, CPCV can be understood as a generalization of CV
for]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [\> 1]{bgcolor="inherit"} *.*

[For]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [= 2, we will obtain
φ\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [, 2\] =]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [− 1 paths.
This is a particularly interesting case, because while training the
classifier on a large portion of the data, θ = 1 −
2/]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [, we can generate almost as many backtest
paths as the number of groups,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [−
1]{bgcolor="inherit"} *.* [An easy rule of thumb is to partition the
data into]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [= φ + 1 groups, where φ is the number
of paths we target, and then form]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00367.jpg){width="47" height="32"} [combinations. In the limit,
we can assign one group per observation,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N*
[=]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [, and generate φ\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T*
[, 2\] =]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [− 1 paths, while training the
classifier on a portion θ = 1 − 2/]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [of the data
per combination.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[If even more paths are needed, we can increase]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k*
[→]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [/2, but as explained earlier that will come
at the cost of using a smaller portion of the dataset for training. In
practice,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [= 2 is often enough to generate the
needed φ paths, by setting]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [= φ + 1
≤]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000596425}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **12.5 How Combinatorial Purged
Cross-Validation Addresses Backtest Overfitting** `</font>`{=html}

[Given a sample of IID random variables,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[∼]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Z* [,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *i* [= 1,
...,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *I* [, where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Z* [is the
standard normal distribution, the expected maximum of that sample can be
approximated as]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00346.jpg){width="574" height="38"}

[where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Z
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}−\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^* [\[.\] is the
inverse of the CDF of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Z* [, γ ≈ 0.5772156649⋅⋅⋅ is
the Euler-Mascheroni constant, and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *I* [≫ 1 (see
Bailey et al. \[2014\] for a proof). Now suppose that a researcher
backtests]{bgcolor="inherit"} *I* [strategies on an instrument that
behaves like a martingale, with Sharpe ratios {]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [}
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}=\ 1,\ ...,`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *I*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
, E\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [\] = 0, σ
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}2`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [\] \> 0,
and]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00432.jpg){width="68" height="29"} [.
Even though the true Sharpe ratio is zero, we expect to find one
strategy with a Sharpe ratio of]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00536.jpg){width="340" height="20"}

[WF backtests exhibit high variance, σ\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [\] ≫ 0,
for at least one reason: A large portion of the decisions are based on a
small portion of the dataset. A few observations will have a large
weight on the Sharpe ratio. Using a warm-up period will reduce the
backtest length, which may contribute to making the variance even
higher. WF\'s high variance leads to false discoveries, because
researchers will select the backtest with the
maximum]{bgcolor="inherit"} *estimated* [Sharpe ratio, even if
the]{bgcolor="inherit"} *true* [Sharpe ratio is zero. That is the reason
it is imperative to control for the number of trials]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*(I)* [in the context of WF backtesting. Without this information, it is
not possible to determine the Family-Wise Error Rate (FWER), False
Discovery Rate (FDR), Probability of Backtest Overfitting (PBO, see
Chapter 11) or similar model assessment statistic.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[CV backtests (Section 12.3) address that source of variance by training
each classifier on equal and large portions of the dataset. Although CV
leads to fewer false discoveries than WF, both approaches still estimate
the Sharpe ratio from a single path for a strategy]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*i* [,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [, and that
estimation may be highly volatile. In contrast, CPCV derives the
distribution of Sharpe ratios from a large number of
paths,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *j* [= 1, ..., φ, with mean
E\[{]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[}
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}=\ 1,\ ...,\ φ`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
\] = μ ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ and
variance σ ^[`<font size="2">`{=html}2`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
\[{]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[}
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}=\ 1,\ ...,\ φ`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
\] = σ ^[`<font size="2">`{=html}2`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ . The
variance of the sample mean of CPCV paths is]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00594.jpg){width="491" height="25"}

[where σ ^[`<font size="2">`{=html}2`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ is the
variance of the Sharpe ratios across paths for
strategy]{bgcolor="inherit"} *i* [, and]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00832.jpg){width="14" height="17"} [is the average off-diagonal
correlation among {]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[}
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}=\ 1,\ ...,\ φ`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
. CPCV leads to fewer false discoveries than CV and WF,
because]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00763.jpg){width="47" height="18"}
[implies that the variance of the sample mean is lower than the variance
of the sample,]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00552.jpg){width="171" height="25"}

[The more uncorrelated the paths are,]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00555.jpg){width="52" height="18"} [, the lower CPCV\'s
variance will be, and in the limit CPCV will report the true Sharpe
ratio E\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [\] with
zero variance,]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00845.jpg){width="149"
height="25"} *.* [There will not be selection bias, because the strategy
selected out of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *i* [= 1, ...,]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*I* [will be the one with the highest]{bgcolor="inherit"} *true* [Sharpe
ratio.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Of course, we know that zero variance is unachievable, since φ has an
upper bound,]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00006.jpg){width="95"
height="28"} [. Still, for a large enough number of paths φ, CPCV could
make the variance of the backtest so small as to make the probability of
a false discovery negligible.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[In Chapter 11, we argued that backtest overfitting may be the most
important open problem in all of mathematical finance. Let us see how
CPCV helps address this problem in practice. Suppose that a researcher
submits a strategy to a journal, supported by an overfit WF backtest,
selected from a large number of undisclosed trials. The journal could
ask the researcher to repeat his experiments using a CPCV for a
given]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [. Because
the researcher did not know in advance the number and characteristics of
the paths to be backtested, his overfitting efforts will be easily
defeated. The paper will be rejected or withdrawn from consideration.
Hopefully CPCV will be used to reduce the number of false discoveries
published in journals and elsewhere.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000604305}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **Exercises** `</font>`{=html}

::: {height="1em"}
:::

1.  [Suppose that you develop a momentum strategy on a futures contract,
    > > where the forecast is based on an AR(1) process. You backtest
    > > this strategy using the WF method, and the Sharpe ratio is 1.5.
    > > You then repeat the backtest on the reversed series and achieve
    > > a Sharpe ratio of --1.5. What would be the mathematical grounds
    > > for disregarding the second result, if any?]{bgcolor="inherit"}

2.  [You develop a mean-reverting strategy on a futures contract. Your
    > > WF backtest achieves a Sharpe ratio of 1.5. You increase the
    > > length of the warm-up period, and the Sharpe ratio drops to 0.7.
    > > You go ahead and present only the result with the higher Sharpe
    > > ratio, arguing that a strategy with a shorter warm-up is more
    > > realistic. Is this selection bias?]{bgcolor="inherit"}

3.  [Your strategy achieves a Sharpe ratio of 1.5 on a WF backtest, but
    > > a Sharpe ratio of 0.7 on a CV backtest. You go ahead and present
    > > only the result with the higher Sharpe ratio, arguing that the
    > > WF backtest is historically accurate, while the CV backtest is a
    > > scenario simulation, or an inferential exercise. Is this
    > > selection bias?]{bgcolor="inherit"}

4.  [Your strategy produces 100,000 forecasts over time. You would like
    > > to derive the CPCV distribution of Sharpe ratios by generating
    > > 1,000 paths. What are the possible combinations of parameters
    > > (]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [) that
    > > will allow you to achieve that?]{bgcolor="inherit"}

5.  [You discover a strategy that achieves a Sharpe ratio of 1.5 in a WF
    > > backtest. You write a paper explaining the theory that would
    > > justify such result, and submit it to an academic journal. The
    > > editor replies that one referee has requested you repeat your
    > > backtest using a CPCV method with]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [= 100
    > > and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [= 2, including your code and full
    > > datasets. You follow these instructions, and the mean Sharpe
    > > ratio is --1 with a standard deviation of 0.5. Furious, you do
    > > not reply, but instead withdraw your submission, and resubmit in
    > > a different journal of higher impact factor. After 6 months,
    > > your paper is accepted. You appease your conscience thinking
    > > that, if the discovery is false, it is the journal\'s fault for
    > > not having requested a CPCV test. You think, "It cannot be
    > > unethical, since it is permitted, and everybody does it." What
    > > are the arguments, scientific or ethical, to justify your
    > > actions?]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000607704}

    probability of backtest overfitting." *Journal of Computational
    Finance* , Vol. 20, No. 4, pp. 39--70. Available at
    [`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}https://ssrn.com/abstract=2326253`</font>`{=html}](https://ssrn.com/abstract=2326253)
    .

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000609384}

`<font size="5">`{=html} **CHAPTER 13** `</font>`{=html}\
\
`<font size="5">`{=html} **Backtesting on Synthetic Data**
`</font>`{=html}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000609631}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **13.1 Motivation** `</font>`{=html}

[In this chapter we will study an alternative backtesting method, which
uses history to generate a synthetic dataset with statistical
characteristics estimated from the observed data. This will allow us to
backtest a strategy on a large number of unseen, synthetic testing sets,
hence reducing the likelihood that the strategy has been fit to a
particular set of
datapoints.^[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000610150}\ [[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ `<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}1`</font>`{=html}\ `</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000681120)]{.small}^
This is a very extensive subject, and in order to reach some depth we
will focus on the backtesting of trading rules.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000610409}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **13.2 Trading Rules** `</font>`{=html}

[Investment strategies can be defined as algorithms that postulate the
existence of a market inefficiency. Some strategies rely on econometric
models to predict prices, using macroeconomic variables such as GDP or
inflation; other strategies use fundamental and accounting information
to price securities, or search for arbitrage-like opportunities in the
pricing of derivatives products, etc. For instance, suppose that
financial intermediaries tend to sell off-the-run bonds two days before
U.S. Treasury auctions, in order to raise the cash needed for buying the
new "paper." One could monetize on that knowledge by selling off-the-run
bonds three days before auctions. But how? Each investment strategy
requires an implementation tactic, often referred to as "trading
rules."]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[There are dozens of hedge fund styles, each running dozens of unique
investment strategies. While strategies can be very heterogeneous in
nature, tactics are relatively homogeneous. Trading rules provide the
algorithm that must be followed to enter and exit a position. For
example, a position will be entered when the strategy\'s signal reaches
a certain value. Conditions for exiting a position are often defined
through thresholds for profit-taking and stop-losses. These entry and
exit rules rely on parameters that are usually calibrated via historical
simulations. This practice leads to the problem of]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*backtest overfitting* [, because these parameters target specific
observations in-sample, to the point that the investment strategy is so
attached to the past that it becomes unfit for the
future.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[An important clarification is that we are interested in the exit
corridor conditions that maximize performance. In other words, the
position already exists, and the question is how to exit it optimally.
This is the dilemma often faced by execution traders, and it should not
be mistaken with the determination of entry and exit thresholds for
investing in a security. For a study of that alternative question, see,
for example, Bertram \[2009\].]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Bailey et al. \[2014, 2017\] discuss the problem of backtest
overfitting, and provide methods to determine to what extent a simulated
performance may be inflated due to overfitting. While assessing the
probability of backtest overfitting is a useful tool to discard
superfluous investment strategies, it would be better to avoid the risk
of overfitting, at least in the context of calibrating a trading rule.
In theory this could be accomplished by deriving the optimal parameters
for the trading rule directly from the stochastic process that generates
the data, rather than engaging in historical simulations. This is the
approach we take in this chapter. Using the entire historical sample, we
will characterize the stochastic process that generates the observed
stream of returns, and derive the optimal values for the trading rule\'s
parameters without requiring a historical
simulation.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000613748}

> **Definition 1: Trading Rule:** [A trading rule for
> strategy]{bgcolor="inherit"} *S* [is defined by the set of

[One way to calibrate (by brute force) the trading rule is
to:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

1.  Define a set of alternative values of *R* , Ω: ={*R* }.
2.  Simulate historically (backtest) the performance of *S* under
    alternative values of *R* ∈ Ω.
3.  Select the optimal *R* \*.

[More formally:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[) maximizes the Sharpe ratio of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *S*

sample of size]{bgcolor="inherit"} *I* [, it is easy to
overfit]{bgcolor="inherit"} *R.* [A trivial overfit occurs when a
pair]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00608.jpg){width="43" height="18"}
[targets a few outliers. Bailey et al. \[2017\] provide a rigorous
definition of backtest overfitting, which can be applied to our study of
trading rules as follows.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **Definition 2: Overfit Trading Rule:** *R* [\* is overfit
> if]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00612.jpg){width="311" height="59"} [,
> where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *j* [=]{bgcolor="inherit"} *I* [+ 1,
> ...]{bgcolor="inherit"} *J* [and Me
> ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}Ω`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ \[.\] is the
> median.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Intuitively, an optimal in-sample (IS,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *i* [∈
\[1,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *I* [\]) trading rule]{bgcolor="inherit"} *R*
[\* is overfit when it is expected to underperform the median of
alternative trading rules]{bgcolor="inherit"} *R* [∈ Ω out-of-sample
(OOS,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *j* [∈ \[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *I* [+
1,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *J* [\]). This is essentially the same definition
we used in chapter 11 to derive PBO. Bailey et al. \[2014\] argue that
it is hard not to overfit a backtest, particularly when there are free
variables able to target specific observations IS, or the number of
elements in Ω is large. A trading rule introduces such free variables,
because]{bgcolor="inherit"} *R* [\* can be determined independently
from]{bgcolor="inherit"} *S.* [The outcome is that the backtest profits
from random noise IS, making]{bgcolor="inherit"} *R* [\* unfit for OOS
opportunities. Those same authors show that overfitting leads to
negative performance OOS when Δπ
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
exhibits serial dependence. While PBO provides a useful method to
evaluate to what extent a backtest has been overfit, it would be
convenient to avoid this problem in the first
place.^[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000622966}\ [[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ `<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}2`</font>`{=html}\ `</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000681367)]{.small}^
To that aim we dedicate the following section.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000623154}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **13.4 Our Framework** `</font>`{=html}

[Until now we have not characterized the stochastic process from which
observations π
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
are drawn. We are interested in finding an optimal trading rule (OTR)
for those scenarios where overfitting would be most damaging, such as
when π
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
exhibits serial correlation. In particular, suppose a discrete
Ornstein-Uhlenbeck (O-U) process on prices]{bgcolor="inherit"}

∼]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [(0, 1). The seed value for this process
is]{bgcolor="inherit"} *P
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[, the level targeted by opportunity]{bgcolor="inherit"} *i*

and φ determines the speed at which]{bgcolor="inherit"} *P

[converges towards]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00811.jpg){width="65"
height="21"} [. Because π

~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*

[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}13.2`</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000623869)

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000627390}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **13.5 Numerical Determination of Optimal
Trading Rules** `</font>`{=html}

[In the previous section we used an O-U specification to characterize
the stochastic process generating the returns of
strategy]{bgcolor="inherit"} *S.* [In this section we will present a
procedure to numerically derive the OTR for any specification in
general, and the O-U specification in particular.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **13.5.1 The Algorithm** `</font>`{=html}

[The algorithm consists of five sequential steps.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

-   **Step 1** : We estimate the input parameters {σ, φ}, by linearizing
    equation (
    [`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}13.2`</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000623869)
    ) as:

    > > [`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}(13.5)`</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000628993)
    > > []{#text00002.html#filepos0000628479}
    > > ![](Image00691.jpg){width="326" height="21"}

[We can then form vectors]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X*
[and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Y* [by sequencing
opportunities:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

(13.6)![](Image00670.jpg){width="461" height="197"}

[Applying OLS on equation (]{bgcolor="inherit"}
[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000628993}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}13.5`</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000628479)
[), we can estimate the original O-U parameters as,]{bgcolor="inherit"}

(13.7)![](Image00748.jpg){width="135" height="118"}

[where cov\[ ·, ·\] is the covariance operator.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

-   **Step 2** : We construct a mesh of stop-loss and profit-taking
    pairs, ![](Image00842.jpg){width="43" height="18"} . For example, a
    Cartesian product of ![](Image00079.jpg){width="205" height="29"}
    and ![](Image00168.jpg){width="164" height="29"} give us 20 × 20
    nodes, each constituting an alternative trading rule *R* ∈ Ω.
-   **Step 3** : We generate a large number of paths (e.g., 100,000) for
    π
    ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
    applying our estimates ![](Image00265.jpg){width="48" height="17"} .
    As seed values, we use the observed initial conditions
    ![](Image00354.jpg){width="117" height="21"} associated with an
    opportunity *i.* Because a position cannot be held for an unlimited
    period of time, we can impose a maximum holding period (e.g., 100
    observations) at which point the position is exited even though
    ![](Image00437.jpg){width="113" height="19"} . This maximum holding
    period is equivalent to the vertical bar of the triple-barrier
    method (Chapter
    3).^[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000630739}\ [[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ `<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}3`</font>`{=html}\ `</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000681646)]{.small}^
-   **Step 4** : We apply the 100,000 paths generated in Step 3 on each
    node of the 20 × 20 mesh ![](Image00500.jpg){width="43" height="18"}
    generated in Step 2. For each node, we apply the stop-loss and
    profit-taking logic, giving us 100,000 values of
    ![](Image00837.jpg){width="30" height="17"} . Likewise, for each
    node we compute the Sharpe ratio associated with that trading rule
    as described in equation (
    [`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}13.1`</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000619385)
    ). See Bailey and López de Prado \[2012\] for a study of the
    confidence interval of the Sharpe ratio estimator. This result can
    be used in three different ways: Step 5a, Step 5b and Step 5c).
-   **Step 5a** : We determine the pair ![](Image00686.jpg){width="43"
    height="18"} within the mesh of trading rules that is optimal, given
    the input parameters ![](Image00774.jpg){width="48" height="17"} and
    the observed initial conditions ![](Image00011.jpg){width="117"
    height="21"} .
-   **Step 5b** : If strategy *S* provides a profit target
    ![](Image00027.jpg){width="17" height="19"} for a particular
    opportunity *i* , we can use that information in conjunction with
    the results in Step 4 to determine the optimal stop-loss,
    ![](Image00463.jpg){width="17" height="19"} .
-   **Step 5c** : If the trader has a maximum stop-loss
    ![](Image00052.jpg){width="17" height="19"} imposed by the fund\'s
    management for opportunity *i* , we can use that information in
    conjunction with the results in Step 4 to determine the optimal
    profit-taking ![](Image00482.jpg){width="17" height="19"} within the
    range of stop-losses ![](Image00075.jpg){width="44" height="23"} .

[Bailey and López de Prado \[2013\] prove that the half-life of the
process in equation (]{bgcolor="inherit"}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}13.2`</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000623869)
[) is]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00521.jpg){width="87" height="32"} [,
with the requirement that φ ∈ (0, 1). From that result, we can determine
the value of φ associated with a certain half-life τ
as]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00631.jpg){width="71" height="27"}
[.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **13.5.2 Implementation** `</font>`{=html}

[Snippet 13.1 provides an implementation in Python of the experiments
conducted in this chapter. Function]{bgcolor="inherit"} `main` [produces
a Cartesian product of parameters]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00707.jpg){width="95" height="21"} [, which characterize the
stochastic process from equation (]{bgcolor="inherit"}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}13.5`</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000628479)
[). Without loss of generality, in all simulations we have used σ = 1.
Then, for each pair]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00799.jpg){width="95"
height="21"} [, function]{bgcolor="inherit"} `batch` [computes the
Sharpe ratios associated with various trading rules.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 13.1 PYTHON CODE FOR THE DETERMINATION OF OPTIMAL TRADING
> RULES**

> ![](Image00033.jpg){width="809" height="318"}

[Snippet 13.2 computes a 20 × 20 mesh of Sharpe ratios, one for each
trading rule]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00126.jpg){width="43"
height="18"} [, given a pair of parameters]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00217.jpg){width="95" height="21"} [. There is a vertical
barrier, as the maximum holding period is set at 100
(]{bgcolor="inherit"} `maxHP = 100` [). We have
fixed]{bgcolor="inherit"} *P
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[= 0, since it is the distance]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00309.jpg){width="141" height="21"} [in equation
(]{bgcolor="inherit"}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}13.5`</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000628479)
[) that drives the convergence, not particular absolute price levels.
Once the first out of three barriers is touched, the exit price is
stored, and the next iteration starts. After all iterations are
completed (1E5), the Sharpe ratio can be computed for that
pair]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00131.jpg){width="43" height="18"} [,
and the algorithm moves to the next pair. When all pairs of trading
rules have been processed, results are reported back
to]{bgcolor="inherit"} `main` [. This algorithm can be parallelized,
similar to what we did for the triple-barrier method in Chapter 3. We
leave that task as an exercise.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **13.6 Experimental Results** `</font>`{=html}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000636644}

[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Table
13.1`</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000637238) [lists the
combinations analyzed in this study. Although different values for these
input parameters would render different numerical results, the
combinations applied allow us to analyze the most general cases. Column
"Forecast" refers to]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00632.jpg){width="65"
height="21"} [; column "Half-Life" refers to τ; column "Sigma" refers to
σ; column "maxHP" stands for maximum holding period.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000637238}

[In the following figures, we have plotted the non-annualized Sharpe
ratios that result from various combinations of profit-taking and
stop-loss exit conditions. We have omitted the negative sign in the
y-axis (stop-losses) for simplicity. Sharpe ratios are represented in
grayscale (lighter indicating better performance; darker indicating
worse performance), in a format known as a heat-map. Performance
(]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00652.jpg){width="30" height="17"} [) is
computed per unit held (]{bgcolor="inherit"} *m
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [= 1),
since other values of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *m
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [would
simply re-scale performance, with no impact on the Sharpe ratio.
Transaction costs can be easily added, but for educational purposes it
is better to plot results without them, so that you can appreciate the
symmetry of the functions.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **13.6.1 Cases with Zero Long-Run Equilibrium**
`</font>`{=html}

[Cases with zero long-run equilibrium are consistent with the business
of market-makers, who provide liquidity under the assumption that price
deviations from current levels will correct themselves over time. The
smaller τ, the smaller is the autoregressive coefficient
(]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00726.jpg){width="71" height="27"} [). A
small autoregressive coefficient in conjunction with a zero expected
profit has the effect that most of the pairs]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00817.jpg){width="51" height="25"} [deliver a zero
performance.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000654709}

[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
13.1`</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000656032) [shows the
heat-map for the parameter combination]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00057.jpg){width="206" height="22"} [. The half-life is so
small that performance is maximized in a narrow range of combinations of
small profit-taking with large stop-losses. In other words, the optimal
trading rule is to hold an inventory long enough until a small profit
arises, even at the expense of experiencing some 5-fold or 7-fold
unrealized losses. Sharpe ratios are high, reaching levels of around
3.2. This is in fact what many market-makers do in practice, and is
consistent with the "asymmetric payoff dilemma" described in Easley et
al. \[2011\]. The worst possible trading rule in this setting would be
to combine a short stop-loss with a large profit-taking threshold, a
situation that market-makers avoid in practice. Performance is closest
to neutral in the diagonal of the mesh, where profit-taking and
stop-losses are symmetric. You should keep this result in mind when
labeling observations using the triple-barrier method (Chapter
3).]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00149.jpg){width="629" height="480"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000656032}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
13.1`</font>`{=html}**](#text00002.html#filepos0000654709) Heat-map for
![](Image00243.jpg){width="206" height="22"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000656231}

[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
13.2`</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000656993) [shows that, if
we increase τ from 5 to 10, the areas of highest and lowest performance
spread over the mesh of pairs]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00333.jpg){width="43" height="18"} [, while the Sharpe ratios
decrease. This is because, as the half-life increases, so does the
magnitude of the autoregressive coefficient (recall
that]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00689.jpg){width="71" height="27"} [),
thus bringing the process closer to a random walk.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00778.jpg){width="640" height="480"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000656993}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
13.2`</font>`{=html}**](#text00002.html#filepos0000656231) Heat-map for
![](Image00015.jpg){width="216" height="21"}

[In]{bgcolor="inherit"} []{#text00002.html#filepos0000657254}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
13.3`</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000657941) [, τ = 25,
which again spreads the areas of highest and lowest performance while
reducing the Sharpe ratio.]{bgcolor="inherit"}
[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000657466}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
13.4`</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000658257) [(τ = 50)
and]{bgcolor="inherit"} []{#text00002.html#filepos0000657585}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
13.5`</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000658573) [(τ = 100)
continue that progression. Eventually, as φ → 1, there are no
recognizable areas where performance can be
maximized.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00108.jpg){width="640" height="480"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000657941}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
13.3`</font>`{=html}**](#text00002.html#filepos0000657254) Heat-map for
![](Image00198.jpg){width="216" height="22"}

![](Image00290.jpg){width="640" height="472"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000658257}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
13.4`</font>`{=html}**](#text00002.html#filepos0000657466) Heat-map for
![](Image00384.jpg){width="216" height="22"}

![](Image00454.jpg){width="640" height="472"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000658573}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
13.5`</font>`{=html}**](#text00002.html#filepos0000657585) Heat-map for
![](Image00516.jpg){width="226" height="21"}

[Calibrating a trading rule on a random walk through historical
simulations would lead to backtest overfitting, because one random
combination of profit-taking and stop-loss that happened to maximize
Sharpe ratio would be selected. This is why backtesting of synthetic
data is so important: to avoid choosing a strategy because some
statistical fluke took place in the past (a single random path). Our
procedure prevents overfitting by recognizing that performance exhibits
no consistent pattern, indicating that there is no optimal trading
rule.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **13.6.2 Cases with Positive Long-Run
Equilibrium** `</font>`{=html}

[Cases with positive long-run equilibrium are consistent with the
business of a position-taker, such as a hedge-fund or asset
manager.]{bgcolor="inherit"} []{#text00002.html#filepos0000659701}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
13.6`</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000660487) [shows the
results for the parameter combination]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00636.jpg){width="206" height="22"} [. Because positions tend
to make money, the optimal profit-taking is higher than in the previous
cases, centered around 6, with stop-losses that range between 4 and 10.
The region of the optimal trading rule takes a characteristic
rectangular shape, as a result of combining a wide stop-loss range with
a narrower profit-taking range. Performance is highest across all
experiments, with Sharpe ratios of around 12.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00710.jpg){width="628" height="480"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000660487}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
13.6`</font>`{=html}**](#text00002.html#filepos0000659701) Heat-map for
![](Image00802.jpg){width="206" height="22"}

[In]{bgcolor="inherit"} []{#text00002.html#filepos0000660748}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
13.7`</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000661506) [, we have
increased the half-life from τ = 5 to τ = 10]{bgcolor="inherit"} *.*
[Now the optimal performance is achieved at a profit-taking centered
around 5, with stop-losses that range between 7 and 10. The range of
optimal profit-taking is wider, while the range of optimal stop-losses
narrows, shaping the former rectangular area closer to a square. Again,
a larger half-life brings the process closer to a random walk, and
therefore performance is now relatively lower than before, with Sharpe
ratios of around 9.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00573.jpg){width="620" height="480"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000661506}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
13.7`</font>`{=html}**](#text00002.html#filepos0000660748) Heat-map for
![](Image00129.jpg){width="216" height="22"}

[In]{bgcolor="inherit"} []{#text00002.html#filepos0000661767}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
13.8`</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000662374) [, we have made
τ = 25]{bgcolor="inherit"} *.* [The optimal profit-taking is now
centered around 3, while the optimal stop-losses range between 9 and 10.
The previous squared area of optimal performance has given way to a
semi-circle of small profit-taking with large stop-loss thresholds.
Again we see a deterioration of performance, with Sharpe ratios of
2.7.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00222.jpg){width="640" height="480"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000662374}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
13.8`</font>`{=html}**](#text00002.html#filepos0000661767) Heat-map for
![](Image00192.jpg){width="216" height="22"}

[In]{bgcolor="inherit"} []{#text00002.html#filepos0000662635}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
13.9`</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000663316) [, the
half-life is raised to τ = 50]{bgcolor="inherit"} *.* [As a result, the
region of optimal performance spreads, while Sharpe ratios continue to
fall to 0.8. This is the same effect we observed in the case of zero
long-run equilibrium (Section 13.6.1), with the difference that because
now]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00404.jpg){width="99" height="21"} [,
there is no symmetric area of worst performance.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00472.jpg){width="634" height="480"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000663316}

13.9`</font>`{=html}**](#text00002.html#filepos0000662635) Heat-map for
![](Image00553.jpg){width="216" height="22"}

appreciate that τ = 100 leads to the natural conclusion of the trend

`<font size="4">`{=html} **13.6.3 Cases with Negative Long-Run
Equilibrium** `</font>`{=html}

[A rational market participant would not initiate a position under the
assumption that a loss is the expected outcome. However, if a trader
recognizes that losses are the expected outcome of a pre-existing
position, she still needs a strategy to stop-out that position while
minimizing such losses.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[We have obtained]{bgcolor="inherit"}
[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000666628}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
13.16`</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000668111) [as a result
of applying parameters]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00086.jpg){width="220" height="22"} [. If we
compare]{bgcolor="inherit"} [`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
13.16`</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000668111)
[with]{bgcolor="inherit"} [`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
13.6`</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000660487) [, it appears
as if one is a rotated complementary of the other.]{bgcolor="inherit"}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
13.6`</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000660487) [resembles a
rotated photographic negative of]{bgcolor="inherit"}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
13.16`</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000668111) [. The reason
is that the profit in]{bgcolor="inherit"}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
13.6`</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000660487) [is translated
into a loss in]{bgcolor="inherit"}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
13.16`</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000668111) [, and the
loss in]{bgcolor="inherit"} [`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
13.6`</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000660487) [is translated
into a profit in]{bgcolor="inherit"}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
13.16`</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000668111) [. One case is
a reverse image of the other, just as a gambler\'s loss is the house\'s
gain.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00785.jpg){width="637" height="480"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000668111}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
13.16`</font>`{=html}**](#text00002.html#filepos0000666628) Heat-map for
![](Image00270.jpg){width="220" height="22"}

[As expected, Sharpe ratios are negative, with a worst performance
region centered around the stop-loss of 6, and profit-taking thresholds
that range between 4 and 10. Now the rectangular shape does not
correspond to a region of best performance, but to a region of worst
performance, with Sharpe ratios of around −12.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[In]{bgcolor="inherit"} []{#text00002.html#filepos0000668755}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
13.17`</font>`{=html}](#text00002.html#filepos0000669207) [, τ = 10, and
now the proximity to a random walk plays in our favor. The region of
worst performance spreads out, and the rectangular area becomes a
square. Performance becomes less negative, with Sharpe ratios of about
−9.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00362.jpg){width="629" height="480"}

[]{#text00002.html#filepos0000669207}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
13.17`</font>`{=html}**](#text00002.html#filepos0000668755) Heat-map for
![](Image00713.jpg){width="229" height="22"}
