`<font size="4">`{=html} **16.4.2 Quasi-Diagonalization**
`</font>`{=html}

[This stage reorganizes the rows and columns of the covariance matrix,
so that the largest values lie along the diagonal. This
quasi-diagonalization of the covariance matrix (without requiring a
change of basis) renders a useful property: Similar investments are
placed together, and dissimilar investments are placed far apart
(see]{bgcolor="inherit"} []{#text00003.html#filepos0000800129}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figures
16.5`</font>`{=html}](#text00003.html#filepos0000809164)
[and]{bgcolor="inherit"} []{#text00003.html#filepos0000800239}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}16.6`</font>`{=html}](#text00003.html#filepos0000809811)
[for an example). The algorithm works as follows: We know that each row
of the linkage matrix merges two branches into one. We replace clusters
in (]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *N*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}−\ 1,\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *N*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}−\ 1,\ 2`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[) with their constituents recursively, until no clusters remain. These
replacements preserve the order of the clustering. The output is a
sorted list of original (unclustered) items. This logic is implemented
in Snippet 16.2.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 16.2 QUASI-DIAGONALIZATION**

> ![](Image00390.jpg){width="753" height="399"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **16.4.3 Recursive Bisection** `</font>`{=html}

[Stage 2 has delivered a quasi-diagonal matrix. The inverse-variance
allocation is optimal for a diagonal covariance matrix (see Appendix
16.A.2 for a proof). We can take advantage of these facts in two
different ways: (1) bottom-up, to define the variance of a contiguous
subset as the variance of an inverse-variance allocation; or (2)
top-down, to split allocations between adjacent subsets in inverse
proportion to their aggregated variances. The following algorithm
formalizes this idea:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

1.  The algorithm is initialized by:
    1.  setting the list of items: *L* = {*L
        ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* }, with
        *L ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* = {*n*
        }
        ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *n*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}=\ 1,\ ...,`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *N*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
    2.  assigning a unit weight to all items: *w
        ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *n*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* =
        1, ∀*n* = 1, ..., *N*
2.  If \|*L ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
    \| = 1,  ∀*L
    ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* ∈ *L* ,
    then stop.
3.  For each *L
    ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* ∈ *L*
    such that \|*L
    ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* \| \>
    1:
    1.  bisect *L
        ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
        into two subsets, *L
        ^[`<font size="2">`{=html}(1)`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
        ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* ∪*L
        ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
        ^[`<font size="2">`{=html}(2)`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^* = *L
        ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* ,
        where ![](Image00268.jpg){width="136" height="29"} , and the
        order is preserved
    2.  define the variance of *L
        ^[`<font size="2">`{=html}(`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html})`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
        ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
        , *j* = 1, 2, as the quadratic form
        ![](Image00359.jpg){width="137" height="30"} , where *V
        ^[`<font size="2">`{=html}(`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html})`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
        ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* is
        the covariance matrix between the constituents of the *L
        ^[`<font size="2">`{=html}(`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html})`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
        ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
        bisection, and ![](Image00440.jpg){width="237" height="43"} ,
        where diag\[.\] and tr\[.\] are the diagonal and trace operators
    3.  compute the split factor: ![](Image00503.jpg){width="132"
        height="47"} , so that 0 ≤ α
        ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ ≤ 1
    4.  re-scale allocations *w
        ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *n*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* by
        a factor of α
        ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ ,
        ∀*n* ∈ *L
        ^[`<font size="2">`{=html}(1)`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
        ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
    5.  re-scale allocations *w
        ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *n*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* by
        a factor of (1 − α
        ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ ),
        ∀*n* ∈ *L
        ^[`<font size="2">`{=html}(2)`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
        ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
4.  Loop to step 2

[Step 3b takes advantage of the quasi-diagonalization bottom-up, because
it defines the variance of the partition]{bgcolor="inherit"} *L
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}(`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html})`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [using
inverse-variance weightings]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00607.jpg){width="27" height="28"} [. Step 3c takes advantage
of the quasi-diagonalization top-down, because it splits the weight in
inverse proportion to the cluster\'s variance. This algorithm guarantees
that 0 ≤]{bgcolor="inherit"} *w
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [≤ 1,
∀]{bgcolor="inherit"} *i* [= 1, ...,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [,
and]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00688.jpg){width="93" height="25"} [,
because at each iteration we are splitting the weights received from
higher hierarchical levels. Constraints can be easily introduced in this
stage, by replacing the equations in steps 3c, 3d, and 3e according to
the user\'s preferences. Stage 3 is implemented in Snippet
16.3.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 16.3 RECURSIVE BISECTION**

> ![](Image00777.jpg){width="787" height="424"}

[This concludes a first description of the HRP algorithm, which solves
the allocation problem in best-case deterministic logarithmic
time,]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00460.jpg){width="153" height="24"}
[, and worst-case deterministic linear time,]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00049.jpg){width="101" height="17"} [. Next, we will put to
practice what we have learned, and evaluate the method\'s accuracy
out-of-sample.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000807661}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **16.5 A Numerical Example** `</font>`{=html}

[We begin by simulating a matrix of observations]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X*
[, of order (10000]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x* [10). The correlation matrix
is visualized in]{bgcolor="inherit"}
[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000808006}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
16.4`</font>`{=html}](#text00003.html#filepos0000808681) [as a
heatmap.]{bgcolor="inherit"} [`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
16.5`</font>`{=html}](#text00003.html#filepos0000809164) [displays the
dendogram of the resulting clusters (stage 1).]{bgcolor="inherit"}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
16.6`</font>`{=html}](#text00003.html#filepos0000809811) [shows the same
correlation matrix, reorganized in blocks according to the identified
clusters (stage 2). Appendix 16.A.3 provides the code used to generate
this numerical example.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00196.jpg){width="594" height="480"}

[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000808681}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
16.4`</font>`{=html}**](#text00003.html#filepos0000808006) Heat-map of
original covariance matrix\
\
This correlation matrix has been computed using function `generateData`
from snippet 16.4 (see Section 16.A.3). The last five columns are
partially correlated to some of the first five series.

![](Image00452.jpg){width="620" height="480"}

[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000809164}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
16.5`</font>`{=html}**](#text00003.html#filepos0000800129) Dendogram of
cluster formation\
\
The clustering procedure has correctly identified that series 9 and 10
were perturbations of series 2, hence (9, 2, 10) are clustered together.
Similarly, 7 is a perturbation of 1, 6 is a perturbation of 3, and 8 is
a perturbation of 5. The only original item that was not perturbated is
4, and that is the one item for which the clustering algorithm found no
similarity.

![](Image00383.jpg){width="594" height="480"}

[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000809811}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
16.6`</font>`{=html}**](#text00003.html#filepos0000800239) Clustered
covariance matrix\
\
Stage 2 quasi-diagonalizes the correlation matrix, in the sense that the
largest values lie along the diagonal. However, unlike PCA or similar
procedures, HRP does not require a change of basis. HRP solves the
allocation problem robustly, while working with the original
investments.

[On this random data, we compute HRP\'s allocations (stage 3), and
compare them to the allocations from two competing methodologies: (1)
Quadratic optimization, as represented by CLA\'s minimum-variance
portfolio (the only portfolio of the efficient frontier that does not
depend on returns' means); and (2) traditional risk parity, exemplified
by the Inverse-Variance Portfolio (IVP). See Bailey and López de Prado
\[2013\] for a comprehensive implementation of CLA, and Appendix 16.A.2
for a derivation of IVP. We apply the standard constraints that 0
≤]{bgcolor="inherit"} *w
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [≤ 1
(non-negativity), ∀]{bgcolor="inherit"} *i* [= 1,
...,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [, and]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00453.jpg){width="93" height="25"} [(full investment).
Incidentally, the condition number for the covariance matrix in this
example is only 150.9324, not particularly high and therefore not
unfavorable to CLA.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[From the allocations in]{bgcolor="inherit"}
[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000811448}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Table
16.1`</font>`{=html}](#text00003.html#filepos0000812181) [, we can
appreciate a few stylized features: First, CLA concentrates 92.66% of
the allocation on the top-5 holdings, while HRP concentrates only
62.57%. Second, CLA assigns zero weight to 3 investments (without the 0
≤]{bgcolor="inherit"} *w
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[constraint, the allocation would have been negative). Third, HRP seems
to find a compromise between CLA\'s concentrated solution and
traditional risk parity\'s IVP allocation. The reader can use the code
in Appendix 16.A.3 to verify that these findings generally hold for
alternative random covariance matrices.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000812181}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Table
16.1`</font>`{=html}**](#text00003.html#filepos0000811448) **A
Comparison of Three Allocations**

\
\

  ------------------------------------ --------- --------- ---------
   **[Weight \#]{bgcolor="inherit"}**   **CLA**   **HRP**   **IVP**
         [1]{bgcolor="inherit"}         14.44%     7.00%    10.36%
         [2]{bgcolor="inherit"}         19.93%     7.59%    10.28%
         [3]{bgcolor="inherit"}         19.73%    10.84%    10.36%
         [4]{bgcolor="inherit"}         19.87%    19.03%    10.25%
         [5]{bgcolor="inherit"}         18.68%     9.72%    10.31%
         [6]{bgcolor="inherit"}          0.00%    10.19%     9.74%
         [7]{bgcolor="inherit"}          5.86%     6.62%     9.80%
         [8]{bgcolor="inherit"}          1.49%     9.10%     9.65%
         [9]{bgcolor="inherit"}          0.00%     7.12%     9.64%
        [10]{bgcolor="inherit"}          0.00%    12.79%     9.61%
  ------------------------------------ --------- --------- ---------

`<font size="2">`{=html} [A characteristic outcome of the three methods
studied: CLA concentrates weights on a few investments, hence becoming
exposed to idiosyncratic shocks. IVP evenly spreads weights through all
investments, ignoring the correlation structure. This makes it
vulnerable to systemic shocks. HRP finds a compromise between
diversifying across all investments and diversifying across cluster,
which makes it more resilient against both types of
shocks.]{bgcolor="inherit"} `</font>`{=html}

[What drives CLA\'s extreme concentration is its goal of minimizing the
portfolio\'s risk. And yet both portfolios have a very similar standard
deviation (σ
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *HRP*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ = 0.4640,
σ ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *CLA*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ =
0.4486). So CLA has discarded half of the investment universe in favor
of a minor risk reduction. The reality of course is that CLA\'s
portfolio is deceitfully diversified, because any distress situation
affecting the top-5 allocations will have a much greater negative impact
on CLA\'s than on HRP\'s portfolio.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000818590}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **16.6 Out-of-Sample Monte Carlo Simulations**
`</font>`{=html}

[In our numerical example, CLA\'s portfolio has lower risk than HRP\'s
in-sample. However, the portfolio with minimum variance in-sample is not
necessarily the one with minimum variance out-of-sample. It would be all
too easy for us to pick a particular historical dataset where HRP
outperforms CLA and IVP (see Bailey and López de Prado \[2014\], and
recall our discussion of selection bias in Chapter 11). Instead, in this
section we follow the backtesting paradigm explained in Chapter 13, and
evaluate via Monte Carlo the performance out-of-sample of HRP against
CLA\'s minimum-variance and traditional risk parity\'s IVP allocations.
This will also help us understand what features make a method preferable
to the rest, regardless of anecdotal
counter-examples.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[First, we generate 10 series of random Gaussian returns (520
observations, equivalent to 2 years of daily history), with 0 mean and
an arbitrary standard deviation of 10%. Real prices exhibit frequent
jumps (Merton \[1976\]) and returns are not cross-sectionally
independent, so we must add random shocks and a random correlation
structure to our generated data. Second, we compute HRP, CLA, and IVP
portfolios by looking back at 260 observations (a year of daily
history). These portfolios are re-estimated and rebalanced every 22
observations (equivalent to a monthly frequency). Third, we compute the
out-of-sample returns associated with those three portfolios. This
procedure is repeated 10,000 times.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[All mean portfolio returns out-of-sample are essentially 0, as
expected. The critical difference comes from the variance of the
out-of-sample portfolio returns: σ
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}2`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *CLA*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ = 0.1157,
σ ^[`<font size="2">`{=html}2`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *IVP*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ = 0.0928,
and σ ^[`<font size="2">`{=html}2`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *HRP*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ =
0.0671]{bgcolor="inherit"} *.* [Although CLA\'s goal is to deliver the
lowest variance (that is the objective of its optimization program), its
performance happens to exhibit the highest variance out-of-sample, and
72.47% greater variance than HRP\'s. This experimental finding is
consistent with the historical evidence in De Miguel et al. \[2009\]. In
other words, HRP would improve the out-of-sample Sharpe ratio of a CLA
strategy by about 31.3%, a rather significant boost. Assuming that the
covariance matrix is diagonal brings some stability to the IVP; however,
its variance is still 38.24% greater than HRP\'s. This variance
reduction out-of-sample is critically important to risk parity
investors, given their use of substantial leverage. See Bailey et al.
\[2014\] for a broader discussion of in-sample vs. out-of-sample
performance.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[The mathematical proof for HRP\'s outperformance over Markowitz\'s CLA
and traditional risk parity\'s IVP is somewhat involved and beyond the
scope of this chapter. In intuitive terms, we can understand the above
empirical results as follows: Shocks affecting a specific investment
penalize CLA\'s concentration. Shocks involving several correlated
investments penalize IVP\'s ignorance of the correlation structure. HRP
provides better protection against both common and idiosyncratic shocks
by finding a compromise between diversification across all investments
and diversification across clusters of investments at multiple
hierarchical levels.]{bgcolor="inherit"}
[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000822428}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
16.7`</font>`{=html}](#text00003.html#filepos0000822841) [plots the time
series of allocations for the first of the 10,000
runs.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00067.jpg){width="367" height="600"}

![](Image00039.jpg){width="400" height="324"}

[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000822841}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
16.7`</font>`{=html}**](#text00003.html#filepos0000822428) (a) Time
series of allocations for IVP.\
\
Between the first and second rebalance, one investment receives an
idiosyncratic shock, which increases its variance. IVP\'s response is to
reduce the allocation to that investment, and spread that former
exposure across all other investments. Between the fifth and sixth
rebalance, two investments are affected by a common shock. IVP\'s
response is the same. As a result, allocations among the seven
unaffected investments grow over time, regardless of their correlation.\
\
(b) Time series of allocations for HRP\
\
HRP\'s response to the idiosyncratic shock is to reduce the allocation
to the affected investment, and use that reduced amount to increase the
allocation to a correlated investment that was unaffected. As a response
to the common shock, HRP reduces allocation to the affected investments
and increases allocation to uncorrelated ones (with lower variance).\
\
(c) Time series of allocations for CLA\
\
CLA allocations respond erratically to idiosyncratic and common shocks.
If we had taken into account rebalancing costs, CLA\'s performance would
have been very negative.

[Appendix 16.A.4 provides the Python code that implements the above
study. The reader can experiment with different parameter configurations
and reach similar conclusions. In particular, HRP\'s out-of-sample
outperformance becomes even more substantial for larger investment
universes, or when more shocks are added, or a stronger correlation
structure is considered, or rebalancing costs are taken into account.
Each of these CLA rebalances incurs transaction costs that can
accumulate into prohibitive losses over time.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000824738}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **16.7 Further Research** `</font>`{=html}

[The methodology introduced in this chapter is flexible, scalable and
admits multiple variations of the same ideas. Using the code provided,
readers can research and evaluate what HRP configurations work best for
their particular problem. For example, at stage 1 they can apply
alternative definitions of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *d
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *j*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00088.jpg){width="22" height="25"}
[and]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00508.jpg){width="25" height="23"} [,
or different clustering algorithms, like biclustering; at stage 3, they
can use different functions for]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00118.jpg){width="24" height="18"} [and α, or alternative
allocation constraints. Instead of carrying out a recursive bisection,
stage 3 could also split allocations top-down using the clusters from
stage 1.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[It is relatively straightforward to incorporate forecasted returns,
Ledoit-Wolf shrinkage, and Black-Litterman--style views to this
hierarchical approach. In fact, the inquisitive reader may have realized
that, at its core, HRP is essentially a robust procedure to avoid matrix
inversions, and the same ideas underlying HRP can be used to replace
many econometric regression methods, notorious for their unstable
outputs (like VAR or VECM).]{bgcolor="inherit"}
[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000826427}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
16.8`</font>`{=html}](#text00003.html#filepos0000827020) [displays (a) a
large correlation matrix of fixed income securities before and (b) after
clustering, with over 2.1 million entries. Traditional optimization or
econometric methods fail to recognize the hierarchical structure of
financial Big Data, where the numerical instabilities defeat the
benefits of the analysis, resulting in unreliable and detrimental
outcomes.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00278.jpg){width="356" height="600"}

[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000827020}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
16.8`</font>`{=html}**](#text00003.html#filepos0000826427) Correlation
matrix before and after clustering\
\
The methodology described in this chapter can be applied to problems
beyond optimization. For example, a PCA analysis of a large fixed income
universe suffers the same drawbacks we described for CLA. Small-data
techniques developed decades and centuries ago (factor models,
regression analysis, econometrics) fail to recognize the hierarchical
nature of financial big data.

[Kolanovic et al. \[2017\] conducted a lengthy study of HRP, concluding
that "HRP delivers superior risk-adjusted returns. Whilst both the HRP
and the MV portfolios deliver the highest returns, the HRP portfolios
match with volatility targets much better than MV portfolios. We also
run simulation studies to confirm the robustness of our findings, in
which HRP consistently deliver a superior performance over MV and other
risk-based strategies \[...\] HRP portfolios are truly diversified with
a higher number of uncorrelated exposures, and less extreme weights and
risk allocations."]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Raffinot \[2017\] concludes that "empirical results indicate that
hierarchical clustering based portfolios are robust, truly diversified
and achieve statistically better risk-adjusted performances than
commonly used portfolio optimization techniques."]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000828570}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **16.8 Conclusion** `</font>`{=html}

[Exact analytical solutions can perform much worse than approximate ML
solutions. Although mathematically correct, quadratic optimizers in
general, and Markowitz\'s CLA in particular, are known to deliver
generally unreliable solutions due to their instability, concentration,
and underperformance. The root cause for these issues is that quadratic
optimizers require the inversion of a covariance matrix. Markowitz\'s
curse is that the more correlated investments are, the greater is the
need for a diversified portfolio, and yet the greater are that
portfolio\'s estimation errors.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[In this chapter, we have exposed a major source of quadratic
optimizers' instability: A matrix of size]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [is
associated with a complete graph with]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00136.jpg){width="82" height="29"} [edges. With so many edges
connecting the nodes of the graph, weights are allowed to rebalance with
complete freedom. This lack of hierarchical structure means that small
estimation errors will lead to entirely different solutions. HRP
replaces the covariance structure with a tree structure, accomplishing
three goals: (1) Unlike traditional risk parity methods, it fully
utilizes the information contained in the covariance matrix, (2)
weights' stability is recovered and (3) the solution is intuitive by
construction. The algorithm converges in deterministic logarithmic (best
case) or linear (worst case) time.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[HRP is robust, visual, and flexible, allowing the user to introduce
constraints or manipulate the tree structure without compromising the
algorithm\'s search. These properties are derived from the fact that HRP
does not require covariance invertibility. Indeed, HRP can compute a
portfolio on an ill-degenerated or even a singular covariance
matrix.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[This chapter focuses on a portfolio construction application; however,
the reader will find other practical uses for making decisions under
uncertainty, particularly in the presence of a nearly singular
covariance matrix: capital allocation to portfolio managers, allocations
across algorithmic strategies, bagging and boosting of machine learning
signals, forecasts from random forests, replacement to unstable
econometric models (VAR, VECM), etc.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Of course, quadratic optimizers like CLA produce the minimum-variance
portfolio in-sample (that is its objective function). Monte Carlo
experiments show that HRP delivers lower out-of-sample variance than CLA
or traditional risk parity methods (IVP). Since Bridgewater pioneered
risk parity in the 1990s, some of the largest asset managers have
launched funds that follow this approach, for combined assets in excess
of \$500 billion. Given their extensive use of leverage, these funds
should benefit from adopting a more stable risk parity allocation
method, thus achieving superior risk-adjusted returns and lower
rebalance costs.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000831910}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **APPENDICES** `</font>`{=html}

[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000832025}

[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000836832}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **16.A.3 Reproducing the Numerical Example**
`</font>`{=html}

[Snippet 16.4 can be used to reproduce our results and simulate
additional numerical examples. Function]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`generateData` [produces a matrix of time series where a
number]{bgcolor="inherit"} `size0` [of vectors are uncorrelated, and a
number]{bgcolor="inherit"} `size1` [of vectors are correlated. The
reader can change the]{bgcolor="inherit"} `np.random.seed`
[in]{bgcolor="inherit"} `generateData` [to run alternative examples and
gain an intuition of how HRP works. Scipy\'s
function]{bgcolor="inherit"} `linkage` [can be used to perform stage 1
(Section 16.4.1), function]{bgcolor="inherit"} `getQuasiDiag` [performs
stage 2 (Section 16.4.2), and function]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`getRecBipart` [carries out stage 3 (Section
16.4.3).]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **16.A.4 Reproducing the Monte Carlo
Experiment** `</font>`{=html}

[Snippet 16.5 implements Monte Carlo experiments on three allocation
methods: HRP, CLA, and IVP. All libraries are standard except for HRP,
which is provided in Appendix 16.A.3, and CLA, which can be found in
Bailey and López de Prado \[2013\]. The subroutine]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`generateData` [simulates the correlated data, with two types of random
shocks: common to various investments and specific to a single
investment. There are two shocks of each type, one positive and one
negative. The variables for the experiments are set as arguments
of]{bgcolor="inherit"} `hrpMC` [. They were chosen arbitrarily, and the
user can experiment with alternative combinations.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    Breaks`</font>`{=html}](#text00003.html#filepos0000850574)

[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000850574}

`<font size="5">`{=html} **CHAPTER 17** `</font>`{=html}\
\
`<font size="5">`{=html} **Structural Breaks** `</font>`{=html}

[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000850809}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **17.1 Motivation** `</font>`{=html}

[In developing an ML-based investment strategy, we typically wish to bet
when there is a confluence of factors whose predicted outcome offers a
favorable risk-adjusted return. Structural breaks, like the transition
from one market regime to another, is one example of such a confluence
that is of particular interest. For instance, a mean-reverting pattern
may give way to a momentum pattern. As this transition takes place, most
market participants are caught off guard, and they will make costly
mistakes. This sort of errors is the basis for many profitable
strategies, because the actors on the losing side will typically become
aware of their mistake once it is too late. Before they accept their
losses, they will act irrationally, try to hold the position, and hope
for a comeback. Sometimes they will even increase a losing position, in
desperation. Eventually they will be forced to stop loss or stop out.
Structural breaks offer some of the best risk/rewards. In this chapter,
we will review some methods that measure the likelihood of structural
breaks, so that informative features can be built upon
them.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000852108}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **17.2 Types of Structural Break Tests**
`</font>`{=html}

[We can classify structural break tests in two general
categories:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

-   **CUSUM tests:** These test whether the cumulative forecasting
    errors significantly deviate from white noise.
-   **Explosiveness tests:** Beyond deviation from white noise, these
    test whether the process exhibits exponential growth or collapse, as
    this is inconsistent with a random walk or stationary process, and
    it is unsustainable in the long run.
    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    -   **Right-tail unit-root tests:** These tests evaluate the
        presence of exponential growth or collapse, while assuming an
        autoregressive specification.
    -   **Sub/super-martingale tests:** These tests evaluate the
        presence of exponential growth or collapse under a variety of
        functional forms.

[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000853372}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **17.3 CUSUM Tests** `</font>`{=html}

[In Chapter 2 we introduced the CUSUM filter, which we applied in the
context of event-based sampling of bars. The idea was to sample a bar
whenever some variable, like cumulative prediction errors, exceeded a
predefined threshold. This concept can be further extended to test for
structural breaks.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **17.3.1 Brown-Durbin-Evans CUSUM Test on
Recursive Residuals** `</font>`{=html}

[This test was proposed by Brown, Durbin and Evans \[1975\]. Let us
assume that at every observation]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [= 1,
...,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [, we count with an array of
features]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [predictive
of a value]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [.
Matrix]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [is
composed of the time series of features]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t*
[≤]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [, {]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [}
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}=\ 1,\ ...,`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
. These authors propose that we compute recursive least squares (RLS)
estimates of β, based on the specification]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00766.jpg){width="105" height="26"}

[which is fit on subsamples (\[1,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [+ 1\],
\[1,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [+ 2\], ..., \[1,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T*
[\]), giving]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [−]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [least
squares estimates]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00004.jpg){width="104"
height="23"} [. We can compute the standardized 1-step ahead recursive
residuals as]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00097.jpg){width="127" height="61"}

![](Image00185.jpg){width="203" height="38"}

[The CUSUM statistic is defined as]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00280.jpg){width="105" height="58"}

![](Image00241.jpg){width="227" height="56"}

[Under the null hypothesis that β is some constant
value,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *H
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [: β
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ = β,
then]{bgcolor="inherit"} *S
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[∼]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [\[0,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t*
[−]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [− 1\]. One caveat of this procedure is that
the starting point is chosen arbitrarily, and results may be
inconsistent due to that.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

Levels** `</font>`{=html}

[This test follows Homm and Breitung \[2012\]. It simplifies the

is, we forecast no change (E
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}−\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
\[Δ]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [\] = 0).
This will allow us to work directly with]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [levels,
hence reducing the computational burden. We compute the standardized

~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ = 0,
then]{bgcolor="inherit"} *S

`<font size="4">`{=html} **17.4 Explosiveness Tests** `</font>`{=html}

[Explosiveness tests can be generally divided between those that test
for one bubble and those that test for multiple bubbles. In this
context, bubbles are not limited to price rallies, but they also include
sell-offs. Tests that allow for multiple bubbles are more robust in the
sense that a cycle of bubble-burst-bubble will make the series appear to
be stationary to single-bubble tests. Maddala and Kim \[1998\], and
Breitung \[2014\] offer good overviews of the
literature.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **17.4.1 Chow-Type Dickey-Fuller Test**
`</font>`{=html}

[A family of explosiveness tests was inspired by the work of Gregory
Chow, starting with Chow \[1960\]. Consider the first order
autoregressive process]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00345.jpg){width="115" height="16"}

[where ϵ ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ is
white noise. The null hypothesis is that]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [follows a
random walk,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *H
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [: ρ = 1, and the
alternative hypothesis is that]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [starts as
a random walk but changes at time τ\*]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [, where
τ\* ∈ (0, 1), into an explosive process:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00276.jpg){width="442" height="91"}

[At time]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [we can test for a switch (from random
walk to explosive process) having taken place at time
τ\*]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [(break date). In order to test this
hypothesis, we fit the following specification,]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00697.jpg){width="179" height="20"}

[where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *D
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [\[τ\*\] is
a dummy variable that takes zero value if]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [\<
τ\*]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [, and takes the value one
if]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [≥ τ\*]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [. Then, the
null hypothesis]{bgcolor="inherit"} *H
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [: δ = 0 is
tested against the (one-sided) alternative]{bgcolor="inherit"} *H
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [: δ \>
1:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00299.jpg){width="101" height="47"}

[The main drawback of this method is that τ\* is unknown. To address
this issue, Andrews \[1993\] proposed a new test where all possible τ\*
are tried, within some interval τ\* ∈ \[τ
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ , 1 − τ
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ \]. As Breitung
\[2014\] explains, we should leave out some of the possible τ\* at the
beginning and end of the sample, to ensure that either regime is fitted
with enough observations (there must be enough zeros and enough ones
in]{bgcolor="inherit"} *D
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [\[τ\*\]).
The test statistic for an unknown τ\* is the maximum of
all]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [(1 − 2τ
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ ) values
of]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00696.jpg){width="53" height="18"}
[.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00533.jpg){width="226" height="33"}

[Another drawback of Chow\'s approach is that it assumes that there is
only one break date τ\*]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [, and that the bubble
runs up to the end of the sample (there is no switch back to a random
walk). For situations where three or more regimes (random walk → bubble
→ random walk ...) exist, we need to discuss the Supremum Augmented
Dickey-Fuler (SADF) test.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **17.4.2 Supremum Augmented Dickey-Fuller**
`</font>`{=html}

[In the words of Phillips, Wu and Yu \[2011\], "standard unit root and
cointegration tests are inappropriate tools for detecting bubble
behavior because they cannot effectively distinguish between a
stationary process and a periodically collapsing bubble model. Patterns
of periodically collapsing bubbles in the data look more like data
generated from a unit root or stationary autoregression than a
potentially explosive process." To address this flaw, these authors
propose fitting the regression specification]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00740.jpg){width="267" height="56"}

[where we test for]{bgcolor="inherit"} *H
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [: β ≤
0,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *H
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [: β \> 0.
Inspired by Andrews \[1993\], Phillips and Yu \[2011\] and Phillips, Wu
and Yu \[2011\] proposed the Supremum Augmented Dickey-Fuller test
(SADF). SADF fits the above regression at each end
point]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [with backwards expanding start points,
then computes]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00347.jpg){width="386" height="61"}

[where]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00513.jpg){width="29" height="26"}
[is estimated on a sample that starts at]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [and ends
at]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [, τ is the minimum sample length used in the
analysis,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [is the left
bound of the backwards expanding window, and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [=
τ, ...,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [. For the estimation
of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *SADF
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [, the
right side of the window is fixed at]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [. The
standard ADF test is a special case of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *SADF
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [, where τ
=]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [− 1.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[There are two critical differences between]{bgcolor="inherit"} *SADF
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [and SDFC:
First,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *SADF
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [is
computed at each]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [∈ \[τ,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T*
[\], whereas SDFC is computed only at]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [. Second,
instead of introducing a dummy variable, SADF recursively expands the
beginning of the sample (]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [∈
\[1,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [− τ\]). By trying all combinations of a
nested double loop on (]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [), SADF does not assume a known number of
regime switches or break dates.]{bgcolor="inherit"}
[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000868411}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
17.1`</font>`{=html}](#text00003.html#filepos0000869015) [displays the
series of E-mini S&P 500 futures prices after applying the ETF trick
(Chapter 2, Section 2.4.1), as well as the SADF derived from that price
series. The SADF line spikes when prices exhibit a bubble-like behavior,
and returns to low levels when the bubble bursts. In the following
sections, we will discuss some enhancements to Phillips' original SADF
method.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00374.jpg){width="640" height="368"}

[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000869015}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
17.1`</font>`{=html}**](#text00003.html#filepos0000868411) Prices (left
y-axis) and SADF (right y-axis) over time

***17.4.2.1 Raw vs. Log Prices***

[It is common to find in the literature studies that carry out
structural break tests on raw prices. In this section we will explore
why log prices should be preferred, particularly when working with long
time series involving bubbles and bursts.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[For raw prices {]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [}, if
ADF\'s null hypotesis is rejected, it means that prices are stationary,
with finite variance. The implication is that
returns]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00792.jpg){width="58" height="29"}
[are not time invariant, for returns' volatility must decrease as prices
rise and increase as prices fall in order to keep the price variance
constant. When we run ADF on raw prices, we assume that returns'
variance is not invariant to price levels. If returns variance happens
to be invariant to price levels, the model will be structurally
heteroscedastic.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[In contrast, if we work with log prices, the ADF specification will
state that]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00395.jpg){width="156" height="18"}

[Let us make a change of variable,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[=]{bgcolor="inherit"} *ky
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [. Now,
log\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [\] =
log\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [\] + log\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [\], and
the ADF specification will state that]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00101.jpg){width="249" height="18"}

[Under this alternative specification based on log prices, price levels
condition returns' mean, not returns' volatility. The difference may not
matter in practice for small samples, where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [≈
1, but SADF runs regressions across decades and bubbles produce levels
that are significantly different between regimes (]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*k* [≠ 1).]{bgcolor="inherit"}

per ADF estimate. A single SADF update requires]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Consider a dollar bar series on E-mini S&P 500 futures. For
(]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [) = (356631, 3),
an ADF estimate requires 11,412,245 FLOPs, and a SADF update requires
2,034,979,648,799 operations (roughly 2.035 TFLOPs). A full SADF time
series requires 241,910,974,617,448,672 operations (roughly 242 PFLOPs).
This number will increase quickly, as the]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T*
[continues to grow. And this estimate excludes notoriously expensive
operations like alignment, pre-processing of data, I/O jobs, etc.
Needless to say, this algorithm\'s double loop requires a large number
of operations. An HPC cluster running an efficiently parallelized
implementation of the algorithm may be needed to estimate the SADF
series within a reasonable amount of time. Chapter 20 will present some
parallelization strategies useful in these
situations.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

***17.4.2.4 Quantile ADF***

[SADF takes the supremum of a series on t-values,]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00308.jpg){width="242" height="21"} [. Selecting the extreme
value introduces some robustness problems, where SADF estimates could
vary significantly depending on the sampling frequency and the specific
timestamps of the samples. A more robust estimator of ADF extrema would
be the following: First, let]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00446.jpg){width="182" height="21"} [. Second, we
define]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Q
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *q*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[=]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Q* [\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *s
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *q* [\] the]{bgcolor="inherit"} *q* [quantile
of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *s
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [, as a
measure of centrality of high ADF values, where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *q*
[∈ \[0, 1\]. Third, we define]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00455.jpg){width="178" height="24"} [, with 0
\<]{bgcolor="inherit"} *v* [≤ min{]{bgcolor="inherit"} *q* [, 1
−]{bgcolor="inherit"} *q* [}, as a measure of dispersion of high ADF
values. For example, we could set]{bgcolor="inherit"} *q* [= 0.95
and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *v* [= 0.025. Note that SADF is merely a
particular case of QADF, where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *SADF
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[=]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Q
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[and]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00471.jpg){width="39" height="24"} [is
not defined because]{bgcolor="inherit"} *q* [= 1.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

***17.4.2.5 Conditional ADF***

[Alternatively, we can address concerns on SADF robustness by computing
conditional moments. Let]{bgcolor="inherit"} *f* [\[]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*x* [\] be the probability distribution function of]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00828.jpg){width="182" height="21"} [, with]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*x* [∈]{bgcolor="inherit"} *s
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [. Then, we
define]{bgcolor="inherit"} *C
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *q*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[=]{bgcolor="inherit"} *K
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}−\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^* [∫
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}∞`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *Qt*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *q*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
]{bgcolor="inherit"} *xf* [\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x*
[\]]{bgcolor="inherit"} *dx* [as a measure of centrality of high ADF
values, and]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00495.jpg){width="274"
height="37"} [as a measure of dispersion of high ADF values, with
regularization constant]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00511.jpg){width="124" height="30"} [. For example, we could
use]{bgcolor="inherit"} *q* [= 0.95.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[By construction,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *C
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *q*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[≤]{bgcolor="inherit"} *SADF
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [. A
scatter plot of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *SADF
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[against]{bgcolor="inherit"} *C
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *q*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[shows that lower boundary, as an ascending line with approximately unit
gradient (see]{bgcolor="inherit"} []{#text00003.html#filepos0000884641}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
17.2`</font>`{=html}](#text00003.html#filepos0000885567) [). When SADF
grows beyond −1.5, we can appreciate some horizontal trajectories,
consistent with a sudden widening of the right fat tail
in]{bgcolor="inherit"} *s
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [. In other
words,]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00528.jpg){width="154" height="24"}
[can reach significantly large values even if]{bgcolor="inherit"} *C
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html},`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *q*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[is relatively small, because]{bgcolor="inherit"} *SADF
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [is
sensitive to outliers.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00550.jpg){width="558" height="420"}

***17.4.2.6 Implementation of SADF***

[This section presents an implementation of the SADF algorithm. The
purpose of this code is not to estimate SADF quickly, but to clarify the
steps involved in its estimation. Snippet 17.1 lists SADF\'s inner loop.
That is the part that estimates]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00668.jpg){width="193" height="47"} [, which is the
backshifting component of the algorithm. The outer loop (not shown here)
repeats this calculation for an advancing]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [,
{]{bgcolor="inherit"} *SADF
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [}
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}=\ 1,\ ...,`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *T*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
. The arguments are:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

-   `logP` : a pandas series containing log-prices
-   `minSL` : the minimum sample length (τ), used by the final
    regression
-   `constant` : the regression\'s time trend component
    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    -   `'nc'` : no time trend, only a constant
    -   `'ct'` : a constant plus a linear time trend
    -   `'ctt'` : a constant plus a second-degree polynomial time trend
-   `lags` : the number of lags used in the ADF specification

> **SNIPPET 17.1 SADF\'S INNER LOOP**

> ![](Image00693.jpg){width="499" height="172"}

[Snippet 17.2 lists function]{bgcolor="inherit"} `getXY` [, which
prepares the numpy objects needed to conduct the recursive
tests.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 17.2 PREPARING THE DATASETS**

> ![](Image00695.jpg){width="776" height="353"}

[Snippet 17.3 lists function]{bgcolor="inherit"} `lagDF` [, which
applies to a dataframe the lags specified in its
argument]{bgcolor="inherit"} `lags` [.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 17.3 APPLY LAGS TO DATAFRAME**

[Finally, Snippet 17.4 lists function]{bgcolor="inherit"} `getBetas` [,

`<font size="4">`{=html} **17.4.3 Sub- and Super-Martingale Tests**
`</font>`{=html}

[In this section we will introduce explosiveness tests that do not rely
on the standard ADF specification. Consider a process that is either a
sub- or super-martingale. Given some observations {]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*y ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [}, we
would like to test for the existence of an explosive time
trend,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *H
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [: β =
0,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *H
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [: β ≠ 0, under
alternative specifications:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

-   Polynomial trend (SM-Poly1):

    > > ![](Image00746.jpg){width="169" height="23"}

-   Polynomial trend (SM-Poly2):

    > > ![](Image00509.jpg){width="206" height="23"}

-   Exponential trend (SM-Exp):

    > > ![](Image00756.jpg){width="334" height="23"}

-   Power trend (SM-Power):

    > > ![](Image00804.jpg){width="365" height="23"}

[Similar to SADF, we fit any of these specifications to each end
point]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [= τ, ...,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [, with
backwards expanding start points, then compute]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00820.jpg){width="218" height="61"}

[The reason for the absolute value is that we are equally interested in
explosive growth and collapse. In the simple regression case (Greene
\[2008\], p. 48), the variance of β is]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00840.jpg){width="100" height="40"} [,
hence]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00002.jpg){width="131" height="26"}
[. The same result is generalizable to the multivariate linear
regression case (Greene \[2008\], pp. 51--52). The]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00022.jpg){width="18" height="28"} [of a weak long-run bubble
may be smaller than the]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00296.jpg){width="18" height="28"} [of a strong short-run
bubble, hence biasing the method towards long-run bubbles. To correct
for this bias, we can penalize large sample lengths by determining the
coefficient φ ∈ \[0, 1\] that yields best explosiveness
signals.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00059.jpg){width="278" height="61"}

[For instance, when φ = 0.5, we compensate for the
lower]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00076.jpg){width="33" height="25"}
[associated with longer sample lengths, in the simple regression case.
For φ → 0,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *SMT
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [will
exhibit longer trends, as that compensation wanes and long-run bubbles
mask short-run bubbles. For φ → 1,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *SMT
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [becomes
noisier, because more short-run bubbles are selected over long-run
bubbles. Consequently, this is a natural way to adjust the explosiveness
signal, so that it filters opportunities targeting a particular holding
period. The features used by the ML algorithm may
include]{bgcolor="inherit"} *SMT
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [estimated
from a wide range of φ values.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000895025}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **Exercises** `</font>`{=html}

::: {height="1em"}
:::

1.  [On a dollar bar series on E-mini S&P 500
    > > futures,]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  Apply the Brown-Durbin-Evans method. Does it recognize the
        dot-com bubble?
    2.  Apply the Chu-Stinchcombe-White method. Does it find a bubble in
        2007--2008?

2.  [On a dollar bar series on E-mini S&P 500
    > > futures,]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  Compute the *SDFC* (Chow-type) explosiveness test. What break
        date does this method select? Is this what you expected?
    2.  Compute and plot the SADF values for this series. Do you observe
        extreme spikes around the dot-com bubble and before the Great
        Recession? Did the bursts also cause spikes?

3.  [Following on exercise 2,]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  Determine the periods where the series exhibited
        1.  Steady conditions
        2.  Unit-Root conditions
        3.  Explosive conditions
    2.  Compute QADF.
    3.  Compute CADF.

4.  [On a dollar bar series on E-mini S&P 500
    > > futures,]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  Compute SMT for SM-Poly1 and SM-Poly 2, where φ = 1. What is
        their correlation?
    2.  Compute SMT for SM-Exp, where φ = 1 and φ = 0.5. What is their
        correlation?
    3.  Compute SMT for SM-Power, where φ = 1 and φ = 0.5. What is their
        correlation?

5.  [If you compute the reciprocal of each price, the series
    > > {]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
    > > ^[`<font size="2">`{=html}−\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
    > > ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [}
    > > turns bubbles into bursts and bursts into
    > > bubbles.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  Is this transformation needed, to identify bursts?
    2.  What methods in this chapter can identify bursts without
        requiring this transformation?

[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000898694}

    149--192.

[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000901244}

`<font size="5">`{=html} **CHAPTER 18** `</font>`{=html}\
\
`<font size="5">`{=html} **Entropy Features** `</font>`{=html}

[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000901478}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **18.1 Motivation** `</font>`{=html}

[Price series convey information about demand and supply forces. In
perfect markets, prices are unpredictable, because each observation
transmits everything that is known about a product or service. When
markets are not perfect, prices are formed with partial information, and
as some agents know more than others, they can exploit that
informational asymmetry. It would be helpful to estimate the
informational content of price series, and form features on which ML
algorithms can learn the likely outcomes. For example, the ML algorithm
may find that momentum bets are more profitable when prices carry little
information, and that mean-reversion bets are more profitable when
prices carry a lot of information. In this chapter, we will explore ways
to determine the amount of information contained in a price
series.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000902479}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **18.2 Shannon\'s Entropy** `</font>`{=html}

[In this section we will review a few concepts from information theory
that will be useful in the remainder of the chapter. The reader can find
a complete exposition in MacKay \[2003\]. The father of information
theory, Claude Shannon, defined entropy as the average amount of
information (over long messages) produced by a stationary source of
data. It is the smallest number of bits per character required to
describe the message in a uniquely decodable way. Mathematically,
Shannon \[1948\] defined the entropy of a discrete random
variable]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X* [with possible
values]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x* [∈]{bgcolor="inherit"} *A*
[as]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00095.jpg){width="204" height="41"}

[with 0 ≤]{bgcolor="inherit"} *H* [\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X* [\] ≤ log
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}2`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
\[\|\|]{bgcolor="inherit"} *A* [\|\|\] where:]{bgcolor="inherit"} *p*
[\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x* [\] is the probability
of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x* [;]{bgcolor="inherit"} *H*
[\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X* [\] = 0⇔∃]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x*
[\|]{bgcolor="inherit"} *p* [\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x* [\] =
1;]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00641.jpg){width="254" height="32"} [for
all]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x* [; and \|\|]{bgcolor="inherit"} *A* [\|\| is
the size of the set]{bgcolor="inherit"} *A* [. This can be interpreted
as the probability weighted average of informational content
in]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X* [, where the bits of information are measured
as]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00715.jpg){width="60" height="32"} [.
The rationale for measuring information as]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00151.jpg){width="59" height="32"} [comes from the observation
that low-probability outcomes reveal more information than
high-probability outcomes. In other words, we learn when something
unexpected happens. Similarly, redundancy is defined
as]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00045.jpg){width="175" height="44"}

[with 0 ≤]{bgcolor="inherit"} *R* [\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X* [\] ≤ 1.
Kolmogorov \[1965\] formalized the connection between redundancy and
complexity of a Markov information source. The mutual information
between two variables is defined as the Kullback-Leibler divergence from
the joint probability density to the product of the marginal probability
densities.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00184.jpg){width="462" height="47"}

[The mutual information (MI) is always non-negative, symmetric, and
equals zero if and only if]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X*
[and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Y* [are independent. For normally distributed
variables, the mutual information is closely related to the familiar
Pearson correlation, ρ.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00494.jpg){width="211" height="38"}

[Therefore, mutual information is a natural measure of the association
between variables, regardless of whether they are linear or nonlinear in
nature (Hausser and Strimmer \[2009\]). The normalized variation of
information is a metric derived from mutual information. For several
entropy estimators, see:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

-   In R:
    [`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}http://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/entropy/entropy.pdf`</font>`{=html}](http://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/entropy/entropy.pdf)
-   In Python:
    [`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}https://code.google.com/archive/p/pyentropy/`</font>`{=html}](https://code.google.com/archive/p/pyentropy/)

[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000907003}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **18.3 The Plug-in (or Maximum Likelihood)
Estimator** `</font>`{=html}

[In this section we will follow the exposition of entropy\'s maximum
likelihood estimator in Gao et al. \[2008\]. The nomenclature may seem a
bit peculiar at first (no pun intended), but once you become familiar
with it you will find it convenient. Given a data
sequence]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *n*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [, comprising the
string of values starting in position 1 and ending in
position]{bgcolor="inherit"} *n* [, we can form a dictionary of all
words of length]{bgcolor="inherit"} *w* [\<]{bgcolor="inherit"} *n* [in
that sequence,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *A
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *w*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}^* [. Consider
an arbitrary word]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *w*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[∈]{bgcolor="inherit"} *A
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *w*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}^* [of
length]{bgcolor="inherit"} *w* [. We denote]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00591.jpg){width="53" height="22"} [the empirical probability
of the word]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *w*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[in]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *n*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [, which means
that]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00678.jpg){width="53" height="22"} [is
the frequency with which]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *w*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [appears
in]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *n*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [. Assuming that
the data is generated by a stationary and ergodic process, then the law
of large numbers guarantees that, for a fixed]{bgcolor="inherit"} *w*
[and large]{bgcolor="inherit"} *n* [, the empirical
distribution]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00760.jpg){width="21"
height="18"} [will be close to the true distribution]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*p ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *w*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [. Under
these circumstances, a natural estimator for the entropy rate (i.e.,
average entropy per bit) is]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00292.jpg){width="288" height="54"}

[Since the empirical distribution is also the maximum likelihood
estimate of the true distribution, this is also often referred to as the
maximum likelihood entropy estimator. The value]{bgcolor="inherit"} *w*
[should be large enough for]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00093.jpg){width="35" height="24"} [to be acceptably close to
the true entropy]{bgcolor="inherit"} *H.* [The value
of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *n* [needs to be much larger
than]{bgcolor="inherit"} *w* [, so that the empirical distribution of
order]{bgcolor="inherit"} *w* [is close to the true distribution.
Snippet 18.1 implements the plug-in entropy
estimator.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 18.1 PLUG-IN ENTROPY ESTIMATOR**

> ![](Image00181.jpg){width="751" height="538"}

[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000910957}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **18.4 Lempel-Ziv Estimators** `</font>`{=html}

[Entropy can be interpreted as a measure of complexity. A complex
sequence contains more information than a regular (predictable)
sequence. The Lempel-Ziv (LZ) algorithm efficiently decomposes a message
into non-redundant substrings (Ziv and Lempel \[1978\]). We can estimate
the compression rate of a message as a function of the number of items
in a Lempel-Ziv dictionary relative to the length of the message. The
intuition here is that complex messages have high entropy, which will
require large dictionaries relative to the length of the string to be
transmitted. Snippet 18.2 shows an implementation of the LZ compression
algorithm.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 18.2 A LIBRARY BUILT USING THE LZ ALGORITHM**

> ![](Image00734.jpg){width="431" height="260"}

[Kontoyiannis \[1998\] attempts to make a more efficient use of the
information available in a message. What follows is a faithful summary
of the exposition in Gao et al. \[2008\]. We will reproduce the steps in
that paper, while complementing them with code snippets that implement
their ideas. Let us define]{bgcolor="inherit"} *L
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *n*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [as 1 plus
the length of the longest match found in the]{bgcolor="inherit"} *n*
[bits prior to]{bgcolor="inherit"} *i* [,]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00371.jpg){width="503" height="31"}

[Snippet 18.3 implements the algorithm that determines the length of the
longest match. A few notes worth mentioning:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

-   The value *n* is constant for a sliding window, and *n* = *i* for an
    expanding window.
-   Computing *L
    ^[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *n*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
    ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
    requires data *x
    ^[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}+`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *n*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}−\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
    ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}−`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *n*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
    . In other words, index *i* must be at the center of the window.
    This is important in order to guarantee that both matching strings
    are of the same length. If they are not of the same length, *l* will
    have a limited range and its maximum will be underestimated.
-   Some overlap between the two substrings is allowed, although
    obviously both cannot start at *i.*

> **SNIPPET 18.3 FUNCTION THAT COMPUTES THE LENGTH OF THE LONGEST
> MATCH**

> ![](Image00447.jpg){width="578" height="319"}

[Ornstein and Weiss \[1993\] formally established
that]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00514.jpg){width="139" height="49"}

[Kontoyiannis uses this result to estimate Shannon\'s entropy rate. He
estimates the average]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00620.jpg){width="43"
height="36"} [, and uses the reciprocal of that average to
estimate]{bgcolor="inherit"} *H.* [The general intuition is, as we
increase the available history, we expect that messages with high
entropy will produce relatively shorter non-redundant substrings. In
contrast, messages with low entropy will produce relatively longer
non-redundant substrings as we parse through the message. Given a data
realization]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x

[The above expressions have been derived under the assumptions of:
stationarity, ergodicity, that the process takes finitely many values,
and that the process satisfies the Doeblin condition. Intuitively, this
condition requires that, after a finite number of
steps]{bgcolor="inherit"} *r* [, no matter what has occurred before,
anything can happen with positive probability. It turns out that this
Doeblin condition can be avoided altogether if we consider a modified
version of the above estimators:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00457.jpg){width="161" height="140"}

[One practical question when estimating]{bgcolor="inherit"}

window size]{bgcolor="inherit"} *n.* [Gao et al. \[2008\] argue
that]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [+]{bgcolor="inherit"} *n*
[=]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [should be approximately equal to the message
length. Considering that the bias of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *L
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *n*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [is of
order]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00645.jpg){width="95" height="31"}
[and the variance of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *L
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *n*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [is
order]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00718.jpg){width="48" height="26"} [,
the bias/variance trade-off is balanced at around]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00069.jpg){width="144" height="29"} [. That
is,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *n* [could be chosen such
that]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [≈]{bgcolor="inherit"} *n* [+ (log 
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}2`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *n* [\])
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}2`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^ . For example,
for]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [= 2
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}8`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^ , a balanced
bias/variance window size would be]{bgcolor="inherit"} *n* [≈ 198, in
which case]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k* [≈ 58.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Kontoyiannis \[1998\] proved that]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00047.jpg){width="39" height="20"} [converges to Shannon\'s
entropy rate with probability 1 as]{bgcolor="inherit"} *n* [approaches
infinity. Snippet 18.4 implements the ideas discussed in Gao et al.
\[2008\], which improve on Kontoyiannis \[1997\] by looking for the
maximum redundancy between two substrings of the same
size.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 18.4 IMPLEMENTATION OF ALGORITHMS DISCUSSED IN GAO ET AL.
> \[2008\]**

> ![](Image00525.jpg){width="879" height="813"}

[One caveat of this method is that entropy rate is defined in the limit.
In the words of Kontoyiannis, "we fix a large
integer]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [as the size of our database." The
theorems used by Kontoyiannis' paper prove asymptotic convergence;
however, nowhere is a monotonicity property claimed. When a message is
short, a solution may be to repeat the same message multiple
times.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[A second caveat is that, because the window for matching must be
symmetric (same length for the dictionary as for the substring being
matched), the last bit is only considered for matching if the message\'s
length corresponds to an even number. One solution is to remove the
first bit of a message with odd length.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[A third caveat is that some final bits will be dismissed when preceded
by irregular sequences. This is also a consequence of the symmetric
matching window. For example, the entropy rate for "10000111" equals the
entropy rate for "10000110," meaning that the final bit is irrelevant
due to the unmatchable "11" in the sixth and seventh bit. When the end
of the message is particularly relevant, a good solution may be to
analyze the entropy of the reversed message. This not only ensures that
the final bits (i.e., the initial ones after the reversing) are used,
but actually they will be used to potentially match every bit. Following
the previous example, the entropy rate of "11100001" is 0.96, while the
entropy rate for "01100001' is 0.84.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000922307}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **18.5 Encoding Schemes** `</font>`{=html}

[Estimating entropy requires the encoding of a message. In this section
we will review a few encoding schemes used in the literature, which are
based on returns. Although not discussed in what follows, it is
advisable to encode information from fractionally (rather than integer)
differentiated series (Chapter 4), as they still contain some
memory.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **18.5.1 Binary Encoding** `</font>`{=html}

[Entropy rate estimation requires the discretization of a continuous
variable, so that each value can be assigned a code from a finite
alphabet. For example, a stream of returns]{bgcolor="inherit"} *r
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [can be
encoded according to the sign, 1 for]{bgcolor="inherit"} *r
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [\> 0, 0
for]{bgcolor="inherit"} *r
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [\< 0,
removing cases where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *r
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [= 0.
Binary encoding arises naturally in the case of returns series sampled
from price bars (i.e., bars that contain prices fluctuating between two
symmetric horizontal barriers, centered around the start price), because
\|]{bgcolor="inherit"} *r
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [\| is
approximately constant.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[When \|]{bgcolor="inherit"} *r
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [\| can
adopt a wide range of outcomes, binary encoding discards potentially
useful information. That is particularly the case when working with
intraday time bars, which are affected by the heteroscedasticity that
results from the inhomogeneous nature of tick data. One way to partially
address this heteroscedasticity is to sample prices according to a
subordinated stochastic process. Examples of that are trade bars and
volume bars, which contain a fixed number of trades or trades for a
fixed amount of volume (see Chapter 2). By operating in this
non-chronological, market-driven clock, we sample more frequently during
highly active periods, and less frequently during periods of less
activity, hence regularizing the distribution of \|]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*r ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [\| and
reducing the need for a large alphabet.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **18.5.2 Quantile Encoding** `</font>`{=html}

[Unless price bars are used, it is likely that more than two codes will
be needed. One approach consists in assigning a code to
each]{bgcolor="inherit"} *r
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [according
to the quantile it belongs to. The quantile boundaries are determined
using an in-sample period (training set). There will be the same number
of observations assigned to each letter for the overall in-sample, and
close to the same number of observations per letter out-of-sample. When
using the method, some codes span a greater fraction
of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *r
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* ['s range
than others. This uniform (in-sample) or close to uniform
(out-of-sample) distribution of codes tends to increase entropy readings
on average.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **18.5.3 Sigma Encoding** `</font>`{=html}

[As an alternative approach, rather than fixing the number of codes, we
could let the price stream determine the actual dictionary. Suppose we
fix a discretization step, σ. Then, we assign the value 0
to]{bgcolor="inherit"} *r
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [∈
\[.min{]{bgcolor="inherit"} *r* [}, min{]{bgcolor="inherit"} *r* [} +
σ)., 1 to]{bgcolor="inherit"} *r
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [∈
\[.min{]{bgcolor="inherit"} *r* [} + σ, min{]{bgcolor="inherit"} *r* [}
+ 2σ). and so on until every observation has been encoded with a total
of]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00234.jpg){width="148" height="36"}
[codes, where ceil\[.\] is the ceiling function. Unlike quantile
encoding, now each code covers the same fraction of]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*r ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* ['s
range. Because codes are not uniformly distributed, entropy readings
will tend to be smaller than in quantile encoding on average; however,
the appearance of a "rare" code will cause spikes in entropy
readings.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000927410}

`</font>`{=html}

be derived as]{bgcolor="inherit"}

least two uses of this result. First, it allows us to benchmark the

normal distribution, and find what combination of estimator, message
length, and encoding gives us an entropy estimate]{bgcolor="inherit"}

is discarded and entropy is underestimated.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Second, we can use the above equation to connect entropy with
volatility, by noting that]{bgcolor="inherit"}
