[Intuitively, entropy measures information as the level
of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *diversity* [contained in a random variable. This
intuition is formalized through the notion of generalized mean. The
implication is that Shannon\'s entropy is a special case of a diversity
measure (hence its connection with volatility). We can now define and
compute alternative measures of diversity, other than entropy,
where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *q* [≠ 1.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000937381}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **18.8 A Few Financial Applications of
Entropy** `</font>`{=html}

[In this section we will introduce a few applications of entropy to the
modelling of financial markets.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **18.8.1 Market Efficiency** `</font>`{=html}

[When arbitrage mechanisms exploit the complete set of opportunities,
prices instantaneously reflect the full amount of available information,
becoming unpredictable (i.e., a martingale), with no discernable
patterns. Conversely, when arbitrage is not perfect, prices contain
incomplete amounts of information, which gives rise to predictable
patterns. Patterns occur when a string contains redundant information,
which enables its compression. The entropy rate of a string determines
its optimal compression rate. The higher the entropy, the lower the
redundancy and the greater the informational content. Consequently, the
entropy of a price string tells us the degree of market efficiency at a
given point in time. A "decompressed" market is an efficient market,
because price information is non-redundant. A "compressed" market is an
inefficient market, because price information is redundant. Bubbles are
formed in compressed (low entropy) markets.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **18.8.2 Maximum Entropy Generation**
`</font>`{=html}

[In a series of papers, Fiedor \[2014a, 2014b, 2014c\] proposes to use
Kontoyiannis \[1997\] to estimate the amount of entropy present in a
price series. He argues that, out of the possible future outcomes, the
one that maximizes entropy may be the most profitable, because it is the
one that is least predictable by frequentist statistical models. It is
the black swan scenario most likely to trigger stop losses, thus
generating a feedback mechanism that will reinforce and exacerbate the
move, resulting in runs in the signs of the returns time
series.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **18.8.3 Portfolio Concentration**
`</font>`{=html}

[Consider an]{bgcolor="inherit"} *NxN* [covariance
matrix]{bgcolor="inherit"} *V* [, computed on returns. First, we compute
an eigenvalue decomposition of the matrix,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *VW*
[=]{bgcolor="inherit"} *W* [Λ. Second, we obtain the factor loadings
vector as]{bgcolor="inherit"} *f
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}ω`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[=]{bgcolor="inherit"} *W* [\'ω, where ω is the vector of
allocations,]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00813.jpg){width="99"
height="25"}
[.^[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000940268}\ [[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ `<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}1`</font>`{=html}\ `</font>`{=html}](#text00003.html#filepos0000963253)]{.small}^
Third, we derive the portion of risk contributed by each principal
component (Bailey and López de Prado \[2012\]) as]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00512.jpg){width="157" height="60"}

[where]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00144.jpg){width="89" height="25"}
[, and θ ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ ∈
\[0, 1\], ∀]{bgcolor="inherit"} *i* [= 1, ...,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N*
[. Fourth, Meucci \[2009\] proposed the following entropy-inspired
definition of portfolio concentration,]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00238.jpg){width="196" height="38"}

[At first, this definition of portfolio concentration may sound
striking, because θ
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ is not a
probability. The connection between this notion of concentration and
entropy is due to the generalized mean, which we discussed in Chapter
18, Section 18.7.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **18.8.4 Market Microstructure**
`</font>`{=html}

[Easley et al. \[1996, 1997\] showed that, when the odds of good news /
bad news are even, the probability of informed trading (PIN) can be
derived as]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00329.jpg){width="123" height="39"}

[where μ is the rate of arrival of informed traders, ϵ is the rate of
arrival of uninformed traders, and α is the probability of an
informational event. PIN can be interpreted as the fraction of orders
that arise from informed traders relative to the overall order
flow.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00564.jpg){width="455" height="42"}

[where]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00483.jpg){width="64" height="35"}
[. Note that 2]{bgcolor="inherit"} *v
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *B*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}τ`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [− 1 represents
the order flow imbalance,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *OI
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}τ`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [, which is a
bounded real-valued variable, where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *OI
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}τ`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [∈ \[ − 1, 1\].
The VPIN theory thus provides a formal link between the probability of
informed trading (PIN) and the persistency of order flow imbalances
under a volume clock. See Chapter 19 for further details on this
microstructural theory.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Persistent order flow imbalance is a necessary, non-sufficient
condition for adverse selection. For market makers to provide liquidity
to informed traders, that order flow imbalance \|]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*OI ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}τ`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [\| must also
have been relatively unpredictable. In other words, market makers are
not adversely selected when their prediction of order flow imbalance is
accurate, even if \|]{bgcolor="inherit"} *OI
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}τ`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [\| ≫ 0. In order
to determine the probability of adverse selection, we must determine how
unpredictable the order flow imbalance is. We can determine this by
applying information theory.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Consider a long sequence of symbols. When that sequence contains few
redundant patterns, it encompasses a level of complexity that makes it
hard to describe and predict. Kolmogorov \[1965\] formulated this
connection between redundancy and complexity. In information theory,
lossless compression is the task of perfectly describing a sequence with
as few bits as possible. The more redundancies a sequence contains, the
greater compression rates can be achieved. Entropy characterizes the
redundancy of a source, hence its Kolmogorov complexity and its
predictability. We can use this connection between the redundancy of a
sequence and its unpredictability (by market makers) to derive the
probability of adverse selection.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Here we will discuss one particular procedure that derives the
probability of adverse selection as a function of the complexity
ingrained in the order flow imbalance. First, given a sequence of volume
bars indexed by τ = 1, ...,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [, each bar of
size]{bgcolor="inherit"} *V* [, we determine the portion of volume
classified as buy,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *v
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *B*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}τ`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [∈ \[0, 1\].
Second, we compute the]{bgcolor="inherit"} *q* [-quantiles on
{]{bgcolor="inherit"} *v
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *B*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}τ`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [} that define a
set]{bgcolor="inherit"} *K* [of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *q* [disjoint
subsets,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *K* [= {]{bgcolor="inherit"} *K
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [,
...,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *K
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *q*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [}. Third,
we produce a mapping from each]{bgcolor="inherit"} *v
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *B*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}τ`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [to one of the
disjoint subsets,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *f* [:]{bgcolor="inherit"} *v
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *B*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}τ`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [→ {1,
...,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *q* [}, where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *f*
[\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *v
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *B*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}τ`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [\]
=]{bgcolor="inherit"} *i* [⇔]{bgcolor="inherit"} *v
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}τ`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *B*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}^*
[∈]{bgcolor="inherit"} *K
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [,
∀]{bgcolor="inherit"} *i* [∈ \[1,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *q* [\]. Fourth,
we quantize {]{bgcolor="inherit"} *v
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *B*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}τ`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [} by assigning
to each value]{bgcolor="inherit"} *v
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *B*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}τ`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [the index of the
subset]{bgcolor="inherit"} *K* [it belongs to,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *f*
[\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *v
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *B*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}τ`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [\]. This results
in a translation of the set of order imbalances {]{bgcolor="inherit"} *v
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *B*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}τ`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [} into a
quantized message]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X* [= \[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *f*
[\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *v
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *B*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[\],]{bgcolor="inherit"} *f* [\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *v
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}2`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *B*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}^* [\],
...,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *f* [\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *v
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *B*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *N*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [\]\].
Fifth, we estimate the entropy]{bgcolor="inherit"} *H*
[\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X* [\] using Kontoyiannis' Lempel-Ziv
algorithm. Sixth, we derive the cumulative distribution
function,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *F* [\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *H*
[\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X* [\]\], and use the time series of
{]{bgcolor="inherit"} *F* [\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *H*
[\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}τ`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [\]\]}
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}τ\ =\ 1,\ ...,`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *N*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
as a feature to predict adverse selection.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000951076}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **Exercises** `</font>`{=html}

::: {height="1em"}
:::

1.  [Form dollar bars on E-mini S&P 500 futures:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  Quantize the returns series using the binary method.
    2.  Quantize the returns series using the quantile encoding, using
        10 letters.
    3.  Quantize the returns series using the sigma encoding, where σ is
        the standard deviation of all bar returns.
    4.  Compute the entropy of the three encoded series, using the
        plug-in method.
    5.  Compute the entropy of the three encoded series, using
        Kontoyiannis' method, with a window size of 100.

2.  [Using the bars from exercise 1:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  Compute the returns series, {*r
        ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* }.
    2.  Encode the series as follows: 0 if *r
        ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ r
        ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}−\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
        \< 0, and 1 if *r
        ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ r
        ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}−\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
        ≥ 0.
    3.  Partition the series into 1000 non-overlapping subsets of equal
        size (you may have to drop some observations at the beginning).
    4.  Compute the entropy of each of the 1000 encoded subsets, using
        the plug-in method.
    5.  Compute the entropy of each of the 1000 encoded subsets, using
        the Kontoyiannis method, with a window size of 100.
    6.  Compute the correlation between results 2.d and 2.e.

3.  [Draw 1000 observations from a standard Normal
    > > distribution:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  What is the true entropy of this process?
    2.  Label the observations according to 8 quantiles.
    3.  Estimate the entropy using the plug-in method.
    4.  Estimate the entropy using the Kontoyiannis method:
        1.  using a window size of 10.
        2.  using a window size of 100.

4.  [Using the draws from exercise 3, {]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x
    > > ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [}
    > > ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}=\ 1,\ ...,\ 1000`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
    > > :]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  Compute *y
        ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* =
        ρ*y
        ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}−\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* +
        *x ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
        , where ρ = .5, *y
        ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* = 0.
    2.  Label {*y
        ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* }
        the observations according to 8 quantiles.
    3.  Estimate the entropy using the plug-in method.
    4.  Estimate the entropy using the Kontoyiannis method
        1.  using a window size of 10.
        2.  using a window size of 100.

5.  [Suppose a portfolio of 10 holdings with equal dollar
    > > allocations.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  The portion of the total risk contributed by the *i* th
        principal component is ![](Image00572.jpg){width="16"
        height="29"} , *i* = 1, ..., 10. What is the portfolio\'s
        entropy?
    2.  The portion of the total risk contributed by the *i* th
        principal component is ![](Image00667.jpg){width="47"
        height="28"} , *i* = 1, ..., 10. What is the portfolio\'s
        entropy?
    3.  The portion of the total risk contributed by the *i* th
        principal component is ![](Image00745.jpg){width="170"
        height="29"} , *i* = 1, ..., 10, α ∈ \[0, 1\]. Plot the
        portolio\'s entropy as a function of α.

`<font size="4">`{=html} **Note** `</font>`{=html}

^[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000963253}\ [[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ `<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}1`</font>`{=html}\ `</font>`{=html}](#text00003.html#filepos0000940268)]{.small}^
   Alternatively, we could have worked with a vector of holdings, should
the covariance matrix had been computed on price changes.

[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000963505}

`<font size="5">`{=html} **CHAPTER 19** `</font>`{=html}\
\
`<font size="5">`{=html} **Microstructural Features** `</font>`{=html}

[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000963747}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **19.1 Motivation** `</font>`{=html}

[Market microstructure studies "the process and outcomes of exchanging
assets under explicit trading rules" (O\'Hara \[1995\]). Microstructural
datasets include primary information about the auctioning process, like
order cancellations, double auction book, queues, partial fills,
aggressor side, corrections, replacements, etc. The main source is
Financial Information eXchange (FIX) messages, which can be purchased
from exchanges. The level of detail contained in FIX messages provides
researchers with the ability to understand how market participants
conceal and reveal their intentions. That makes microstructural data one
of the most important ingredients for building predictive ML
features.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00003.html#filepos0000964629}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **19.2 Review of the Literature**
`</font>`{=html}

[The depth and complexity of market microstructure theories has evolved
over time, as a function of the amount and variety of the data
available. The first generation of models used solely price information.
The two foundational results from those early days are trade
classification models (like the tick rule) and the Roll \[1984\] model.
The second generation of models came after volume datasets started to
become available, and researchers shifted their attention to study the
impact that volume has on prices. Two examples for this generation of
models are Kyle \[1985\] and Amihud \[2002\].]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[The third generation of models came after 1996, when Maureen O\'Hara,
David Easley, and others published their "probability of informed
trading" (PIN) theory (Easley et al. \[1996\]). This constituted a major
breakthrough, because PIN explained the bid-ask spread as the
consequence of a sequential strategic decision between liquidity
providers (market makers) and position takers (informed traders).
Essentially, it illustrated that market makers were sellers of the
option to be adversely selected by informed traders, and the bid-ask
spread is the premium they charge for that option. Easley et al.
\[2012a, 2012b\] explain how to estimate VPIN, a high-frequency estimate
of PIN under volume-based sampling.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[These are the main theoretical frameworks used by the microstructural
literature. O\'Hara \[1995\] and Hasbrouck \[2007\] offer a good
compendium of low-frequency microstructural models. Easley et al.
\[2013\] present a modern treatment of high-frequency microstructural
models.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00004.html}

[]{#text00004.html#filepos0000966533}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **19.3 First Generation: Price Sequences**
`</font>`{=html}

[The first generation of microstructural models concerned themselves
with estimating the bid-ask spread and volatility as proxies for
illiquidity. They did so with limited data and without imposing a
strategic or sequential structure to the trading
process.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **19.3.1 The Tick Rule** `</font>`{=html}

[In a double auction book, quotes are placed for selling a security at
various price levels (offers) or for buying a security at various price
levels (bids). Offer prices always exceed bid prices, because otherwise
there would be an instant match. A trade occurs whenever a buyer matches
an offer, or a seller matches a bid. Every trade has a buyer and a
seller, but only one side initiates the trade.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[The tick rule is an algorithm used to determine a trade\'s aggressor
side. A buy-initiated trade is labeled "1", and a sell-initiated trade
is labeled "-1", according to this logic:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00839.jpg){width="186" height="91"}

[where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *p
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [is the
price of the trade indexed by]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [= 1,
...,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [, and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *b
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [is arbitrarily
set to 1. A number of studies have determined that the tick rule
achieves high classification accuracy, despite its relative simplicity
(Aitken and Frino \[1996\]). Competing classification methods include
Lee and Ready \[1991\] and Easley et al. \[2016\].]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Transformations of the {]{bgcolor="inherit"} *b
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [} series
can result in informative features. Such transformations include: (1)
Kalman Filters on its future expected value, E
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *b
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}+\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[\]; (2) structural breaks on such predictions (Chapter 17), (3) entropy
of the {]{bgcolor="inherit"} *b
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [} sequence
(Chapter 18); (4) t-values from Wald-Wolfowitz\'s tests of runs on
{]{bgcolor="inherit"} *b
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [}; (5)
fractional differentiation of the cumulative {]{bgcolor="inherit"} *b
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [}
series,]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00648.jpg){width="56" height="24"}
[(Chapter 5); etc.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **19.3.2 The Roll Model** `</font>`{=html}

[Roll \[1984\] was one of the first models to propose an explanation for
the effective bid-ask spread at which a security trades. This is useful
in that bid-ask spreads are a function of liquidity, hence Roll\'s model
can be seen as an early attempt to measure the liquidity of a security.
Consider a mid-price series {]{bgcolor="inherit"} *m
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [}, where
prices follow a Random Walk with no drift,]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00375.jpg){width="116" height="16"}

[hence price changes Δ]{bgcolor="inherit"} *m
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[=]{bgcolor="inherit"} *m
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[−]{bgcolor="inherit"} *m
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}−\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[are independently and identically drawn from a Normal
distribution]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00262.jpg){width="113" height="25"}

[These assumptions are, of course, against all empirical observations,
which suggest that financial time series have a drift, they are
heteroscedastic, exhibit serial dependency, and their returns
distribution is non-Normal. But with a proper sampling procedure, as we
saw in Chapter 2, these assumptions may not be too unrealistic. The
observed prices, {]{bgcolor="inherit"} *p
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [}, are the
result of sequential trading against the bid-ask
spread:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00259.jpg){width="105" height="19"}

[where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *c* [is half the bid-ask spread,
and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *b
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [∈ { − 1,
1} is the aggressor side. The Roll model assumes that buys and sells are
equally likely,]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00435.jpg){width="219"
height="29"} [, serially independent, E\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *b
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ b
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}−\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[\] = 0, and independent from the noise, E\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *b
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ u
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [\] =
0]{bgcolor="inherit"} *.* [Given these assumptions, Roll derives the
values of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *c* [and σ
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}2`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *u*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ as
follows:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00498.jpg){width="394" height="36"}

![](Image00702.jpg){width="165" height="25"}

[resulting in]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00684.jpg){width="241"
height="25"} [and σ
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}2`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *u*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ = σ
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}2`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
\[Δ]{bgcolor="inherit"} *p
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [\] +
2σ\[Δ]{bgcolor="inherit"} *p
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [,
Δ]{bgcolor="inherit"} *p
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}−\ 1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[\]. In conclusion, the bid-ask spread is a function of the serial
covariance of price changes, and the true (unobserved) price\'s noise,
excluding microstructural noise, is a function of the observed noise and
the serial covariance of price changes.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[The reader may question the need for Roll\'s model nowadays, when
datasets include bid-ask prices at multiple book levels. One reason the
Roll model is still in use, despite its limitations, is that it offers a
relatively direct way to determine the]{bgcolor="inherit"} *effective*
[bid-ask spread of securities that are either rarely traded, or where
the published quotes are not representative of the levels at which
market makers' are willing to provide liquidity (e.g., corporate,
municipal, and agency bonds). Using Roll\'s estimates, we can derive
informative features regarding the market\'s liquidity
conditions.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **19.3.3 High-Low Volatility Estimator**
`</font>`{=html}

[Beckers \[1983\] shows that volatility estimators based on high-low
prices are more accurate than the standard estimators of volatility
based on closing prices. Parkinson \[1980\] derives that, for
continuously observed prices following a geometric Brownian
motion,]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00770.jpg){width="268" height="59"}

![](Image00008.jpg){width="260" height="59"}

[where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *k
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}1`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [=
4log\[2\],]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00102.jpg){width="76"
height="37"} [,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *H
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [is the
high price for bar]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [, and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *L
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [is the low
price for bar]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t.* [Then the volatility feature σ
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *HL*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ can be
robustly estimated based on observed high-low
prices.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **19.3.4 Corwin and Schultz** `</font>`{=html}

[Building on the work of Beckers \[1983\], Corwin and Schultz \[2012\]
introduce a bid-ask spread estimator from high and low prices. The
estimator is based on two principles: First, high prices are almost
always matched against the offer, and low prices are almost always
matched against the bid. The ratio of high-to-low prices reflects
fundamental volatility as well as the bid-ask spread. Second, the
component of the high-to-low price ratio that is due to volatility
increases proportionately with the time elapsed between two
observations.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Corwin and Schultz show that the spread, as a percentage of price, can
be estimated as]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00189.jpg){width="121" height="41"}

[where]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00283.jpg){width="258" height="63"}

![](Image00091.jpg){width="225" height="60"}

![](Image00161.jpg){width="170" height="54"}

[and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *H
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}−\ 1,`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[is the high price over 2 bars (]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [− 1
and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [), whereas]{bgcolor="inherit"} *L
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}−\ 1,`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[is the low price over 2 bars (]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [− 1
and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [). Because α
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ \<
0⇒]{bgcolor="inherit"} *S
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [\< 0, the
authors recommend setting negative alphas to 0 (see Corwin and Schultz
\[2012\], p. 727). Snippet 19.1 implements this algorithm.
The]{bgcolor="inherit"} `corwinSchultz` [function receives two
arguments, a series dataframe with columns (]{bgcolor="inherit"} `High`
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"} `Low` [), and an integer
value]{bgcolor="inherit"} `sl` [that defines the sample length used to
estimate β ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 19.1 IMPLEMENTATION OF THE CORWIN-SCHULTZ ALGORITHM**

> ![](Image00114.jpg){width="882" height="847"}

[Note that volatility does not appear in the final Corwin-Schultz
equations. The reason is that volatility has been replaced by its
high/low estimator. As a byproduct of this model, we can derive the
Becker-Parkinson volatility as shown in Snippet
19.2.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 19.2 ESTIMATING VOLATILITY FOR HIGH-LOW PRICES**

> ![](Image00056.jpg){width="457" height="181"}

[This procedure is particularly helpful in the corporate bond market,
where there is no centralized order book, and trades occur through bids
wanted in competition (BWIC). The resulting feature, bid-ask
spread]{bgcolor="inherit"} *S* [, can be estimated recursively over a
rolling window, and values can be smoothed using a Kalman
filter.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00004.html#filepos0000980046}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **19.4 Second Generation: Strategic Trade
Models** `</font>`{=html}

[Second generation microstructural models focus on understanding and
measuring illiquidity. Illiquidity is an important informative feature
in financial ML models, because it is a risk that has an associated
premium. These models have a stronger theoretical foundation than
first-generation models, in that they explain trading as the strategic
interaction between informed and uninformed traders. In doing so, they
pay attention to signed volume and order flow
imbalance.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Most of these features are estimated through regressions. In practice,
I have observed that the t-values associated with these microstructural
estimates are more informative than the (mean) estimates themselves.
Although the literature does not mention this observation, there is a
good argument for preferring features based on t-values over features
based on mean values: t-values are re-scaled by the standard deviation
of the estimation error, which incorporates another dimension of
information absent in mean estimates.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **19.4.1 Kyle\'s Lambda** `</font>`{=html}

[Kyle \[1985\] introduced the following strategic trade model. Consider
a risky asset with terminal value]{bgcolor="inherit"} *v*
[∼]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [\[]{bgcolor="inherit"} *p
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [, Σ
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ \], as well as two
traders:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

-   A noise trader who trades a quantity *u* = *N* \[0, σ
    ^[`<font size="2">`{=html}2`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
    ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *u*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ \],
    independent of *v* .
-   An informed trader who knows *v* and demands a quantity *x* ,
    through a market order.

[The market maker observes the total order flow]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y*
[=]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x* [+]{bgcolor="inherit"} *u* [, and sets a
price]{bgcolor="inherit"} *p* [accordingly. In this model, market makers
cannot distinguish between orders from noise traders and informed
traders. They adjust prices as a function of the order flow imbalance,
as that may indicate the presence of an informed trader. Hence, there is
a positive relationship between price change and order flow imbalance,
which is called market impact.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[The informed trader conjectures that the market maker has a linear
price adjustment function,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *p* [=
λ]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y* [+ μ, where λ is an inverse measure of
liquidity. The informed trader\'s profits are π = (]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*v* [−]{bgcolor="inherit"} *p* [)]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x* [, which are
maximized at]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00139.jpg){width="62"
height="26"} [, with second order condition λ \> 0.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Conversely, the market maker conjectures that the informed trader\'s
demand is a linear function of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *v*
[:]{bgcolor="inherit"} *x* [= α + β]{bgcolor="inherit"} *v* [, which
implies]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00221.jpg){width="65" height="26"}
[and]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00287.jpg){width="53" height="29"} [.
Note that lower liquidity means higher λ, which means lower demand from
the informed trader.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Kyle argues that the market maker must find an equilibrium between
profit maximization and market efficiency, and that under the above
linear functions, the only possible solution occurs
when]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00421.jpg){width="52" height="14"}

![](Image00180.jpg){width="98" height="61"}

![](Image00623.jpg){width="94" height="61"}

![](Image00673.jpg){width="81" height="61"}

[Finally, the informed trader\'s expected profit can be rewritten
as]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00751.jpg){width="312" height="61"}

[The implication is that the informed trader has three sources of
profit:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

-   The security\'s mispricing.
-   The variance of the noise trader\'s net order flow. The higher the
    noise, the easier the informed trader can conceal his intentions.
-   The reciprocal of the terminal security\'s variance. The lower the
    volatility, the easier to monetize the mispricing.

[In Kyle\'s model, the variable λ captures price impact. Illiquidity
increases with uncertainty about]{bgcolor="inherit"} *v* [and decreases
with the amount of noise. As a feature, it can be estimated by fitting
the regression]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00846.jpg){width="152" height="24"}

~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *i*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ for every

approximates the effective cost of trading (market
impact).]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Consistent with most of the literature, Hasbrouck recommends 5-minute
time-bars for sampling ticks. However, for the reasons discussed in
Chapter 2, better results can be achieved through stochastic sampling
methods that are synchronized with market activity.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00004.html#filepos0000992251}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **19.5 Third Generation: Sequential Trade
Models** `</font>`{=html}

[As we have seen in the previous section, strategic trade models feature
a single informed trader who can trade at multiple times. In this
section we will discuss an alternative kind of model, where randomly
selected traders arrive at the market sequentially and
independently.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Since their appearance, sequential trade models have become very
popular among market makers. One reason is, they incorporate the sources
of uncertainty faced by liquidity providers, namely the probability that
an informational event has taken place, the probability that such event
is negative, the arrival rate of noise traders, and the arrival rate of
informed traders. With those variables, market makers must update quotes
dynamically, and manage their inventories.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **19.5.1 Probability of Information-based
Trading** `</font>`{=html}

[Easley et al. \[1996\] use trade data to determine the probability of
information-based trading (PIN) of individual securities. This
microstructure model views trading as a game between market makers and
position takers that is repeated over multiple trading
periods.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Denote a security\'s price as]{bgcolor="inherit"} *S* [, with present
value]{bgcolor="inherit"} *S
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}0`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [. However, once
a certain amount of new information has been incorporated into the
price,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *S* [will be either]{bgcolor="inherit"} *S
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *B*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [(bad news)
or]{bgcolor="inherit"} *S
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *G*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [(good
news). There is a probability α that new information will arrive within
the timeframe of the analysis, a probability δ that the news will be
bad, and a probability (1 − δ) that the news will be good. These authors
prove that the expected value of the security\'s price can then be
computed at time]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [as]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00687.jpg){width="352" height="24"}

[Following a Poisson distribution, informed traders arrive at a rate μ,
and uninformed traders arrive at a rate ε. Then, in order to avoid
losses from informed traders, market makers reach breakeven at a bid
level]{bgcolor="inherit"} *B
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00743.jpg){width="320" height="46"}

[and the breakeven ask level]{bgcolor="inherit"} *A
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [at
time]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [must be,]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00013.jpg){width="375" height="56"}

[It follows that the breakeven bid-ask spread is determined
as]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00106.jpg){width="554" height="56"}

[For the standard case when]{bgcolor="inherit"}

obtain]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00796.jpg){width="345" height="43"}

[This equation tells us that the critical factor that determines the
price range at which market makers provide liquidity
is]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00382.jpg){width="133" height="42"}

[The subscript]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [indicates that the probabilities
α and δ are estimated at that point in time. The authors apply a
Bayesian updating process to incorporate information after each trade
arrives to the market.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[In order to determine the value]{bgcolor="inherit"} *PIN
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [, we must
estimate four non-observable parameters, namely {α, δ, μ, ε}. A
maximum-likelihood approach is to fit a mixture of three Poisson
distributions,]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *V

volume traded against the ask (buy-initiated trades),
and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *V

volume traded against the bid (sell-initiated
trades).]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **19.5.2 Volume-Synchronized Probability of
Informed Trading** `</font>`{=html}

[Easley et al. \[2008\] proved that]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00524.jpg){width="560" height="45"}

[and in particular, for a sufficiently large μ,]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00634.jpg){width="150" height="22"}

[Easley et al. \[2011\] proposed a high-frequency estimate of PIN, which
they named volume-synchronized probability of informed trading (VPIN).
This procedure adopts a]{bgcolor="inherit"} *volume clock* [, which
synchronizes the data sampling with market activity, as captured by
volume (see Chapter 2). We can then estimate]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00497.jpg){width="169" height="53"}

[where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *V
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *B*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}τ`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [is the sum of
volumes from buy-initiated trades within volume bar
τ,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *V
^[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *S*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}τ`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [is the sum of
volumes from sell-initiated trades within volume bar τ,
and]{bgcolor="inherit"} *n* [is the number of bars used to produce this
estimate. Because all volume bars are of the same
size,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *V* [, we know that by
construction]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00683.jpg){width="253" height="53"}

[Hence, PIN can be estimated in high-frequency as]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00036.jpg){width="364" height="57"}

[For additional details and case studies of VPIN, see Easley et al.
\[2013\]. Using linear regressions, Andersen and Bondarenko \[2013\]
concluded that VPIN is not a good predictor of volatility. However, a
number of studies have found that VPIN indeed has predictive power: Abad
and Yague \[2012\], Bethel et al. \[2012\], Cheung et al. \[2015\], Kim
et al. \[2014\], Song et al. \[2014\], Van Ness et al. \[2017\], and Wei
et al. \[2013\], to cite a few. In any case, linear regression is a
technique that was already known to 18th-century mathematicians (Stigler
\[1981\]), and economists should not be surprised when it fails to
recognize complex non-linear patterns in 21st-century financial
markets.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00004.html#filepos0001000562}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **19.6 Additional Features from Microstructural
Datasets** `</font>`{=html}

[The features we have studied in Sections 19.3 to 19.5 were suggested by
market microstructure theory. In addition, we should consider
alternative features that, although not suggested by the theory, we
suspect carry important information about the way market participants
operate, and their future intentions. In doing so, we will harness the
power of ML algorithms, which can learn how to use these features
without being specifically directed by theory.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **19.6.1 Distibution of Order Sizes**
`</font>`{=html}

[Easley et al. \[2016\] study the frequency of trades per trade size,
and find that trades with round sizes are abnormally frequent. For
example, the frequency rates quickly decay as a function of trade size,
with the exception of round trade sizes {5, 10, 20, 25, 50, 100, 200,
...}. These authors attribute this phenomenon to so-called "mouse" or
"GUI" traders, that is, human traders who send orders by clicking
buttons on a GUI (Graphical User Interface). In the case of the E-mini
S&P 500, for example, size 10 is 2.9 times more frequent than size 9;
size 50 is 10.9 times more likely than size 49; size 100 is 16.8 times
more frequent than size 99; size 200 is 27.2 times more likely than size
199; size 250 is 32.5 times more frequent than size 249; size 500 is
57.1 times more frequent than size 499. Such patterns are not typical of
"silicon traders," who usually are programmed to randomize trades to
disguise their footprint in markets.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[A useful feature may be to determine the normal frequency of
round-sized trades, and monitor deviations from that expected value. The
ML algorithm could, for example, determine if a larger-than-usual
proportion of round-sized trades is associated with trends, as human
traders tend to bet with a fundamental view, belief, or conviction.
Conversely, a lower-than-usual proportion of round-sized trades may
increase the likelihood that prices will move sideways, as silicon
traders do not typically hold long-term views.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **19.6.2 Cancellation Rates, Limit Orders,
Market Orders** `</font>`{=html}

[Eisler et al. \[2012\] study the impact of market orders, limit orders,
and quote cancellations. These authors find that small stocks respond
differently than large stocks to these events. They conclude that
measuring these magnitudes is relevant to model the dynamics of the
bid-ask spread.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Easley et al. \[2012\] also argue that large quote cancellation rates
may be indicative of low liquidity, as participants are publishing
quotes that do not intend to get filled. They discuss four categories of
predatory algorithms:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

-   **Quote stuffers:** They engage in "latency arbitrage." Their
    strategy involves overwhelming an exchange with messages, with the
    sole intention of slowing down competing algorithms, which are
    forced to parse messages that only the originators know can be
    ignored.
-   **Quote danglers:** This strategy sends quotes that force a squeezed
    trader to chase a price against her interests. O\'Hara \[2011\]
    presents evidence of their disruptive activities.
-   **Liquidity squeezers:** When a distressed large investor is forced
    to unwind her position, predatory algorithms trade in the same
    direction, draining as much liquidity as possible. As a result,
    prices overshoot and they make a profit (Carlin et al. \[2007\]).
-   **Pack hunters:** Predators hunting independently become aware of
    one another\'s activities, and form a pack in order to maximize the
    chances of triggering a cascading effect (Donefer \[2010\], Fabozzi
    et al. \[2011\], Jarrow and Protter \[2011\]). NANEX \[2011\] shows
    what appears to be pack hunters forcing a stop loss. Although their
    individual actions are too small to raise the regulator\'s
    suspicion, their collective action may be market-manipulative. When
    that is the case, it is very hard to prove their collusion, since
    they coordinate in a decentralized, spontaneous manner.

[These predatory algorithms utilize quote cancellations and various
order types in an attempt to adversely select market makers. They leave
different signatures in the trading record, and measuring the rates of
quote cancellation, limit orders, and market orders can be the basis for
useful features, informative of their intentions.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **19.6.3 Time-Weighted Average Price Execution
Algorithms** `</font>`{=html}

[Easley et al. \[2012\] demonstrate how to recognize the presence of
execution algorithms that target a particular time-weighted average
price (TWAP). A TWAP algorithm is an algorithm that slices a large order
into small ones, which are submitted at regular time intervals, in an
attempt to achieve a pre-defined time-weighted average price. These
authors take a sample of E-mini S&P 500 futures trades between November
7, 2010, and November 7, 2011. They divide the day into 24 hours, and
for every hour, they add the volume traded at each second, irrespective
of the minute. Then they plot these aggregate volumes as a surface where
the x-axis is assigned to volume per second, the y-axis is assigned to
hour of the day, and the z-axis is assigned to the aggregate volume.
This analysis allows us to see the distribution of volume within each
minute as the day passes, and search for low-frequency traders executing
their massive orders on a chronological time-space. The largest
concentrations of volume within a minute tend to occur during the first
few seconds, for almost every hour of the day. This is particularly true
at 00:00--01:00 GMT (around the open of Asian markets), 05:00--09:00 GMT
(around the open of U.K. and European equities), 13:00--15:00 GMT
(around the open of U.S. equities), and 20:00--21:00 GMT (around the
close of U.S. equities).]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[A useful ML feature may be to evaluate the order imbalance at the
beginning of every minute, and determine whether there is a persistent
component. This can then be used to front-run large institutional
investors, while the larger portion of their TWAP order is still
pending.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **19.6.4 Options Markets** `</font>`{=html}

[Muravyev et al. \[2013\] use microstructural information from U.S.
stocks and options to study events where the two markets disagree. They
characterize such disagreement by deriving the underlying bid-ask range
implied by the put-call parity quotes and comparing it to the actual
bid-ask range of the stock. They conclude that disagreements tend to be
resolved in favor of stock quotes, meaning that
option]{bgcolor="inherit"} *quotes* [do not contain economically
significant information. At the same time, they do find that
option]{bgcolor="inherit"} *trades* [contain information not included in
the stock price. These findings will not come as a surprise to portfolio
managers used to trade relatively illiquid products, including stock
options. Quotes can remain irrational for prolonged periods of time,
even as sparse prices are informative.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Cremers and Weinbaum \[2010\] find that stocks with relatively
expensive calls (stocks with both a high volatility spread and a high
change in the volatility spread) outperform stocks with relatively
expensive puts (stocks with both a low volatility spread and a low
change in the volatility spread) by 50 basis points per week. This
degree of predictability is larger when option liquidity is high and
stock liquidity is low.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[In line with these observations, useful features can be extracted from
computing the put-call implied stock price, derived from option trades.
Futures prices only represent mean or expected future values. But option
prices allow us to derive the entire distribution of outcomes being
priced. An ML algorithm can search for patterns across the Greek letters
quoted at various strikes and expiration dates.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **19.6.5 Serial Correlation of Signed Order
Flow** `</font>`{=html}

[Toth et al. \[2011\] study the signed order flow of London Stock
Exchange stocks, and find that order signs are positively autocorrelated
for many days. They attribute this observation to two candidate
explanations: Herding and order splitting. They conclude that on
timescales of less than a few hours, the persistence of order flow is
overwhelmingly due to splitting rather than herding.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Given that market microstructure theory attributes the persistency of
order flow imbalance to the presence of informed traders, it makes sense
to measure the strength of such persistency through the serial
correlation of the signed volumes. Such a feature would be complementary
to the features we studied in Section 19.5.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00004.html#filepos0001010555}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **19.7 What Is Microstructural Information?**
`</font>`{=html}

[Let me conclude this chapter by addressing what I consider to be a
major flaw in the market microstructure literature. Most articles and
books on this subject study asymmetric information, and how strategic
agents utilize it to profit from market makers. But how is information
exactly defined in the context of trading? Unfortunately, there is no
widely accepted definition of information in a microstructural sense,
and the literature uses this concept in a surprisingly loose, rather
informal way (López de Prado \[2017\]). This section proposes a proper
definition of information, founded on signal processing, that can be
applied to microstructural studies.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Consider a features matrix]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X* [=
{]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [}
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}=\ 1,\ ...,`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *T*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
that contains information typically used by market makers to determine
whether they should provide liquidity at a particular level, or cancel
their passive quotes. For example, the columns could be all of the
features discussed in this chapter, like VPIN, Kyle\'s lambda,
cancellation rates, etc. Matrix]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X* [has one row for
each decision point. For example, a market maker may reconsider the
decision to either provide liquidity or pull out of the market every
time 10,000 contracts are traded, or whenever there is a significant
change in prices (recall sampling methods in Chapter 2), etc. First, we
derive an array]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y* [= {]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [}
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}=\ 1,\ ...,`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *T*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
that assigns a label 1 to an observation that resulted in a
market-making profit, and labels as 0 an observation that resulted in a
market-making loss (see Chapter 3 for labeling methods). Second, we fit
a classifier on the training set (]{bgcolor="inherit"} *X*
[,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *y* [). Third, as new out-of-sample observations
arrive τ \>]{bgcolor="inherit"} *T* [, we use the fit classifier to
predict the label]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00438.jpg){width="110"
height="19"} [. Fourth, we derive the cross-entropy loss of these
predictions,]{bgcolor="inherit"} *L
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}τ`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [, as described
in Chapter 9, Section 9.4. Fifth, we fit a kernel density estimator
(KDE) on the array of negative cross-entropy losses, {
−]{bgcolor="inherit"} *L
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [}
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}=`</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}\ *T*\ `</font>`{=html}\ `<font size="2">`{=html}+\ 1,\ ...,\ τ`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
, to derive its cumulative distribution function,]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*F.* [Sixth, we estimate the microstructural information at
time]{bgcolor="inherit"} *t* [as φ
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}τ`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~
=]{bgcolor="inherit"} *F* [\[ −]{bgcolor="inherit"} *L
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}τ`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [\], where φ
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}τ`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ ∈ (0,
1).]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[This microstructural information can be understood as the complexity
faced by market makers' decision models. Under normal market conditions,
market makers produce]{bgcolor="inherit"} *informed forecasts* [with low
cross-entropy loss, and are able to profit from providing liquidity to
position takers. However, in the presence of (asymmetrically) informed
traders, market makers produce]{bgcolor="inherit"} *uninformed
forecasts* [, as measured by high cross-entropy loss, and they are
adversely selected. In other words, microstructural information can only
be defined and measured relative to the predictive power of market
makers. The implication is that {φ
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}τ`</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ } should become an
important feature in your financial ML toolkit.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Consider the events of the flash crash of May 6, 2010. Market makers
wrongly predicted that their passive quotes sitting on the bid could be
filled and sold back at a higher level. The crash was not caused by a
single inaccurate prediction, but by the accumulation of thousands of
prediction errors (Easley et al. \[2011\]). If market makers had
monitored the rising cross-entropy loss of their predictions, they would
have recognized the presence of informed traders and the dangerously
rising probability of adverse selection. That would have allowed them to
widen the bid-ask spread to levels that would have stopped the order
flow imbalance, as sellers would no longer have been willing to sell at
those discounts. Instead, market makers kept providing liquidity to
sellers at exceedingly generous levels, until eventually they were
forced to stop-out, triggering a liquidity crisis that shocked markets,
regulators, and academics for months and years.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00004.html#filepos0001016132}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **Exercises** `</font>`{=html}

::: {height="1em"}
:::

1.  [From a time series of E-mini S&P 500 futures tick
    > > data,]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  Apply the tick rule to derive the series of trade signs.
    2.  Compare to the aggressor\'s side, as provided by the CME (FIX
        tag 5797). What is the accuracy of the tick rule?
    3.  Select the cases where FIX tag 5797 disagrees with the tick
        rule.
        ::: {height="10pt"}
        :::

        1.  Can you see anything distinct that would explain the
            disagreement?
        2.  Are these disagreements associated with large price jumps?
            Or high cancelation rates? Or thin quoted sizes?
        3.  Are these disagreements more likely to occur during periods
            of high or low market activity?

2.  [Compute the Roll model on the time series of E-mini S&P 500 futures
    > > tick data.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  What are the estimated values of σ
        ^[`<font size="2">`{=html}2`</font>`{=html}]{.small}^
        ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *u*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ and
        *c* ?
    2.  Knowing that this contract is one of the most liquid products in
        the world, and that it trades at the tightest possible bid-ask
        spread, are these values in line with your expectations?

3.  [Compute the high-low volatility estimator (]{bgcolor="inherit"}
    > > Section19.3.3.[) on E-mini S&P 500 futures:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  Using weekly values, how does this differ from the standard
        deviation of close-to-close returns?
    2.  Using daily values, how does this differ from the standard
        deviation of close-to-close returns?
    3.  Using dollar bars, for an average of 50 bars per day, how does
        this differ from the standard deviation of close-to-close
        returns?

4.  [Apply the Corwin-Schultz estimator to a daily series of E-mini S&P
    > > 500 futures.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  What is the expected bid-ask spread?
    2.  What is the implied volatility?
    3.  Are these estimates consistent with the earlier results, from
        exercises 2 and 3?

5.  [Compute Kyle\'s lambda from:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  tick data.
    2.  a time series of dollar bars on E-mini S&P 500 futures, where
        1.  *b
            ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
            is the volume-weighted average of the trade signs.
        2.  *V
            ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
            is the sum of the volumes in that bar.
        3.  Δ*p
            ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *t*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
            is the change in price between two consecutive bars.

6.  [Repeat exercise 5, this time applying Hasbrouck\'s lambda. Are
    > > results consistent?]{bgcolor="inherit"}

7.  [Repeat exercise 5, this time applying Amihud\'s lambda. Are results
    > > consistent?]{bgcolor="inherit"}

8.  [Form a time series of volume bars on E-mini S&P 500
    > > futures,]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  Compute the series of VPIN on May 6, 2010 (flash crash).
    2.  Plot the series of VPIN and prices. What do you see?

9.  [Compute the distribution of order sizes for E-mini S&P 500
    > > futures]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  Over the entire period.
    2.  For May 6, 2010.
    3.  Conduct a Kolmogorov-Smirnov test on both distributions. Are
        they significantly different, at a 95% confidence level?

10. [Compute a time series of daily quote cancellations rates, and the
    > > portion of market orders, on the E-mini S&P 500 futures
    > > dataset.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  What is the correlation between these two series? Is it
        statistically significant?
    2.  What is the correlation between the two series and daily
        volatility? Is this what you expected?

11. [On the E-mini S&P 500 futures tick data:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  Compute the distribution of volume executed within the first 5
        seconds of every minute.
    2.  Compute the distribution of volume executed every minute.
    3.  Compute the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test on both distributions. Are
        they significantly different, at a 95% confidence level?

12. [On the E-mini S&P 500 futures tick data:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  Compute the first-order serial correlation of signed volumes.
    2.  Is it statistically significant, at a 95% confidence level?

`</font>`{=html}

:::

    Vectorization`</font>`{=html}](#text00004.html#filepos0001034475)

    Computers`</font>`{=html}](#text00004.html#filepos0001074501)

    Computational Intelligence and Forecasting

[]{#text00004.html#filepos0001034475}

`<font size="5">`{=html} **CHAPTER 20** `</font>`{=html}\
\
`<font size="5">`{=html} **Multiprocessing and Vectorization**
`</font>`{=html}

[]{#text00004.html#filepos0001034726}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **20.1 Motivation** `</font>`{=html}

[Multiprocessing is essential to ML. ML algorithms are computationally
intensive, and they will require an efficient use of all your CPUs,
servers, and clusters. For this reason, most of the functions presented
throughout this book were designed for asynchronous multiprocessing. For
example, we have made frequent use of a mysterious function
called]{bgcolor="inherit"} `mpPandasObj` [, without ever defining it. In
this chapter we will explain what this function does. Furthermore, we
will study in detail how to develop multiprocessing engines. The
structure of the programs presented in this chapter is agnostic to the
hardware architecture used to execute them, whether we employ the cores
of a single server or cores distributed across multiple interconnected
servers (e.g., in a high-performance computing cluster or a
cloud).]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00004.html#filepos0001035757}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **20.2 Vectorization Example** `</font>`{=html}

[Vectorization, also known as array programming, is the simplest example
of parallelization, whereby an operation is applied at once to the
entire set of values. As a minimal example, suppose that you need to do
a brute search through a 3-dimensional space, with 2 nodes per
dimension. The un-vectorized implementation of that Cartesian product
will look something like Snippet 20.1. How would this code look if you
had to search through 100 dimensions, or if the number of dimensions was
defined by the user during runtime?]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 20.1 UN-VECTORIZED CARTESIAN PRODUCT**

> ![](Image00730.jpg){width="598" height="158"}

[A vectorized solution would replace all explicit iterators
(e.g.,]{bgcolor="inherit"} `For. . .loops` [) with matrix algebra
operations or compiled iterators or generators. Snippet 20.2 implements
the vectorized version of Snippet 20.1. The vectorized version is
preferable for four reasons: (1) slow nested]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`For. . .loops` [are replaced with fast iterators; (2) the code infers
the dimensionality of the mesh from the dimensionality
of]{bgcolor="inherit"} `dict0` [; (3) we could run 100 dimensions
without having to modify the code, or need 100]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`For. . .loops` [; and (4) under the hood, Python can run operations in
C or C + + .]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 20.2 VECTORIZED CARTESIAN PRODUCT**

> ![](Image00313.jpg){width="731" height="129"}

[]{#text00004.html#filepos0001037784}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **20.3 Single-Thread vs. Multithreading vs.
Multiprocessing** `</font>`{=html}

[A modern computer has multiple CPU sockets. Each CPU has many cores
(processors), and each core has several threads. Multithreading is the
technique by which several applications are run in parallel on two or
more threads under the same core. One advantage of multithreading is
that, because the applications share the same core, they share the same
memory space. That introduces the risk that several applications may
write on the same memory space at the same time. To prevent that from
happening, the Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) assigns write access to one
thread per core at a time. Under the GIL, Python\'s multithreading is
limited to one thread per processor. For this reason, Python achieves
parallelism through multiprocessing rather than through actual
multithreading. Processors do not share the same memory space, hence
multiprocessing does not risk writing to the same memory space; however,
that also makes it harder to share objects between
processes.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Python functions implemented for running on a single-thread will use
only a fraction of a modern computer\'s, server\'s, or cluster\'s power.
Let us see an example of how a simple task can be run inefficiently when
implemented for single-thread execution. Snippet 20.3 finds the earliest
time 10,000 Gaussian processes of length 1,000 touch a symmetric double
barrier of width 50 times the standard deviation.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 20.3 SINGLE-THREAD IMPLEMENTATION OF A ONE-TOUCH DOUBLE
> BARRIER**

> ![](Image00364.jpg){width="946" height="525"}

[Compare this implementation with Snippet 20.4. Now the code splits the
previous problem into 24 tasks, one per processor. The tasks are then
run asynchronously in parallel, using 24 processors. If you run the same
code on a cluster with 5000 CPUs, the elapsed time will be about 1/5000
of the single-thread implementation.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 20.4 MULTIPROCESSING IMPLEMENTATION OF A ONE-TOUCH DOUBLE
> BARRIER**

> ![](Image00825.jpg){width="944" height="477"}

[Moreover, you could implement the same code to multiprocess a
vectorized function, as we did with function]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`applyPtSlOnT1` [in Chapter 3, where parallel processes execute
subroutines that include vectorized pandas objects. In this way, you
will achieve two levels of parallelization at once. But why stop there?
You could achieve three levels of parallelization at once by running
multiprocessed instances of vectorized code in an HPC cluster, where
each node in the cluster provides the third level of parallelization. In
the next sections, we will explain how multiprocessing
works.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00004.html#filepos0001041072}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **20.4 Atoms and Molecules** `</font>`{=html}

[When preparing jobs for parallelization, it is useful to distinguish
between atoms and molecules. Atoms are indivisible tasks. Rather than
carrying out all these tasks sequentially in a single thread, we want to
group them into molecules, which can be processed in parallel using
multiple processors. Each molecule is a subset of atoms that will be
processed sequentially, by a callback function, using a single thread.
Parallelization takes place at the molecular level.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **20.4.1 Linear Partitions** `</font>`{=html}

[The simplest way to form molecules is to partition a list of atoms in
subsets of equal size, where the number of subsets is the minimum
between the number of processors and the number of atoms.
For]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [subsets we need to find
the]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N + 1* [indices that enclose the partitions.
This logic is demonstrated in Snippet 20.5.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 20.5 THE** ` LINPARTS` **FUNCTION**

> ![](Image00255.jpg){width="724" height="181"}

[It is common to encounter operations that involve two nested loops. For
example, computing a SADF series (Chapter 17), evaluating multiple
barrier touches (Chapter 3), or computing a covariance matrix on
misaligned series. In these situations, a linear partition of the atomic
tasks would be inefficient, because some processors would have to solve
a much larger number of operations than others, and the calculation time
will depend on the heaviest molecule. A partial solution is to partition
the atomic tasks in a number of jobs that is a multiple of the number of
processors, then front-load the jobs queue with the heavy molecules. In
this way, the light molecules will be assigned to processors that have
completed the heavy molecules first, keeping all CPUs busy until the job
queue is depleted. In the next section, we will discuss a more complete
solution.]{bgcolor="inherit"} []{#text00004.html#filepos0001043446}
[`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
20.1`</font>`{=html}](#text00004.html#filepos0001043753) [plots a linear
partition of 20 atomic tasks of equal complexity into 6
molecules.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00085.jpg){width="640" height="480"}

[]{#text00004.html#filepos0001043753}

[**`<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}Figure
20.1`</font>`{=html}**](#text00004.html#filepos0001043446) A linear
partition of 20 atomic tasks into 6 molecules
