`<font size="4">`{=html} **20.5 Multiprocessing Engines**
`</font>`{=html}

[It would be a mistake to write a parallelization wrapper for each
multiprocessed function. Instead, we should develop a library that can
parallelize unknown functions, regardless of their arguments and output
structure. That is the goal of a multiprocessing engine. In this
section, we will study one such engine, and once you understand the
logic, you will be ready to develop your own, including all sorts of
customized properties.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **20.5.1 Preparing the Jobs** `</font>`{=html}

[In previous chapters we have made frequent use of
the]{bgcolor="inherit"} `mpPandasObj` [. That function receives six
arguments, of which four are optional:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

-   `func` : A callback function, which will be executed in parallel
-   `pdObj` : A tuple containing:
    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    -   The name of the argument used to pass molecules to the callback
        function
    -   A list of indivisible tasks (atoms), which will be grouped into
        molecules
-   `numThreads` : The number of threads that will be used in parallel
    (one processor per thread)
-   `mpBatches` : Number of parallel batches (jobs per core)
-   `linMols` : Whether partitions will be linear or double-nested
-   `kargs` : Keyword arguments needed by `func`

[Snippet 20.7 lists how]{bgcolor="inherit"} `mpPandasObj` [works. First,
atoms are grouped into molecules, using]{bgcolor="inherit"} `linParts`
[(equal number of atoms per molecule) or]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`nestedParts` [(atoms distributed in a lower-triangular structure).
When]{bgcolor="inherit"} `mpBatches` [is greater than 1, there will be
more molecules than cores. Suppose that we divide a task into 10
molecules, where molecule 1 takes twice as long as the rest. If we run
this process in 10 cores, 9 of the cores will be idle half of the
runtime, waiting for the first core to process molecule 1.
Alternatively, we could set]{bgcolor="inherit"} `mpBatches =10` [so as
to divide that task in 100 molecules. In doing so, every core will
receive equal workload, even though the first 10 molecules take as much
time as the next 20 molecules. In this example, the run
with]{bgcolor="inherit"} `mpBatches =10` [will take half of the time
consumed by]{bgcolor="inherit"} `mpBatches =1` [.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Second, we form a list of jobs. A job is a dictionary containing all
the information needed to process a molecule, that is, the callback
function, its keyword arguments, and the subset of atoms that form the
molecule. Third, we will process the jobs sequentially
if]{bgcolor="inherit"} `numThreads = =1` [(see Snippet 20.8), and in
parallel otherwise (see Section 20.5.2). The reason that we want the
option to run jobs sequentially is for debugging purposes. It is not
easy to catch a bug when programs are run in multiple
processors.^[]{#text00004.html#filepos0001055345}\ [[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ `<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}1`</font>`{=html}\ `</font>`{=html}](#text00004.html#filepos0001073132)]{.small}^
Once the code is debugged, we will want to use]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`numThreads > 1` [. Fourth, we stitch together the output from every
molecule into a single list, series, or dataframe.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 20.7 THE** ` MPPANDASOBJ` **, USED AT VARIOUS POINTS IN THE
> BOOK**

> ![](Image00639.jpg){width="913" height="649"}

[In Section 20.5.2 we will see the multiprocessing counterpart to
function]{bgcolor="inherit"} `processJobs_` [of Snippet
20.8.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 20.8 SINGLE-THREAD EXECUTION, FOR DEBUGGING**

> ![](Image00712.jpg){width="509" height="178"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **20.5.2 Asynchronous Calls** `</font>`{=html}

[Python has a parallelization library called]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`multiprocessing` [. This library is the basis for multiprocessing
engines such as]{bgcolor="inherit"} `joblib`
[,^[]{#text00004.html#filepos0001056787}\ [[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ `<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}2`</font>`{=html}\ `</font>`{=html}](#text00004.html#filepos0001073443)]{.small}^
which is the engine used by many]{bgcolor="inherit"} `sklearn`
[algorithms.^[]{#text00004.html#filepos0001056984}\ [[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ `<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}3`</font>`{=html}\ `</font>`{=html}](#text00004.html#filepos0001073702)]{.small}^
Snippet 20.9 illustrates how to do an asynchronous call to
Python\'s]{bgcolor="inherit"} `multiprocessing` [library.
The]{bgcolor="inherit"} `reportProgress` [function keeps us informed
about the percentage of jobs completed.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 20.9 EXAMPLE OF ASYNCHRONOUS CALL TO PYTHON\'S
> MULTIPROCESSING LIBRARY**

> ![](Image00805.jpg){width="932" height="628"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **20.5.3 Unwrapping the Callback**
`</font>`{=html}

[In Snippet 20.9, the instruction]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`pool.imap_unordered()` [parallelized]{bgcolor="inherit"} `expandCall`
[, by running each item in]{bgcolor="inherit"} `jobs` [(a molecule) in a
single thread. Snippet 20.10 lists]{bgcolor="inherit"} `expandCall` [,
which unwraps the items (atoms) in the job (molecule), and executes the
callback function. This little function is the trick at the core of the
multiprocessing engine: It transforms a dictionary into a task. Once you
understand the role it plays, you will be able to develop your own
engines.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 20.10 PASSING THE JOB (MOLECULE) TO THE CALLBACK FUNCTION**

> ![](Image00040.jpg){width="771" height="150"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **20.5.4 Pickle/Unpickle Objects**
`</font>`{=html}

[Multiprocessing must pickle methods in order to assign them to
different processors. The problem is, bound methods are not
pickable.^[]{#text00004.html#filepos0001058995}\ [[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ `<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}4`</font>`{=html}\ `</font>`{=html}](#text00004.html#filepos0001074097)]{.small}^
The work around is to add functionality to your engine, that tells the
library how to deal with this kind of objects. Snippet 20.11 contains
the instructions that should be listed at the top of your
multiprocessing engine library. If you are curious about the precise
reason this piece of code is needed, you may want to read Ascher et al.
\[2005\], Section 7.5.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 20.11 PLACE THIS CODE AT THE BEGINNING OF YOUR ENGINE**

> ![](Image00132.jpg){width="781" height="402"}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **20.5.5 Output Reduction** `</font>`{=html}

[Suppose that you divide a task into 24 molecules, with the goal that
the engine assigns each molecule to one available core.
Function]{bgcolor="inherit"} `processJobs` [in Snippet 20.9 will capture
the 24 outputs and store them in a list. This approach is effective in
problems that do not involve large outputs. If the outputs must be
combined into a single output, first we will wait until the last
molecule is completed, and then we will process the items in the list.
The latency added by this post-processing should not be significant, as
long as the outputs are small in size and number.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[However, when the outputs consume a lot of RAM, and they need to be
combined into a single output, storing all those outputs in a list may
cause a memory error. It would be better to perform the output reduction
operation on the fly, as the results are returned asynchronously
by]{bgcolor="inherit"} `func` [, rather than waiting for the last
molecule to be completed. We can address this concern by improving
processJobs. In particular, we are going to pass three additional
arguments that determine how the molecular outputs must
be]{bgcolor="inherit"} *reduced* [into a single output. Snippet 20.12
lists an enhanced version of]{bgcolor="inherit"} `processJobs` [, which
contains three new arguments:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

::: {height="6pt"}
:::

-   `redux` : This is a callback to the function that carries out the
    reduction. For example, `redux = pd.DataFrame.add` , if output
    dataframes ought to be summed up.
-   `reduxArgs` : This is a dictionary that contains the keyword
    arguments that must be passed to `redux` (if any). For example, if
    `redux = pd.DataFrame.join` , then a possibility is
    `reduxArgs = {‘how’:‘outer’}` .
-   `reduxInPlace` : A boolean, indicating whether the `redux` operation
    should happen *in-place* or not. For example, `redux = dict.update`
    and `redux = list.append` require `reduxInPlace = True` , since
    appending a list and updating a dictionary are both in-place
    operations.

> **SNIPPET 20.12 ENHANCING** ` PROCESSJOBS` **TO PERFORM ON-THE-FLY
> OUTPUT REDUCTION**

> ![](Image00225.jpg){width="910" height="592"}

[Now that]{bgcolor="inherit"} `processJobsRedux` [knows what to do with
the outputs, we can also enhance]{bgcolor="inherit"} `mpPandasObj` [from
Snippet 20.7. In Snippet 20.13, the new function]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`mpJobList` [passes the three output reduction arguments
to]{bgcolor="inherit"} `processJobsRedux` [. This eliminates the need to
process an outputed list, as]{bgcolor="inherit"} `mpPandasObj` [did,
hence saving memory and time.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 20.13 ENHANCING** ` MPPANDASOBJ` **TO PERFORM ON-THE-FLY
> OUTPUT REDUCTION**

> ![](Image00316.jpg){width="940" height="314"}

[]{#text00004.html#filepos0001063528}

`<font size="4">`{=html} **20.6 Multiprocessing Example**
`</font>`{=html}

[What we have presented so far in this chapter can be used to speed-up,
by several orders of magnitude, many lengthy and large-scale
mathematical operations. In this section we will illustrate an
additional motivation for multiprocessing: memory
management.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[Suppose that you have conducted a spectral decomposition of a
covariance matrix of the form]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Z*
[\']{bgcolor="inherit"} *Z* [, as we did in Chapter 8, Section 8.4.2,
where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Z* [has size]{bgcolor="inherit"} *TxN* [.
This has resulted in an eigenvectors matrix]{bgcolor="inherit"} *W* [and
an eigenvalues matrix Λ, such that]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Z*
[\']{bgcolor="inherit"} *ZW* [=]{bgcolor="inherit"} *W* [Λ. Now you
would like to derive the orthogonal principal components that explain a
user-defined portion of the total variance, 0 ≤ τ ≤ 1. In order to do
that, we compute]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00407.jpg){width="66"
height="17"} [, where]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00474.jpg){width="17"
height="17"} [contains the first]{bgcolor="inherit"} *M*
[≤]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N* [columns of]{bgcolor="inherit"} *W* [, such
that]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00556.jpg){width="245" height="25"} [.
The computation of]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00658.jpg){width="66"
height="17"} [can be parallelized by noting that]{bgcolor="inherit"}

![](Image00341.jpg){width="160" height="57"}

[where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Z
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *b*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [is a
sparse]{bgcolor="inherit"} *TxN* [matrix with only]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*TxN ~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *b*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [items
(the rest are empty),]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00827.jpg){width="22"
height="21"} [is a]{bgcolor="inherit"} *NxM* [matrix with
only]{bgcolor="inherit"} *N
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *b*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~ xM* [items
(the rest are empty), and]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00064.jpg){width="105" height="26"} [. This sparsity is created
by dividing the set of columns into a partition of]{bgcolor="inherit"}
*B* [subsets of columns, and loading into]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Z
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *b*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [only
the]{bgcolor="inherit"} *b* [th subset of the columns. This notion of
sparsity may sound a bit complicated at first, however Snippet 20.14
demonstrates how pandas allows us to implement it in a seamless way.
Function]{bgcolor="inherit"} `getPCs` [receives]{bgcolor="inherit"}
![](Image00155.jpg){width="17" height="17"} [through the
argument]{bgcolor="inherit"} `eVec` [. The argument]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`molecules` [contains a subset of the file names in]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`fileNames` [, where each file represents]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Z
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *b*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [. The key
concept to grasp is that we compute the dot product of
a]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Z
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *b*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [with the
slice of the rows of]{bgcolor="inherit"} ![](Image00248.jpg){width="22"
height="21"} [defined by the columns in]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Z
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *b*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~* [, and that
molecular results are aggregated on the fly (]{bgcolor="inherit"}
`redux = pd.DataFrame.add` [).]{bgcolor="inherit"}

> **SNIPPET 20.14 PRINCIPAL COMPONENTS FOR A SUBSET OF THE COLUMNS**

> ![](Image00197.jpg){width="893" height="319"}

[This approach presents two advantages: First,
because]{bgcolor="inherit"} `getPCs` [loads
dataframes]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Z
~[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ *b*\ `</font>`{=html}]{.small}~*
[sequentially, for a sufficiently large]{bgcolor="inherit"} *B* [, the
RAM is not exhausted. Second,]{bgcolor="inherit"} `mpJobList` [executes
the molecules in parallel, hence speeding up the
calculations.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[In real life ML applications, we often encounter datasets
where]{bgcolor="inherit"} *Z* [contains billions of datapoints. As this
example demonstrates, parallelization is not only beneficial in terms of
reducing run time. Many problems could not be solved without
parallelization, as a matter of memory limitations, even if we were
willing to wait longer.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

[]{#text00004.html#filepos0001069003}

2.  [The instructions in Snippet 20.2 are very useful for unit testing,
    > > brute force searches, and scenario analysis. Can you remember
    > > where else in the book have you seen them? Where else could they
    > > have been used?]{bgcolor="inherit"}

3.  [Adjust Snippet 20.4 to form molecules using a two-nested loops
    > > scheme, rather than a linear scheme.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

4.  [Compare with]{bgcolor="inherit"} `timeit` [:]{bgcolor="inherit"}

    ::: {height="6pt"}
    :::

    1.  Snippet 20.4, by repeating 10 batches of 100 executions. What is
        the minimum elapsed time for each snippet?
    2.  Modify Snippet 20.4 (from exercise 3), by repeating 10 batches
        of 100 executions. What is the minimum elapsed time for each
        snippet?

5.  [Simplify Snippet 20.4 by using]{bgcolor="inherit"} `mpPandasObj`
    > > [.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

6.  [Modify]{bgcolor="inherit"} `mpPandasObj` [to handle the possibility
    > > of forming molecules using a two-nested loops scheme with an
    > > upper triangular structure.]{bgcolor="inherit"}

^[]{#text00004.html#filepos0001073132}\ [[`<font size="2">`{=html}\ `<font color="#0000FF">`{=html}1`</font>`{=html}\ `</font>`{=html}](#text00004.html#filepos0001055345)]{.small}^
   *Heisenbugs* , named after Heisenberg\'s uncertainty principle,
describe bugs that change their behavior when scrutinized.
Multiprocessing bugs are a prime example.

.

.
